topics.title: Introduction: History of Bakery and Confectionery - Trends, Bakery Products - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT412 - BAKERY AND CONFECTIONERY PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction: History of Bakery and Confectionery - Trends, Bakery Products - Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Bakery and Confectionery

**Bakery** refers to the art, science, and commercial production of flour-based baked goods such as bread, biscuits, cakes, and pastries. It is a sub-discipline of **Cereal Technology** that relies on the interaction of **gluten-forming proteins**, **starch gelatinization**, and **leavening agents** to produce an aerated, palatable crumb structure.

**Confectionery** refers to the preparation of sugar-based food products, including candies, chocolates, toffees, and sweets. It is broadly classified into two streams:
- **Sugar confectionery** (candies, toffees, jellies, gums)
- **Chocolate confectionery** (chocolate bars, coatings, truffles)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> "Bakery and Confectionery Products is the branch of Food Technology that deals with the formulation, processing, packaging, and quality evaluation of cereal-based (bakery) and sugar-based (confectionery) convenience foods, encompassing raw material characterization, dough/rheology science, thermal processing, and shelf-life engineering."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **bakery as a controlled chemistry lab disguised as a kitchen**:
- The **flour** is the skeleton (like steel in a building).
- The **water** is the activator that "wakes up" the gluten proteins.
- The **yeast** (or baking powder) is the tiny balloon-blower that fills the structure with gas.
- The **oven heat** is the setting agent that hardens the structure into a permanent form.

Without any one of these four, you don't get bread — you get a flat, gummy, or collapsed mess. The same is true in **confectionery**: sugar + water + heat + agitation = candy; without the right ratio of these, you get either a burnt syrup or a runny liquid.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Bakery & Confectionery

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Bread oven temperature | **200 °C – 230 °C** | Crust formation & crumb setting |
| Cake baking temperature | **160 °C – 180 °C** | Gentle setting, prevents dome collapse |
| Sugar syrup for candy (hard crack) | **150 °C – 154 °C** | Final moisture < 2 % |
| Baker's Percentage (flour basis) | **100 %** | Universal formulation language |
| Yeast activity range (optimum) | **30 °C – 38 °C** | Max CO₂ generation |
| pH of fermented dough | **4.5 – 5.5** | Sourness indicator |
| Moisture in bread (sliced) | **35 % – 42 %** | Shelf-life determinant |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Baker's Percentage** system is the **universal language** of the baking industry. Every other ingredient is expressed as a percentage relative to flour weight (which is always fixed at 100 %). This is analogous to the **stoichiometric coefficient** in chemistry — it allows reproducible scaling of any recipe.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Historical Evolution — Structured Logic Steps

### A. The Stone Age → Egyptian Era (Pre-3000 BC)
1. Humans discovered wild grains accidentally dropped near fire pits.
2. The **Egyptians** (~3000 BC) are credited with inventing **leavened bread** by leaving dough out — wild yeasts from the air fermented it.
3. They built the **first purpose-built ovens** (beehive-shaped clay ovens).

### B. Greek & Roman Era (500 BC – 500 AD)
1. Greeks advanced bread-making with **closed ovens** and recognized different bread types.
2. Romans established the first **professional bakeries (pistrinae)** — bakeries became a public profession.
3. Honey-based **fruit confections** emerged in Roman banquets.

### C. Middle Ages (500 – 1500 AD)
1. Monastic bakeries in Europe refined bread and pastry techniques.
2. **Spice trade** introduced cardamom, cinnamon, nutmeg into confections.
3. **Marchpane (marzipan)** — ancestor of modern confectionery — emerged in the Middle East and Mediterranean.

### D. Industrial Revolution (1700s – 1800s)
1. **1886** — American engineer **August Oetiker** patented the first biscuit-cutting machine.
2. **Read & Barrett (Bristol, UK, 1849)** introduced the first factory-produced biscuits using cylinder rolling.
3. **1860s** — Louis Pasteur explained yeast fermentation scientifically.

### E. Modern Era (1900 – Present)
1. **1906** — Otto Frederick Rohwedder invented the **sliced bread machine**.
2. **1928** — Isaac Carasso founded **Danone** (yogurt + pastries chain).
3. **1950s** — Wonder Bread popularized soft, pre-sliced, factory-produced bread.
4. **1970s** — Sliced, wrapped, and gas-flushed **artisan bread** disrupted mass market.
5. **2000s – Today** — Gluten-free, organic, clean-label, plant-based, and functional bakery products dominate.

## 2.2 Modern Trends in Bakery and Confectionery

| Trend | Description | Market Driver |
|---|---|---|
| **Clean Label** | No artificial additives; recognisable ingredients | Consumer transparency demand |
| **Gluten-Free** | Rice, maize, sorghum, or amaranth-based flours | Celiac disease, lifestyle diets |
| **Functional Bakery** | Added probiotics, prebiotics, omega-3, vitamins | Health & wellness movement |
| **Fortified Products** | Iron, folic acid, B-vitamins in bread | Government nutrition programs |
| **Plant-Based / Vegan** | Egg-free, dairy-free, honey-free | Environmental & ethical concerns |
| **Artisan / Premium** | Sourdough, long fermentation, stone-baked | Gourmet retail growth |
| **Low-Sugar / Sugar-Free** | Stevia, erythritol, monk fruit in confectionery | Diabetic & weight-conscious consumers |
| **Sustainable Packaging** | Compostable, recyclable wrappers | ESG mandates |
| **Frozen Bakery** | Par-baked and frozen dough/bread | Convenience retail (Horeca sector) |
| **3D-Printed Confectionery** | Custom shapes, sugar-based printing | Novelty and customization |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Baker's Percentage** | $\% X = \frac{W_X}{W_{flour}} \times 100$ | $W_X$ = weight of ingredient X; $W_{flour}$ = weight of flour (kg) | Universal recipe scaling in industry |
| **Total Dough Yield** | $Y = \frac{W_{flour} + W_{water} + W_{others}}{W_{flour}} \times 100$ | All in kg; $Y$ in % | Predicts finished bread weight |
| **Hydration Ratio** | $H = \frac{W_{water}}{W_{flour}} \times 100$ | % | Determines crumb openness (e.g., baguette ≈ 65 %, brioche ≈ 75 %) |
| **Loss on Baking** | $LoB = \frac{W_{dough} - W_{bread}}{W_{dough}} \times 100$ | Typically **10 % – 15 %** | Quality control benchmark |
| **Sugar Syrup Concentration (Brix)** | $^\circ Brix = \frac{W_{sugar}}{W_{solution}} \times 100$ | W in kg | Confectionery endpoint test |
| **Candy Stage Temperature (Seeding)** | $T_c = T_b - 12\,°C$ | $T_b$ = boiling point of syrup | Boil-back method to prevent crystallization |
| **Specific Volume of Bread** | $SV = \frac{V_{loaf}}{W_{loaf}}$ | cm³/g; target = **3.5 – 4.5 cm³/g** | Crumb texture indicator |
| **Maillard Reaction (approx.)** | Rate $\approx 2 \times$ per **+10 °C** above 140 °C | Temperature | Crust browning kinetics |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, **Baker's Percentage** questions are **frequently asked** (almost every semester). Memorize the formula, the concept that flour = 100 %, and the typical ranges for water (50–75 %), salt (1.8–2.2 %), yeast (1–3 %), and sugar (5–30 % depending on product type).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Engineering**: Baker's percentage allows engineers to scale recipes for **continuous mixing systems** producing 10,000 loaves/hour.
- **Quality Control Labs**: Specific volume (SV) and moisture are **kinematic indicators** in HACCP-based quality audits.
- **Confectionery Industry**: Brix and candy-stage temperatures are critical for **sugar-cooking evaporators** and **vacuum batch cookers**.
- **R&D**: Fortification percentages (e.g., iron at **15 mg/kg** as per FSSAI) are calculated using baker's percentage logic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Baker's Percentage Calculation

**Problem:** A bakery uses the following formula for white bread:
- Flour = 10 kg
- Water = 6.2 kg
- Salt = 0.2 kg
- Sugar = 0.8 kg
- Yeast = 0.3 kg
- Shortening = 0.4 kg
- **Find the baker's percentage of each ingredient and total dough yield.**

### Step 1: Anchor Flour at 100 %

$$ \%Flour = \frac{W_{flour}}{W_{flour}} \times 100 = \frac{10}{10} \times 100 = 100\,\% $$

### Step 2: Calculate Hydration (Water %)

$$ \%Water = \frac{W_{water}}{W_{flour}} \times 100 = \frac{6.2}{10} \times 100 = 62\,\% $$

> This is a **typical hydration** for lean white bread (range 60 – 65 %).

### Step 3: Calculate Salt %

$$ \%Salt = \frac{W_{salt}}{W_{flour}} \times 100 = \frac{0.2}{10} \times 100 = 2.0\,\% $$

### Step 4: Calculate Sugar %

$$ \%Sugar = \frac{W_{sugar}}{W_{flour}} \times 100 = \frac{0.8}{10} \times 100 = 8.0\,\% $$

### Step 5: Calculate Yeast %

$$ \%Yeast = \frac{W_{yeast}}{W_{flour}} \times 100 = \frac{0.3}{10} \times 100 = 3.0\,\% $$

### Step 6: Calculate Shortening %

$$ \%Shortening = \frac{W_{shortening}}{W_{flour}} \times 100 = \frac{0.4}{10} \times 100 = 4.0\,\% $$

### Step 7: Total Dough Yield

$$ Y = \frac{W_{flour} + W_{water} + W_{salt} + W_{sugar} + W_{yeast} + W_{shortening}}{W_{flour}} \times 100 $$

$$ Y = \frac{10 + 6.2 + 0.2 + 0.8 + 0.3 + 0.4}{10} \times 100 $$

$$ Y = \frac{17.9}{10} \times 100 = 179\,\% $$

### Step 8: Interpret

- The dough weight is **1.79 times** the flour weight.
- Expected bread yield after baking (Loss on Baking ≈ 12 %):
  $$ W_{bread} = 17.9 \times (1 - 0.12) = 17.9 \times 0.88 = 15.75\,kg $$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Sugar Syrup Brix Calculation (Confectionery)

**Problem:** A confectioner dissolves 65 kg of sucrose in 35 kg of water and boils until 5 kg of water evaporates. Find the final Brix.

### Step 1: Initial Setup

Initial sugar = 65 kg
Initial water = 35 kg
Evaporated water = 5 kg
Final water = 35 − 5 = 30 kg
Final total solution = 65 + 30 = 95 kg

### Step 2: Apply Brix Formula

$$ ^\circ Brix = \frac{W_{sugar}}{W_{solution}} \times 100 = \frac{65}{95} \times 100 $$

$$ ^\circ Brix = 68.42\,^\circ Brix $$

> This corresponds to a **medium-soft ball stage** (~ 68 – 70 % solids), used for **fudge and fondant**.

## 3.3 Comprehensive Classification Matrix of Bakery Products

| Category | Sub-Types | Key Characteristics | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bread** | Lean bread | No sugar/fat; crusty | French baguette, ciabatta |
|  | Enriched bread | Sugar, fat, milk added | Sandwich bread, milk bread |
|  | Whole-wheat bread | ≥ 80 % whole wheat | Atta bread, brown bread |
|  | Sourdough | Wild yeast + lactobacilli | San Francisco sourdough |
|  | Flatbread | No oven, pan-cooked | Naan, pita, chapati |
|  | Sweet bread | Sugar, eggs, fat | Brioche, panettone |
| **Cakes** | Sponge cake | Whipped eggs, no chemical leavener | Genoise |
|  | Butter cake | Chemical leavener, high fat | Pound cake, Madeira |
|  | Foam-type | Egg-white foam | Angel food cake |
|  | Chiffon | Oil + egg foam | Chiffon cake |
| **Biscuits & Cookies** | Hard biscuits | Low sugar, low fat | Marie, cream cracker |
|  | Short dough | High fat, tender | Shortbread, butter cookies |
|  | Wire-cut | Soft dough extruded | Chocolate chip cookies |
|  | Sandwich biscuits | Cream-filled | Oreo, Bourbon |
| **Pastries** | Shortcrust | 1:2:3 ratio (fat:flour:water) | Tart, quiche shell |
|  | Puff pastry | 27+ folds, 729 layers | Croissant, mille-feuille |
|  | Choux | Twice-cooked | Éclairs, profiteroles |
|  | Filo | Paper-thin sheets | Baklava, strudel |
| **Confectionery** | Hard candy | High sugar, low moisture | Lollipops, boiled sweets |
|  | Soft candy | Intermediate moisture | Toffees, caramels |
|  | Gums & jellies | Gelling agents | Gummy bears, jelly beans |
|  | Chocolate | Cocoa solids + cocoa butter | Dark, milk, white chocolate |
|  | Marshmallow | Whipped sugar-gelatin | Mallows |
|  | Lozenges | Compressed sugar | Throat lozenges |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Leavening Agents Comparison

| Leavening Agent | Mechanism | Speed | Residual Flavor | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Baker's Yeast** (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) | Biological CO₂ from sugar fermentation | Slow (1–4 h) | Mild | Bread, pizza |
| **Baking Powder** | Chemical (acid + carbonate) | Fast (instant) | None | Cakes, cookies |
| **Baking Soda** | Acid neutralization | Very fast | Slight soapy | Gingerbread |
| **Sourdough Starter** | Wild fermentation | Very slow (8–24 h) | Tangy | Sourdough bread |
| **Steam** | Water vapor expansion | Very fast | None | Puff pastry, éclairs |
| **Air (Whipping)** | Mechanical incorporation | Instant | None | Sponge cake, meringue |


## 4.2 Block Diagram — Bakery Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Reception] --> B[Weighing and Sifting of Dry Ingredients]
    B --> C[Blending Stage 1: Flour + Salt + Sugar]
    C --> D[Dissolution of Yeast in Warm Water]
    D --> E[Mixing / Kneading 15 to 20 min]
    E --> F[Bulk Fermentation 1 to 2 h at 30 to 35 C]
    F --> G[Punching and Folding]
    G --> H[Dividing and Rounding]
    H --> I[Intermediate Proofing 10 to 20 min]
    I --> J[Sheeting and Moulding]
    J --> K[Final Proofing 45 to 75 min at 35 C, 80 percent RH]
    K --> L[Baking 25 to 35 min at 200 to 230 C]
    L --> M[Cooling on Spiral Cooler 30 to 60 min]
    M --> N[Slicing Optional]
    N --> O[Packaging Modified Atmosphere]
    O --> P[Storage and Dispatch]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define "Baker's Percentage" and state the role of flour in this system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Baker's Percentage is a formulation system in which flour weight is taken as 100 % (the reference ingredient) and all other ingredients are expressed as a percentage relative to flour weight.

**Formula:**
$$ \%Ingredient = \frac{W_{ingredient}}{W_{flour}} \times 100 $$

**Role of Flour:** Flour is the **structural backbone** of all bakery products. It provides:
1. **Gluten proteins** (glutenin and gliadin) that form an elastic network,
2. **Starch granules** that gelatinize and set the crumb structure,
3. Acts as the **carrier of water** in the dough matrix.

(3 marks distributed: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark roles.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**List any six current trends in the bakery and confectionery industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — list any 6, ½ mark each):**

1. Clean-label products (no artificial additives).
2. Gluten-free bakery items.
3. Functional and fortified products (with added vitamins, minerals, probiotics).
4. Plant-based / vegan bakery.
5. Frozen par-baked products for retail.
6. Low-sugar and sugar-free confectionery.
7. Sustainable and compostable packaging.
8. Premium artisan / sourdough products.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 MARKS

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

> (a) Discuss the **historical evolution of bakery** from ancient civilizations to the modern industrial era, highlighting at least **five key milestones**. (7 marks)
> (b) Explain in detail the **classification of bakery products** with suitable examples and the role of **leavening agents** in each category. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Egyptian Era (~3000 BC) — 1.5 marks**
- Egyptians are credited with the discovery of **leavened bread** when dough left in warm air fermented via wild yeasts.
- They built the first **purpose-built beehive clay ovens**.
- They pioneered the use of **sourdough-like fermentation** for bread.

**2. Roman Era (500 BC – 500 AD) — 1.5 marks**
- Establishment of **pistrinae** (public bakeries) — first professional baking industry.
- Romans developed **water-mills** for large-scale flour production.
- Introduction of **pan loaf** (baking in moulds).

**3. Middle Ages (500 – 1500 AD) — 1 mark**
- Monastic bakeries refined bread and pastry techniques.
- **Marchpane (marzipan)** emerges in the Mediterranean.
- Spice-trade introduces **cinnamon, nutmeg, cardamom** into confections.

**4. Industrial Revolution (18th – 19th century) — 1.5 marks**
- **1689** — French royal kitchens formalize patisserie.
- **1849** — Read & Barrett open the first **commercial biscuit factory** in Bristol, UK.
- **1886** — August Oetiker patents the **biscuit-cutting machine**.
- **1860** — Louis Pasteur explains yeast fermentation.

**5. Modern Era (20th – 21st century) — 1.5 marks**
- **1906** — Rohwedder's **sliced bread machine**.
- **1950s** — Mass-produced sandwich bread (Wonder Bread).
- **1980s** — Frozen dough technology.
- **2000s onwards** — Gluten-free, clean-label, functional, plant-based, and 3D-printed products dominate.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Classification of Bakery Products:**

| Category | Examples | Leavening Agent |
|---|---|---|
| **Bread (Lean)** | French baguette, ciabatta | Baker's yeast (slow fermentation) |
| **Bread (Enriched)** | Milk bread, brioche | Yeast + sugar boost |
| **Cakes (Butter)** | Pound cake | Baking powder / soda |
| **Cakes (Sponge)** | Genoise | Whipped air + steam |
| **Cakes (Foam)** | Angel food | Egg-white foam (air) |
| **Biscuits (Hard)** | Marie, cream cracker | None (dense) |
| **Cookies (Soft)** | Chocolate chip | Baking soda / powder |
| **Pastries (Puff)** | Croissant | Laminated fat + steam |
| **Pastries (Choux)** | Éclairs, profiteroles | Steam (high moisture dough) |
| **Flatbreads** | Naan, pita | None (often) |

**Role of Leavening Agents — 2 marks:**
- Generate gas (CO₂ or water vapour) within the dough/batter.
- Create **porous, aerated texture** and increase volume.
- Improve **mouthfeel, lightness, and digestibility**.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (ALTERNATIVE)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> (a) Explain the **concept of Baker's Percentage**. A bread formula contains 50 kg flour, 30 kg water, 1 kg salt, 2 kg sugar, 1.5 kg yeast, and 1 kg shortening. **Calculate the baker's percentage of each ingredient and total dough yield.** (7 marks)
> (b) Discuss in detail the **current trends in bakery and confectionery industry** and how they influence **product development and consumer choice**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Concept (2 marks):**
Baker's Percentage is a formulation system where flour = 100 %; all other ingredients are expressed relative to flour.

$$ \%Ingredient = \frac{W_{ingredient}}{W_{flour}} \times 100 $$

**Calculations (4 marks):**

| Ingredient | Weight (kg) | Baker's % |
|---|---|---|
| Flour | 50 | 100.0 % |
| Water | 30 | 60.0 % |
| Salt | 1 | 2.0 % |
| Sugar | 2 | 4.0 % |
| Yeast | 1.5 | 3.0 % |
| Shortening | 1 | 2.0 % |

**Total Dough Yield (1 mark):**

$$ Y = \frac{50 + 30 + 1 + 2 + 1.5 + 1}{50} \times 100 = \frac{85.5}{50} \times 100 = 171\,\% $$

This is a typical lean bread formula (low sugar, low fat, moderate hydration = 60 %).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. Clean Label Movement (1.5 marks)**
- Consumers demand recognizable, natural ingredients.
- Removal of artificial colours, flavours, and preservatives.
- Industry response: use of natural emulsifiers (lecithin from sunflower), natural flavours (vanilla extract), and simpler ingredient declarations.

**2. Gluten-Free Products (1.5 marks)**
- Driven by celiac disease (~1 % global prevalence) and lifestyle choices.
- Alternative flours: rice, maize, sorghum, amaranth, buckwheat, chickpea.
- Challenge: replicating gluten's viscoelastic functionality using hydrocolloids (xanthan gum, HPMC).

**3. Functional & Fortified Bakery (1 mark)**
- Fortification with iron, folic acid, B-vitamins (mandated in many countries).
- Functional ingredients: probiotics, prebiotics, omega-3, plant sterols.

**4. Plant-Based / Vegan (1 mark)**
- Replacement of eggs, dairy, honey with plant proteins (pea, soy), aquafaba, coconut milk.

**5. Frozen & Convenience Bakery (1 mark)**
- Par-baked frozen dough for retail bakeries (Horeca sector growth).
- Extended shelf life through MAP and cryoprotectants.

**6. Premium & Artisan (1 mark)**
- Long-fermentation sourdough, single-origin chocolate, handcrafted confections.
- Premium pricing in metro urban markets.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **In Baker's Percentage questions**, students often forget to **anchor flour = 100 %** and divide by 100 directly. Always show the formula first.
> 2. **Confusing "Total Dough Yield" with "Total %"**: Total dough yield > 100 % (because flour itself is 100 %, plus other ingredients). Students write "Y = 100 %" — this is wrong.
> 3. **In historical questions**, students list dates without explaining the **significance** of each milestone. Marks are awarded for *why* the event mattered, not just *when*.
> 4. **In classification questions**, students often give only 3–4 categories. The question expects **at least 5–6 categories** for full marks.
> 5. **Spelling errors** in technical terms (e.g., "Chifon" instead of "Chiffon", "marziphan" instead of "marzipan") may cost half marks in strict valuation.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction**

- ✅ **Bakery** = flour-based; **Confectionery** = sugar-based.
- ✅ Ancient Egypt (3000 BC) = first leavened bread; Rome = first professional bakeries.
- ✅ **Baker's Percentage**: Flour = 100 %; all other ingredients expressed relative to flour.
- ✅ Standard bread hydration: **60 – 65 %**; enriched bread: **65 – 75 %**.
- ✅ Total dough yield of a typical white bread ≈ **170 – 180 %**.
- ✅ **Loss on baking** (LoB) ≈ **10 – 15 %** of dough weight.
- ✅ Bread oven temp: **200 – 230 °C**; Cake baking temp: **160 – 180 °C**.
- ✅ Six major bakery categories: **Bread, Cakes, Biscuits/Cookies, Pastries, Confectionery, Flatbreads**.
- ✅ Major leavening agents: **Yeast, Baking Powder, Baking Soda, Steam, Air (whipping)**.
- ✅ Top 6 current trends: **Clean Label, Gluten-Free, Functional, Plant-Based, Frozen/Par-Baked, Premium/Artisan**.
- ✅ Sugar syrup candy stages: **Thread (110 °C), Soft Ball (115 °C), Hard Ball (121 °C), Soft Crack (132 °C), Hard Crack (150 °C)**.
- ✅ Specific volume of good bread: **3.5 – 4.5 cm³/g**.
- ✅ FSSAI standards, food safety, and HACCP are now integral to bakery/confectionery quality.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specifications, Composition, and Raw Materials; Flour Types - Characteristics, Water, Salt - Usage and Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT412 - BAKERY AND CONFECTIONERY PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specifications, Composition, and Raw Materials; Flour Types - Characteristics, Water, Salt - Usage and Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bakery Products — The Engineering of Wheat-Based Foods

**Bakery and Confectionery Technology** is the applied science and engineering discipline concerned with the controlled transformation of cereal-derived flours, water, salt, sugar, fat, leavening agents, and other functional ingredients into aerated, structured, palatable, and shelf-stable finished products through the application of mechanical, thermal, and biochemical processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 1 Anchor Concept):**
> Bakery products are defined as **ready-to-eat (RTE) or ready-to-bake (RTB) food items** produced primarily from **cereal flours** (wheat, rye, oats, maize, rice), using **water, salt, leavening agents, sugar, fat, and miscellaneous additives**, processed by **mechanical mixing, fermentation/chemical leavening, and thermal baking** in ovens operating typically in the range of **180 °C – 250 °C**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a bakery as a **chemical reactor disguised as a kitchen**. Flour is the structural polymer (the *steel* of the building), water is the *welder* that activates gluten, salt is the *quality-control supervisor* that tightens the gluten mesh and regulates fermentation, and the oven is the *construction site* where everything is set permanently. Without any one of these four pillars, the building (bread) collapses or never rises.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Raw Material Hierarchy (KTU 2024 Module 1)**
> 1. **Flour** — Primary structural and nutritional substrate
> 2. **Water** — Hydration medium, gluten developer, reaction solvent
> 3. **Salt** — Flavor potentiator, fermentation regulator, gluten modifier
> 4. *Supplementary:* Sugar, fat, yeast, milk powder, eggs, additives (covered in Module 2)

## 1.2 Raw Material Specifications — Why They Matter

Every ingredient entering a bakery is governed by **technical specifications** that define:
- **Chemical composition** (moisture, protein, ash, gluten content)
- **Physical characteristics** (particle size, color, granulation)
- **Microbiological standards** (TPC < 10⁴ CFU/g for flour; **coliforms absent in 0.1 g**)
- **Functional performance** (water absorption, dough development time, stability)
- **Sensory attributes** (taste, odor, appearance)
- **Regulatory compliance** (FSSAI 2011 / Codex Alimentarius limits)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Specification Benchmarks for Wheat Flour (BIS / FSSAI):**
> - **Moisture:** ≤ **14.0 %** (max)
> - **Total ash (on dry basis):** ≤ **1.0 %** (for refined atta) to **2.0 %** (for whole wheat)
> - **Protein (Nx5.7):** ≥ **9.0 %** (cake) to **14.0 %** (bread)
> - **Wet gluten:** ≥ **28 %** (strong bread flour)
> - **Falling Number (Hagberg):** ≥ **250 seconds** (sound, non-sprouted wheat)
> - **Granularity:** passes through **212 µm (70 mesh)** sieve

## 1.3 Composition of Bakery End-Products (Approximate Proximate)

| Component | Bread (%) | Cake (%) | Biscuit (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture | 35 – 40 | 18 – 25 | 2 – 6 |
| Protein | 7 – 10 | 5 – 7 | 6 – 8 |
| Carbohydrate | 45 – 55 | 55 – 65 | 65 – 75 |
| Fat | 1 – 3 | 10 – 20 | 15 – 25 |
| Ash (minerals) | 1.5 – 2.5 | 0.8 – 1.5 | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| Crude fiber | 0.5 – 1.5 | 0.1 – 0.5 | 0.5 – 1.0 |
| **Energy (kcal/100 g)** | **~ 250** | **~ 350** | **~ 450** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 FLOUR — The Structural Backbone

### 2.1.1 Definition and Botanical Origin

**Flour** is the fine powder obtained by **milling cleaned cereal grains**, primarily **wheat (*Triticum aestivum*)**, after removal of bran and germ to varying degrees. Wheat is uniquely suited to bakery because it contains two viscoelastic proteins — **glutenin (elastic)** and **gliadin (plastic/viscous)** — which, upon hydration and mechanical work, form a **gluten complex**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Proximate Composition of Wheat Flour (per 100 g, dry basis):**
> - Starch: **65 – 75 %**
> - Protein: **8 – 15 %**
> - Pentosans: **1.5 – 2.5 %**
> - Lipids: **1 – 2 %**
> - Minerals (ash): **0.4 – 2.0 %**
> - Moisture: **12 – 14 %**
> - Enzymes (amylases, proteases, lipases): trace

### 2.1.2 Wheat Kernel Anatomy & Milling Fractions

The wheat kernel has three anatomical regions that yield different flour streams:

| Kernel Part | Botanical Identity | % of Kernel | Flour Yielded |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Endosperm** | Starchy storage tissue | 82 – 84 % | Straight flour / refined flour |
| **Bran** | Outer protective layers (pericarp, testa, aleurone) | 13 – 17 % | Whole wheat / atta |
| **Germ** | Embryo | 2 – 3 % | Whole wheat only (removed in white flour to limit rancidity) |

**Milling extraction rate (%) = (Mass of flour obtained / Mass of clean wheat) × 100**

- **Straight-run flour:** ~ **70 – 75 %** extraction (pure endosperm)
- **Standard bread flour:** ~ **72 – 78 %**
- **Whole wheat flour:** ~ **95 – 100 %** (retains bran + germ)
- **Atta (Indian chapatti flour):** ~ **90 – 95 %** (high bran retention)

## 2.2 TYPES OF FLOUR — Characteristics & Functional Classification

### 2.2.1 Classification by Wheat Hardness (Texture)

| Flour Type | Wheat Class | Protein % | Granulation | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hard / Strong flour (bread flour)** | Hard red winter / spring wheat | 11.5 – 14.0 | Coarse | Bread, buns, pizza, hard rolls |
| **Medium flour (all-purpose / maida)** | Mixture of hard + soft | 9.0 – 11.5 | Medium | Cakes, biscuits, general use |
| **Soft / Weak flour (cake / pastry flour)** | Soft red / soft white wheat | 6.5 – 9.0 | Fine | Cakes, pastries, cookies |
| **Durum flour (semolina)** | *Triticum durum* | 12.0 – 15.0 | Coarse, granular | Pasta, noodles, some breads |

### 2.2.2 Classification by Ash / Mineral Content

| Grade of Flour | Max Ash (% d.b.) | Color | End Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First clear (patent)** | 0.40 | Very white | Confectionery, cakes |
| **Second clear** | 0.65 | White | General baking |
| **Straight grade** | 0.70 – 0.80 | Cream-white | Bread, biscuit |
| **Whole wheat (atta)** | 1.50 – 2.00 | Light brown | Chapatti, brown bread |
| **Graham flour** | 1.50 – 2.00 | Speckled brown | Health bread |

### 2.2.3 Specialty Flours (KTU 2024 — High Yield)

| Flour | Source / Treatment | Functional Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Self-rising flour** | Flour + salt + baking powder (1.5 %) | Ready leavening for quick breads |
| **Enriched flour** | Refined + thiamine, niacin, iron, folate | Nutritional restoration |
| **Bleached flour** | Treated with Cl₂, benzoyl peroxide, or azodicarbonamide (ADA) | Whitening, gluten strengthening |
| **Pre-gelatinized flour** | Heat-/moisture-treated | Cold-water swelling (instant puddings, batters) |
| **Vital wheat gluten** | Wet-extracted, dried (75 % protein) | Protein fortification for high-fibre/low-gluten flours |
| **Sprouted wheat flour** | Germinated wheat dried & milled | High α-amylase, sweeter, more bioactives |
| **Stone-ground flour** | Ground between millstones (cold process) | Retains germ oil, more flavour |
| **Gluten-free flour** | Rice, maize, sorghum, millet, buckwheat, tapioca | Celiac-safe baking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cereal Grain Gluten Substitutes (KTU 2024 — Common Exam Q):**
> - **Rye:** Contains glutenin + gliadin but forms weaker gluten; needs sour-dough fermentation
> - **Oats:** Contains **avenin** (related but not identical to gliadin); often contaminated with wheat
> - **Rice / Maize / Sorghum:** Contain **no gluten-forming proteins** — require binders (xanthan gum, HPMC, psyllium)

## 2.3 WATER — The Universal Activator

### 2.3.1 Functional Role of Water in Baking

Water is the **second most important ingredient** in baking (after flour). It is the medium in which nearly all physico-chemical and biochemical reactions occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Eight Functions of Water in Baking (KTU 2024 High-Yield):**
> 1. **Hydrates flour proteins** (glutenin + gliadin → gluten network)
> 2. **Hydrates and gelatinises starch** on heating (≥ 60 °C)
> 3. **Dissolves** salt, sugar, yeast, and soluble additives
> 4. **Activates native enzymes** (amylases, proteases, lipases)
> 5. **Controls dough consistency** (water absorption capacity, WAC)
> 6. **Medium for yeast fermentation** (C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂ ↑)
> 7. **Controls oven heat transfer** (thermal conductivity ≈ **0.6 W/m·K**)
> 8. **Determines final product moisture** (shelf-life and softness)

### 2.3.2 Water Specifications for Bakery

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Potable Water Standards (WHO + FSSAI 2011) for Bakery Use:**
> - **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids):** ≤ **500 mg/L** (acceptable) to **1000 mg/L** (max)
> - **pH:** **6.5 – 8.5**
> - **Total hardness (as CaCO₃):** **100 – 300 mg/L** (soft to moderately hard)
> - **Chloride (as Cl⁻):** ≤ **250 mg/L**
> - **Iron (Fe):** ≤ **0.3 mg/L** (excess causes discolouration, rancidity)
> - **Free chlorine residual:** **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L** (sanitisation)
> - **Total coliforms:** absent in **100 mL**
> - **Temperature:** **20 – 30 °C** (dough mixing) — chilled water (≤ 10 °C) used for retardation

### 2.3.3 Water Absorption and Dough Hydration Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Baker's Percentage (Foundation Equation of Bakery Formulation):**
> The baker's percentage expresses every ingredient's weight as a **percentage of flour weight** (which is fixed at 100 %).
>
> $$\text{Ingredient \%} = \frac{\text{Weight of ingredient}}{\text{Weight of flour}} \times 100$$
>
> This makes **flour = 100 %** (base) and **water absorption (WA) = 55 – 75 %** of flour weight.

**Water absorption (WA, %) is influenced by:**
- Flour protein content (↑ protein → ↑ WA) — typically **+0.5 to +1.0 %** WA per **+1 %** protein
- Damaged starch content (↑ damaged starch → ↑ WA) — typical bread flour: **6 – 10 %** damaged starch
- Pentosan content (↑ pentosans → ↑ WA, ~ **15 – 20×** water of its own weight)
- Particle fineness (finer → faster hydration)

**Farinograph Water Absorption (WA, %)** is the standard QC parameter: the amount of water required to bring **500 g of flour** to a dough consistency of **500 Brabender Units (BU)** at the centre of the curve.

## 2.4 SALT (NaCl) — The Multifunctional Regulator

### 2.4.1 Functional Roles of Salt

> [!NOTE]
> **The Seven Functions of Salt in Baking (KTU 2024 High-Yield):**
> 1. **Flavour enhancer** — raises perceived intensity of wheat and yeast notes
> 2. **Gluten tightening** — strengthens the gluten mesh by ionic charge-shielding of proteins
> 3. **Fermentation retarder** — slows CO₂ evolution by **~ 20 – 30 %**, allowing controlled proofing
> 4. **Osmotic regulation** of yeast cells
> 5. **Crust colour** — moderates Maillard browning (excess salt → pale crust)
> 6. **Shelf-life extension** — water activity (a_w) reduction at surface
> 7. **Dough handling** — reduces stickiness on equipment

### 2.4.2 Salt Specifications and Usage Levels

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Purity (NaCl)** | ≥ **99.5 %** (food grade) |
| **Iodine content** | ≥ **15 ppm** (iodised salt, FSSAI) |
| **Insoluble matter** | ≤ **1.0 %** |
| **Moisture** | ≤ **6.0 %** |
| **Granulation** | Fine (≤ 200 µm) for dough; coarse for topping |
| **Anti-caking agents** | ≤ **2 %** (Ca₃(PO₄)₂ or MgCO₃ permitted) |
| **Heavy metals (Pb)** | ≤ **2.0 mg/kg** |

**Usage levels in baker's percentage:**
- **Bread:** **1.8 – 2.2 %** of flour weight
- **Buns / rolls:** **1.5 – 2.0 %**
- **Biscuits (savoury):** **1.0 – 1.5 %**
- **Sweet biscuits / cakes:** **0.5 – 1.0 %** (compensated by sugar)
- **No-salt bread:** **0 %** (clinical / renal diet)

> [!WARNING]
> **Salt omission consequences:** Dough becomes slack, sticky, runny; yeast over-ferments producing acidic, coarse-textured crumb; crust becomes overly dark due to uncontrolled Maillard browning; flavour is flat ("cardboard-like").

## 2.5 KTU 2024 High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Parameter | Hard Bread Flour | Soft Cake Flour | Whole Wheat Atta |
|---|---|---|---|
| Protein (Nx5.7) % | 11.5 – 14.0 | 6.5 – 9.0 | 10.0 – 12.5 |
| Wet gluten % | 28 – 36 | 18 – 24 | 26 – 32 |
| Damaged starch % | 6 – 10 | 3 – 5 | 5 – 8 |
| Ash (d.b.) % | 0.50 – 0.65 | 0.36 – 0.45 | 1.40 – 1.90 |
| Water absorption % | 60 – 70 | 48 – 55 | 65 – 78 |
| pH (10 % slurry) | 6.0 – 6.8 | 6.0 – 6.6 | 6.2 – 6.9 |
| Falling Number (s) | 250 – 350 | 280 – 350 | 250 – 320 |
| Particle size (µm) | 100 – 200 | 60 – 100 | 150 – 300 |
| Colour (K/J units) | 2.0 – 4.0 (K) | 1.0 – 2.5 (K) | 14 – 20 (J) |

| Raw Material | Baker's % (Bread) | Baker's % (Cake) |
|---|---|---|
| Flour | 100.0 | 100.0 |
| Water | 58 – 65 | 50 – 60 |
| Salt | 1.8 – 2.2 | 0.5 – 1.0 |
| Yeast (compressed) | 2.0 – 3.0 | — |
| Baking powder | — | 3.0 – 5.0 |
| Sugar | 2 – 5 | 80 – 120 |
| Fat | 2 – 4 | 30 – 50 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Functional Mechanisms & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Wheat Flour Extraction Rate

Let us define:
- $M_w$ = mass of clean wheat grain entering the first break roll (kg)
- $M_f$ = total mass of all flour streams collected across all milling stages (kg)
- $M_b$ = mass of bran separated as a by-product (kg)
- $M_g$ = mass of germ separated (kg)

By conservation of mass in the milling system:

$$M_w = M_f + M_b + M_g + M_{loss}$$

where $M_{loss}$ accounts for moisture evaporation, dust, and handling losses (typically **0.5 – 1.5 %** of $M_w$).

The **extraction rate ($E_r$, %)** is defined as:

$$E_r = \frac{M_f}{M_w} \times 100$$

**Numerical example (KTU 2024 standard):**
A roller mill processes 1000 kg of clean hard wheat per hour. The combined flour streams yield 720 kg of bread-grade flour, 200 kg of bran, 30 kg of germ, and 50 kg of measurable losses.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_r &= \frac{M_f}{M_w} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{720}{1000} \times 100 \\
&= 72.0\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 72 % extraction rate is classified as a **straight-grade patent flour**. White pan breads are typically made from **70 – 75 %** extraction flour. A drop from 72 % to 70 % means **2 % of additional endosperm is being removed** — improving brightness and ash control but reducing yield (and economic margin).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Baker's Percentage System

Let total dough weight be $D_{total}$, with individual ingredient weights $w_i$ (flour, water, salt, etc.).

The sum-of-ingredients identity is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = D_{total} - w_{loss} \quad \text{(moisture, oven loss)}$$

The **baker's percentage ($B_i$, %)** for ingredient *i* is anchored on flour weight $w_{flour} = 100$:

$$B_i = \frac{w_i}{w_{flour}} \times 100$$

The **total dough formula (% of flour)** is:

$$D_{formula} = 100 + B_{water} + B_{salt} + B_{sugar} + B_{fat} + B_{yeast} + \cdots$$

**Worked example (White Pan Bread — 1000 g flour batch):**

Given: Flour = 1000 g, Water = 620 g, Salt = 20 g, Sugar = 40 g, Fat = 30 g, Yeast = 25 g, Improver = 2 g.

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{flour} &= \frac{1000}{1000} \times 100 = 100.0\ \% \\
B_{water} &= \frac{620}{1000} \times 100 = 62.0\ \% \\
B_{salt} &= \frac{20}{1000} \times 100 = 2.0\ \% \\
B_{sugar} &= \frac{40}{1000} \times 100 = 4.0\ \% \\
B_{fat} &= \frac{30}{1000} \times 100 = 3.0\ \% \\
B_{yeast} &= \frac{25}{1000} \times 100 = 2.5\ \% \\
B_{improver} &= \frac{2}{1000} \times 100 = 0.2\ \% \\
\end{aligned}$$

Sum of baker's percentages:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{formula} &= 100 + 62 + 2.0 + 4.0 + 3.0 + 2.5 + 0.2 \\
&= 173.7\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Insight:** The sum of baker's percentages **does NOT equal 100** — it always exceeds 100 because flour is anchored as the base. A typical pan bread formula totals **170 – 180 %**, and a typical biscuit formula totals **180 – 220 %** (more sugar and fat).

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Mechanism: Gluten Formation (Hydration → Development)

This is the **most exam-relevant functional mechanism** in Module 1. We expand each step because it is commonly asked for **7-mark sub-parts** in KTU ESE.

**Step 1 — Dry state (before water addition).**
In the flour, glutenin (high-MW, ~10⁶ Da) and gliadin (low-MW, ~30,000–80,000 Da) exist as **discrete, collapsed protein bodies** interspersed among starch granules. No intermolecular bonds are present.

**Step 2 — Water addition (hydration phase, 0 – 5 min).**
Water is preferentially absorbed by hydrophilic sites on the protein surface (–OH, –COOH, –NH₂). Water molecules form **monolayer and multilayer hydrogen-bonded shells** around protein molecules. Approximate water uptake of flour = **30 % of flour weight** within the first 5 minutes.

$$\text{Water uptake (W)} = \frac{w_{abs}}{w_{flour}} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Mixing (mechanical energy input, 5 – 15 min).**
Mechanical work (paddle/hook action) brings hydrated protein molecules into close contact, promoting:
- **Disulfide–sulfhydryl interchange reactions** between –SS– bonds of glutenin and –SH of gliadin
- **Hydrophobic interactions** between non-polar side chains
- **Hydrogen bonds** along the peptide backbone

**Step 4 — Gluten network formation.**
Glutenin polymers cross-link via disulfide bridges to form an **elastic, continuous 3D network**. Gliadin monomers fill the interstices, providing **plasticity and flow**. The resulting viscoelastic mass is the **gluten complex**.

$$\text{Glutenin (elastic)} + \text{Gliadin (plastic)} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O + Energy}} \text{Gluten complex (viscoelastic)}$$

**Step 5 — Effect of salt.**
Salt ions (Na⁺ and Cl⁻) **shield the charged side chains** (–COO⁻, –NH₃⁺) of proteins, **decreasing electrostatic repulsion** and allowing the protein chains to come closer. This **tightens the gluten mesh**, increases dough **stability and resistance to extension**, and reduces **tackiness**.

**Step 6 — Effect of continued mixing (over-mixing).**
Excess mechanical work cleaves disulfide bonds and physically shears the gluten strands → dough becomes **soft, sticky, slack, and short** (over-developed). Farinograph stability time drops.

**Step 7 — Effect of resting (autolyse).**
Standing time (20 – 60 min) allows even water distribution and full hydration; gluten re-aligns. Dough extensibility increases.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Mechanism: Starch Gelatinisation During Baking

**Step 1 — Below 50 °C:**
Starch granules (semi-crystalline) absorb water slowly; native structure intact.

**Step 2 — 50 – 60 °C (onset of gelatinisation):**
Water penetrates the amorphous regions of the granule. Granule begins to swell.

**Step 3 — 60 – 80 °C (gelatinisation range, T_gelatinisation):**
- Wheat starch $T_{gel}$ ≈ **58 – 64 °C**
- Maize starch $T_{gel}$ ≈ **62 – 72 °C**
- Rice starch $T_{gel}$ ≈ **68 – 78 °C**

Crystallinity is lost; amylose leaches out; granule swells **3 – 5×** original volume.

**Step 4 — 80 – 95 °C (peak swelling):**
Maximum water uptake. Viscosity peaks (amylograph).

**Step 5 — On cooling (retrogradation):**
Amylose re-associates into a 3D network → **crumb-setting**. Over 24 – 72 h, amylopectin recrystallises → **staling (bread firming)**.

$$\text{Staling Rate} \propto f(\text{amylopectin retrogradation, moisture migration})$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Cause-Effect Relationships in Baking (High-Yield)

| Variable Changed | Effect on Dough | Effect on Final Product |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Flour protein (e.g., 10 % → 13 %) | Tighter, more elastic | Smaller loaf volume, chewier crumb |
| ↑ Water (+5 %) | Softer, slack, sticky | Larger volume, more open crumb, possible collapse |
| ↑ Salt (+0.5 %) | Tighter, less sticky, slower proof | Finer crumb, paler crust, better flavour |
| ↓ Salt (omission) | Slack, sticky, fast fermenting | Coarse crumb, sour, very dark crust |
| ↑ Damaged starch (5 % → 12 %) | More water-absorbing, sticky | Gummy crumb, reduced volume |
| Soft flour replacing hard | Weaker, more extensible | Tender cake/cookie; cannot make bread |
| Whole wheat replacing white | Lower gluten development, coarser | Denser, more flavourful, lower volume |
| Bleached flour replacing unbleached | Slightly stronger gluten (Cl₂ treatment) | Finer crumb, whiter, softer cake |

## 3.6 Numerical Problem — Solving Water Absorption Adjustment

**Problem:** A bakery uses 10 kg of flour with a farinograph water absorption of **62.0 %**. They substitute **30 % of bread flour with whole-wheat flour** having WA of **75.0 %**. What is the **new water absorption** of the blend (baker's percent)?

**Solution — Step 1:** Determine effective WA of the blend.

$$\begin{aligned}
WA_{blend} &= (0.70 \times WA_{bread}) + (0.30 \times WA_{whole}) \\
&= (0.70 \times 62.0) + (0.30 \times 75.0) \\
&= 43.4 + 22.5 \\
&= 65.9\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to actual water weight for a 10 kg flour batch.

$$w_{water} = 10 \times 0.659 = 6.59 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3:** Working salt at 2.0 % baker's percent.

$$w_{salt} = 10 \times 0.020 = 0.20 \text{ kg (200 g)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Standard:** Always express final answers to **one decimal place** for water absorption (e.g., 65.9 %) and **two decimal places** for absolute weights in kg or g.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wheat Kernel — Anatomy and Milling Fractions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheat Kernel Triticum aestivum] --> B[Endosperm 82 to 84 percent]
    A --> C[ Bran 13 to 17 percent]
    A --> D[Germ Embryo 2 to 3 percent]
    B --> B1[Starchy endosperm]
    B --> B2[Peripheral aleurone]
    C --> C1[Pericarp outer]
    C --> C2[Testa pigment]
    C --> C3[Aleurone inner]
    D --> D1[Embryonic axis]
    D --> D2[Scutellum]
    B1 --> E1[Straight flour 70 to 75 percent extraction]
    B1 --> E2[Patent flour 35 to 45 percent]
    B1 --> E3[Clear flour 25 to 30 percent]
    C3 --> F1[Whole wheat flour 95 to 100 percent]
    C3 --> F2[Atta chapatti flour 90 to 95 percent]
    D2 --> G1[Germ fraction high oil]
```

## 4.2 Flour Classification by Wheat Type and End-Use

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FW[Flour] --> A[Hard wheat flour]
    FW --> B[Soft wheat flour]
    FW --> C[Durum wheat semolina]
    FW --> D[Specialty flour]
    A --> A1[Bread flour 12 to 14 percent protein]
    A --> A2[High gluten flour 14 percent plus]
    A --> A3[All purpose flour 10 to 12 percent]
    B --> B1[Cake pastry flour 7 to 9 percent protein]
    B --> B2[Biscuit cookie flour 8 to 10 percent]
    C --> C1[Semolina coarse]
    C --> C2[Durum fine]
    D --> D1[Self rising flour]
    D --> D2[Whole wheat atta]
    D --> D3[Gluten free rice maize sorghum]
    D --> D4[Vital wheat gluten 75 percent protein]
    D --> D5[Pre gelatinized flour]
    A1 --> P1[Bread buns pizza bagels]
    B1 --> P2[Sponge cake muffins cookies]
    C1 --> P3[Pasta noodles]
    D3 --> P4[Celiac safe products]
```

## 4.3 Functional Role Map of Water in Dough

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Water in dough] --> F1[Hydration of glutenin and gliadin]
    W --> F2[Starch granule swelling]
    W --> F3[Enzyme activation amylase protease]
    W --> F4[Yeast metabolism substrate]
    W --> F5[Salt sugar dissolution]
    W --> F6[Heat transfer medium]
    W --> F7[Control of dough rheology]
    F1 --> G1[Gluten network formation viscoelasticity]
    F2 --> G2[Gas retention crumb structure]
    F3 --> G3[Sugar release from starch]
    F4 --> G4[Carbon dioxide gas production]
    F5 --> G5[Homogeneous dough system]
    F6 --> G6[Uniform baking crumb setting]
    F7 --> G7[Final product moisture and shelf life]
```

## 4.4 Salt's Mode of Action in Dough System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Salt NaCl] --> M1[Flavour enhancement]
    S --> M2[Gluten mesh tightening]
    S --> M3[Yeast retardation]
    S --> M4[Crust colour moderation]
    S --> M5[Osmotic balance]
    S --> M6[Dough handling improvement]
    M1 --> R1[Better bread taste]
    M2 --> R2[Stronger dough more gas retention]
    M3 --> R3[Controlled proofing finer texture]
    M4 --> R4[Uniform golden crust]
    M5 --> R5[Stable yeast cell metabolism]
    M6 --> R6[Less sticky easier machining]
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture of Bakery Raw Material Interaction

```mermaid
    end

    end

    end

        GZ[Gelatinisation 60 to 80 C]

    end

    end
    F --> MX
    WT --> MX
    ST --> MX
    MX --> DN
    MX --> GM
```

## 4.6 Comparative Composition Wheel of Different Flour Types

```mermaid
    P2[All Purpose 11 percent protein] --> A2[Balanced gluten medium absorption]

    P4[Whole Wheat 12 percent protein] --> A4[Strong but interrupted by bran medium absorption]
    P5[Semolina 13 percent protein] --> A5[Coarse granular high pigment yellow]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "extraction rate" of wheat flour. What is the typical extraction range for straight-grade bread flour and whole wheat atta?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Extraction rate is the **percentage of clean wheat grain that is converted into flour** during the milling process.

$$\text{Extraction Rate \%} = \frac{\text{Mass of flour obtained}}{\text{Mass of clean wheat}} \times 100$$

- **Straight-grade bread flour:** typically **70 – 75 %** extraction (pure endosperm).
- **Whole wheat atta:** typically **95 – 100 %** extraction (retains bran and germ).

Higher extraction rate increases fibre, ash, and vitamin content, but reduces bread volume and crumb whiteness due to bran particles interrupting the gluten network.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any six functional roles of water in bakery products.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. Hydrates flour proteins to form the gluten network
2. Hydrates and gelatinises starch during baking
3. Dissolves salt, sugar, and other soluble ingredients
4. Activates native enzymes (amylases, proteases)
5. Serves as the medium for yeast fermentation
6. Controls dough consistency and final moisture content

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a) Classify the different types of wheat flour based on wheat hardness and ash content. Explain their characteristic protein ranges and end-uses. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification by wheat hardness:**

| Flour Type | Wheat Class | Protein (%) | End-Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hard/Strong (Bread) flour | Hard red wheat | 11.5 – 14.0 | Bread, buns, pizza, rolls |
| Medium (All-purpose/Maida) | Hard + Soft blend | 9.0 – 11.5 | Cakes, biscuits, general baking |
| Soft (Cake/Pastry) | Soft red/white wheat | 6.5 – 9.0 | Cakes, pastries, cookies |
| Durum semolina | *Triticum durum* | 12.0 – 15.0 | Pasta, noodles, some breads |

Hard wheat has a vitreous, translucent endosperm and high protein; soft wheat has a mealy, opaque endosperm and low protein.

**Classification by ash content (mineral residue):**

| Flour Grade | Max Ash (% d.b.) | End-Use |
|---|---|---|
| First patent (clear) | ≤ 0.40 | Confectionery, fine cakes |
| Second clear | 0.40 – 0.65 | General baking |
| Straight grade | 0.65 – 0.80 | Bread, biscuit |
| Whole wheat atta | 1.50 – 2.00 | Chapatti, brown bread |

Lower ash corresponds to purer endosperm flour. Higher ash means more bran, more minerals, darker colour.

**(b) Discuss the specifications of water used in bakery. Why is chilled water preferred for dough retardation? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Specifications of water for bakery use:**

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| TDS | ≤ 500 mg/L (acceptable) |
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| Total hardness | 100 – 300 mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| Iron (Fe) | ≤ 0.3 mg/L |
| Chloride | ≤ 250 mg/L |
| Microbiological | Coliforms absent in 100 mL |

**Why chilled water (4 – 10 °C) is preferred for dough retardation:**

1. **Slows yeast activity** — reduced metabolic rate at low temperature slows CO₂ generation, extending the proofing window without over-fermentation.
2. **Controls acid development** — lactic and acetic acid production by lactic acid bacteria is suppressed, preventing sourness in retarded dough.
3. **Reduces enzyme activity** — α-amylase and protease are less active, preserving starch integrity and gluten strength.
4. **Delays dough collapse** — controlled gas retention during cold storage (0 – 4 °C).
5. **Improves flavour development** — slow cold fermentation produces more flavour esters and alcohols.
6. **Operational scheduling** — allows overnight proofing or weekend bake-off planning.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) Explain the mechanism of gluten formation in wheat flour dough. How does salt modify the gluten network? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism of gluten formation (step-by-step):**

1. **Native state:** Glutenin (elastic, MW ~10⁶ Da) and gliadin (plastic, MW ~30,000 – 80,000 Da) exist as discrete proteins in the flour.
2. **Hydration:** Water molecules form hydrogen bonds with –OH, –COOH, and –NH₂ groups on the protein surface.
3. **Mechanical work:** Mixing brings hydrated proteins into contact, promoting disulfide–sulfhydryl interchange (–SS– ↔ –SH).
4. **Network formation:** Glutenin forms a continuous elastic 3D network via disulfide cross-links; gliadin fills interstices, providing plasticity.
5. **Viscoelastic complex:** The resulting mass is viscoelastic — elastic from glutenin and plastic from gliadin.

**Effect of salt (NaCl):**

- Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions **shield the charged side chains** of gluten proteins (–COO⁻, –NH₃⁺).
- This **reduces electrostatic repulsion** between protein molecules.
- Protein chains come closer, forming **more disulfide cross-links** and a **tighter gluten mesh**.
- Dough becomes **less sticky, more resistant to extension, and more stable** during fermentation.
- Fermentation is **slowed by 20 – 30 %** → more controlled gas retention, finer crumb.

**(b) Compare hard bread flour and soft cake flour across all major specification parameters. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hard Bread Flour | Soft Cake Flour |
|---|---|---|
| Wheat type | Hard red winter/spring | Soft red/white |
| Protein (%) | 11.5 – 14.0 | 6.5 – 9.0 |
| Wet gluten (%) | 28 – 36 | 18 – 24 |
| Damaged starch (%) | 6 – 10 | 3 – 5 |
| Ash (%, d.b.) | 0.50 – 0.65 | 0.36 – 0.45 |
| Water absorption (%) | 60 – 70 | 48 – 55 |
| Particle size (µm) | 100 – 200 (coarse) | 60 – 100 (fine) |
| Granulation | Granular, sharp | Fine, smooth |
| Colour (K/J) | 2.0 – 4.0 K | 1.0 – 2.5 K |
| Falling Number (s) | 250 – 350 | 280 – 350 |
| End-product | Bread, buns, pizza | Sponge cake, pastry |
| Dough character | Strong, elastic, tight | Weak, extensible, tender |
| Crumb texture | Chewy, fine uniform | Tender, soft, delicate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often **confuse protein % with gluten %**. **Protein (Nx5.7)** is the *total Kjeldahl nitrogen × 5.7*, while **wet gluten** is the *gluten mass obtained by washing dough under a standardised procedure*. A flour with 12 % protein may yield 30 – 35 % wet gluten. Marking penalty: **–1 mark** if these are mixed up.
> Also avoid stating that **strong flour is "better"** — for cake/cookie application, soft flour is correct; using bread flour in cakes would produce a tough, inedible product.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1**

- ✅ **Flour** is the **primary structural substrate** — provides starch (energy) and gluten-forming proteins (structure).
- ✅ **Gluten = Glutenin (elastic) + Gliadin (plastic)** — formed upon hydration and mechanical work.
- ✅ **Hard wheat = high protein = strong gluten = bread**. **Soft wheat = low protein = weak gluten = cake**.
- ✅ **Extraction rate = (flour mass / wheat mass) × 100**. Patent ≈ 40 – 45 %; straight ≈ 70 – 75 %; whole wheat ≈ 95 – 100 %.
- ✅ **Water absorption** is increased by **high protein, high damaged starch, high pentosans, fine granulation**.
- ✅ **Baker's percentage anchors flour at 100 %** — every other ingredient is a percentage of flour weight.
- ✅ **Salt (1.8 – 2.2 % baker's % in bread)** tightens gluten, slows yeast ~20 – 30 %, enhances flavour, and moderates Maillard browning.
- ✅ **Omission of salt** → slack, sticky dough, coarse acidic crumb, dark crust.
- ✅ **Damaged starch** is created intentionally during milling to provide α-amylase substrate for fermentation gas.
- ✅ **Wheat is the only cereal with functional gluten**; rye contains weaker gluten; oats contain avenin; rice/maize/sorghum are gluten-free.
- ✅ **Falling Number ≥ 250 s** indicates sound, non-sprouted wheat; low FN indicates sprouting and excessive α-amylase (slack, sticky dough).
- ✅ **Chilled water (4 – 10 °C)** is used for **dough retardation** — slows yeast, controls acid, preserves gluten.
- ✅ **BIS / FSSAI standards**: flour moisture ≤ 14 %; coliforms absent in 0.1 g; salt NaCl ≥ 99.5 %; iodisation ≥ 15 ppm iodine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Quick-Reference Keywords (use in answers to score bonus marks):**
> *Viscoelastic, hygroscopic, colloidal, gelatinisation, retrogradation, amylose leaching, disulfide cross-link, farinograph, alveograph, extraction rate, falling number, water activity, baker's percentage, autolyse, retardation, Maillard, pentosan, damaged starch.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
