## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
**List any three functions of sugar in bakery products.**

**Model Answer:**

The three important functions of sugar in bakery products are:

1. **Fermentation Substrate:** Sugar (especially glucose, fructose, maltose) is metabolized by yeast to produce **carbon dioxide and ethanol**, which leavens the dough and contributes to flavor.

2. **Crust Browning:** Reducing sugars participate in the **Maillard reaction** with amino acids during baking (> 140 °C), forming brown melanoidin pigments and characteristic aroma compounds.

3. **Tenderization (Anti-Gluten Effect):** Sugar competes with gluten proteins for available water, delaying protein hydration and limiting gluten development, resulting in a **softer, finer crumb** texture.

> (Any three correctly described functions — full 3 marks)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**  
**Differentiate between biological and chemical leavening agents with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Biological Leavening | Chemical Leavening |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Yeast metabolizes sugars → CO₂ + ethanol | Acid–base reaction → CO₂ |
| **Speed** | Slow (requires fermentation time) | Fast (reacts immediately on hydration/heating) |
| **By-products** | Ethanol, organic acids, flavor compounds | Salt residues (Na/K salts) |
| **Example** | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (Baker's yeast) | Baking powder (NaHCO₃ + acid) |
| **Application** | Bread, pizza, rolls | Cakes, cookies, biscuits |

> (Tabular comparison with one example each → 3 marks)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
**(a)** Describe in detail the **role and types of production enzymes** used in bakery. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Explain the **multifunctional roles of sugar** in bakery and confectionery products. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Part (a) — Production Enzymes in Bakery (7 Marks)**

**Definition:**
Production enzymes are biocatalysts added to flour/dough that accelerate specific biochemical transformations, enhancing dough rheology, fermentation, crumb structure, and shelf life.

**Major Enzymes & Functions:**

**1. Amylases (3 marks coverage)**
- *Fungal α-amylase (FAA):* Hydrolyzes damaged starch → maltose + dextrins → provides fermentable sugars → enhances gassing power and crust browning.
- *Maltogenic α-amylase (MAA):* Survives baking (> 90 °C); modifies amylopectin → reduces retrogradation → extends shelf life, softens crumb.

**2. Proteases (1.5 marks)**
- Hydrolyze gluten proteins → controlled softening → improves biscuit spread, dough extensibility, mix tolerance.

**3. Xylanases / Hemicellulases (1.5 marks)**
- Degrade insoluble pentosans → release water → improve dough machinability, oven spring, crumb uniformity.

**4. Other enzymes (1 mark)**
- **Lipase:** In-situ emulsifier formation.
- **Transglutaminase:** Cross-links gluten → strength.
- **Glucose oxidase:** Generates H₂O₂ → oxidative gelation.

**Industrial advantages:** Clean-label, replace chemical oxidizers (bromate), controlled dosage, GRAS status.

---

### **Part (b) — Multifunctional Roles of Sugar (7 Marks)**

**1. Sweetening Agent (1 mark)**
Direct sensory perception; threshold differs by sugar type (fructose sweetest).

**2. Fermentation Substrate (1.5 marks)**
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \to 2\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$
Provides energy and CO₂ for yeast — essential in yeast-leavened products.

**3. Tenderization — Anti-Gluten Effect (1 mark)**
Sugar binds water → delays gluten hydration → softer texture. High-ratio cakes have > 100 % sugar on flour weight.

**4. Browning Reactions (2 marks)**
- **Maillard Reaction** (reducing sugars + amino groups → melanoidins; > 140 °C).
- **Caramelization** (thermal degradation of sugars; > 160 °C) → color + flavor.

**5. Moisture Retention / Humectancy (1 mark)**
Sugar binds water via hydrogen bonding → retards staling, extends freshness (esp. in cakes, marshmallows).

**6. Structural / Aeration Aid (0.5 marks)**
In creaming and meringues, sugar stabilizes foam structure by raising viscosity at air–liquid interfaces.

---

#### **QUESTION B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
**(a)** Classify **leavening agents** in detail with their mechanisms of action. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Discuss the **functions of milk** in bakery products with reference to its major components. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Part (a) — Classification of Leavening Agents (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** Leavening agents generate gas within a dough/batter to produce a porous, aerated cellular crumb.

**Classification (with mechanisms):**

**1. Biological Leavening — Yeast (3 marks)**
- Organism: *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*
- Mechanism: Fermentation of sugars → CO₂ + ethanol + flavor.
- Reaction: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \to 2\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$
- Conditions: pH 4.5–6.0, 30–35 °C.
- Used in: Bread, pizza, croissants.

**2. Chemical Leavening (2.5 marks)**
- **Baking Soda (NaHCO₃):** Alkaline; needs acid for CO₂ release; used with buttermilk, vinegar, molasses.
- **Baking Powder:** Mixture of NaHCO₃ + acid (cream of tartar / SAPP / MCP) + starch (buffer).
  - Fast-acting → reacts at room temperature.
  - Slow-acting → reacts at oven temperature (60–65 °C).
  - Double-acting → two-stage release.
- Used in: Cakes, cookies, pancakes.

**3. Physical Leavening (1.5 marks)**
- **Steam:** Water → vapor at 100 °C → 1700× volume expansion.
  - Used in: Puff pastry, choux, popovers.
- **Mechanical aeration:** Whipping air into fat (creaming) or egg whites (sponge).
  - Used in: Sponge cakes, meringues, angel food cakes.

---

### **Part (b) — Functions of Milk in Bakery Products (7 Marks)**

**Introduction:**
Milk is a complete food supplied in bakery as whole milk, skim milk, condensed milk, milk powder, or whey. It contains lactose, casein, whey proteins, milk fat, calcium, and vitamins.

**Functional Contributions:**

**1. Crust Browning — Lactose (2 marks)**
- Lactose is a reducing disaccharide → reacts with proteins via **Maillard reaction** → deep golden-brown crust.
- Gives bread, brioche, and milk loaves their characteristic color and aroma.

**2. Nutritional Fortification (1.5 marks)**
- High-quality proteins (casein, lactalbumin), calcium, phosphorus, vitamins A, D, B-complex.
- Increases biological value of baked products.

**3. Dough Strengthening — Milk Proteins (1.5 marks)**
- Casein forms protein film; whey proteins coagulate on heating → reinforces crumb structure.
- Calcium ions cross-link caseinate → improves texture.

**4. Flavor Enhancement (1 mark)**
- Rich, creamy mouthfeel; masks raw flour taste.
- Buttermilk → lactic acid → tangy flavor (sourdough-style).

**5. Buffering Capacity (0.5 marks)**
- pH 6.6–6.8 → stabilizes fermentation pH → prevents over-acidification in long-proof doughs.

**6. Shelf-Life Extension (0.5 marks)**
- Lactose retains moisture → softer crumb over time.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ **DO NOT** confuse **physical leavening** (steam/air) with **chemical leavening**. Steam leavening is purely a phase change — no chemical reaction occurs.
> - ❌ **DO NOT** claim **sucrose participates directly in Maillard reaction**. Sucrose is **non-reducing**; it must first be hydrolyzed to glucose + fructose.
> - ❌ **DO NOT** list "texture improvement" alone for enzymes. Always state the **substrate** (e.g., damaged starch, gluten, pentosans).
> - ✅ **Always** include the **enzyme name**, **substrate**, **product**, and **functional outcome** in tabular form for enzyme-related 14-mark questions.
> - ✅ In baking powder questions, **always specify the acid type** (fast, slow, double-acting) along with examples.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Production Enzymes
- **Fungal α-amylase** = starch hydrolysis (dough conditioning).
- **Maltogenic α-amylase** = anti-staling agent (active in baking).
- **Proteases** = controlled gluten breakdown (biscuit texture).
- **Xylanases** = pentosan hydrolysis (dough relaxation).
- **Transglutaminase** = protein crosslinking (dough strengthening).

### 🔑 Sugar Functions (Mnemonic: **"S-F-T-B-M-H"**)
- **S**weetness
- **F**ermentation substrate
- **T**enderization
- **B**rowning (Maillard + Caramelization)
- **M**oisture retention
- **H**umectant / Antimicrobial at high levels

### 🔑 Milk Functions (Mnemonic: **"M-C-N-F"**)
- **M**aillard browning (lactose)
- **C**alcium + Casein (dough strength)
- **N**utrition
- **F**lavor enhancement

### 🔑 Leavening Agents (Mnemonic: **"B-C-P"**)
- **B**iological (Yeast → CO₂ + ethanol)
- **C**hemical (NaHCO₃ + acid → CO₂; includes fast/slow/double-acting baking powders)
- **P**hysical (Steam 1700× expansion; mechanical air incorporation)

### 🔑 Critical Thresholds
| Threshold | Value |
|---|---|
| Yeast sugar tolerance | < 60 % S/F |
| Salt inhibition of yeast | > 2 % on flour wt |
| Caramelization temperature | > 160 °C |
| Maillard active | > 140 °C |
| Yeast optimum temperature | 30–35 °C |
| Steam expansion ratio | ~1700× at 100 °C |
| Theoretical CO₂ from glucose | 48.86 % by mass |
| Theoretical CO₂ from NaHCO₃ | 52.4 % by mass |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Tip for KTU Exams:** Always write the **equation + substrate + product + outcome** for enzymes; the **function + threshold** for sugar; the **component + role** for milk; and the **class + example + mechanism** for leavening. This structure fetches full marks under KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Baking Technology: Major Baking Ingredients and Their Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT412 - BAKERY AND CONFECTIONERY PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Baking Technology: Major Baking Ingredients and Their Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of industrial baking technology, **major baking ingredients** are the foundational raw materials that are systematically combined in defined proportions to form doughs or batters, which upon controlled application of heat, undergo simultaneous **physical**, **chemical**, and **biochemical** transformations to yield finished bakery products such as bread, cakes, biscuits, and pastries.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Bakery and Confectionery Products, the major baking ingredients are conventionally classified into **eight functional categories**:

1. **Flour (the structural backbone)**
2. **Sugar (the sweetener & tenderizer)**
3. **Fats and Oils (the shortening / lubricants)**
4. **Eggs (the emulsifiers & structure builders)**
5. **Leavening Agents (the aerators)**
6. **Liquids (water / milk — the hydrating medium)**
7. **Salt (the flavor regulator & gluten controller)**
8. **Flavoring & Auxiliary Ingredients (spices, fruits, chocolate, etc.)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Every question on this topic is expected to test the **functional role** of each ingredient — *what it does, why it is essential, and what happens if it is missing or substituted*. Memorizing ingredient lists alone will not fetch marks; understanding the **functional chemistry** is mandatory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine that you are constructing a **building** 🏢:
- **Flour** is the *concrete and steel framework* — it gives shape and holds everything together.
- **Sugar** is the *interior decoration* — it makes the product sweet, soft, and appealing.
- **Fat** is the *lubricating oil in the machinery* — it keeps things tender and prevents the structure from becoming too rigid.
- **Eggs** are the *skilled laborers* — they bind, emulsify, and aerate simultaneously.
- **Leavening agents** are the *air-conditioning system* — they pump gas (CO₂) into the structure to make it light.
- **Liquids** are the *water supply* — they hydrate the dry materials and activate reactions.
- **Salt** is the *quality-control supervisor* — tiny amount, but without it, the whole operation goes out of control.
- **Flavoring agents** are the *paint and furnishings* — they give the final aesthetic identity.

Without any one of these "departments", the building either collapses, becomes unappetizing, or simply never forms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuitive Principle:** Baking is essentially a *controlled hydration and aeration science*. Almost every ingredient either **provides structure**, **modifies structure**, or **creates gas to inflate the structure**. Keeping this triad (Structure ↔ Modifier ↔ Aerator) in mind makes the entire syllabus intuitively logical.

### 1.3 Standard Baking Metrics (Memorize These)

- **Baker's Percentage Reference Base**: Flour weight is **always taken as 100 %**.
- **Hydration Ratio** (typical bread dough): **60 – 75 %** of flour weight.
- **Standard Salt Level**: **1.8 – 2.2 %** of flour weight.
- **Standard Yeast Level (compressed)**: **2 – 3 %** of flour weight in bread.
- **Cake Hydration**: Typically **100 – 130 %** (batter system, not dough).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Functional Categories — Step-by-Step Logic

#### 2.1.1 FLOUR — The Structural Backbone

- **What it is**: A finely ground powder obtained by milling cereal grains (wheat being the dominant choice for yeast-leavened products).
- **Key components**:
  - **Starch** (~70 %) → provides bulk, gelatinizes with heat, sets the crumb.
  - **Gluten proteins** (gliadin + glutenin, ~10–12 %) → form the viscoelastic network when hydrated.
  - **Enzymes** (amylases, proteases) → catalyze starch and protein breakdown.
  - **Lipids** (~1–2 %) → minor but functional.
- **Why it matters**: When water is added and the dough is mechanically developed, glutenin and gliadin hydrate and align to form a **gluten network** that traps the gas produced by yeast or chemical leaveners.
- **Functional Substitutes**: Rye flour (lower gluten), rice flour (gluten-free), whole wheat (higher fiber).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** When asked "why is flour called the structural backbone of bakery products?", always answer in terms of the **gluten-starch matrix** — never just say "it gives body".

#### 2.1.2 SUGAR — The Sweetener, Tenderizer, and Browning Agent

- **Forms used**: Sucrose (most common), dextrose, fructose, invert sugar, maltose, honey, corn syrup.
- **Five Major Functions**:
  1. **Sweetness** — primary sensory role.
  2. **Tenderization** — interferes with gluten formation (competes for water).
  3. **Moisture retention** — hygroscopic; extends shelf-life (especially in cakes).
  4. **Browning** — participates in the **Maillard reaction** and **caramelization** at high oven temperatures.
  5. **Fermentation fuel** — yeast metabolizes sugar to CO₂ and ethanol.
- **Granulation matters**: Fine sugar dissolves faster (cakes), coarse sugar gives texture (muffin tops).

#### 2.1.3 FATS AND OILS — The Shortening / Tenderizing Agent

- **Forms**: Butter, margarine, shortening (hydrogenated vegetable fat), lard, and liquid oils.
- **Functional Roles**:
  1. **Shortening effect** — fat coats flour particles, physically preventing gluten from over-developing → tender, crumbly texture.
  2. **Aeration** — in creaming method, fat traps air bubbles that expand during baking.
  3. **Flavor carrier** — fat is a medium for fat-soluble flavors.
  4. **Moisture retention** — reduces staling by slowing starch retrogradation.
  5. **Lubrication** — improves mouthfeel and machinability of dough.

#### 2.1.4 EGGS — The Multi-Tasking Functional Ingredient

Eggs are arguably the most *versatile* ingredient in baking:
- **Structure**: Egg proteins (ovalbumin, conalbumin) coagulate on heating → set the crumb.
- **Emulsification**: Lecithin in the yolk stabilizes fat-water mixtures (key in mayo-style batters).
- **Aeration (Whipping)**: Egg whites foam when whipped → angel food cakes, meringues, sponge cakes.
- **Color**: Yolk contributes yellow color (carotenoids) and participates in Maillard browning.
- **Flavor**: Adds richness.

#### 2.1.5 LEAVENING AGENTS — The Aerators

Two broad classes:
- **Biological**: Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) → produces CO₂ via fermentation.
- **Chemical**:
  - **Baking soda** (NaHCO₃) → needs acidic activator.
  - **Baking powder** → NaHCO₃ + acid salt (e.g., cream of tartar) + buffer.
- **Mechanical**: Air incorporation by creaming, whisking, lamination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** For 7-mark questions, students must be able to differentiate between **single-acting** and **double-acting** baking powders. Single-acting reacts at room temperature upon wetting (used in refrigerated biscuits). Double-acting has a slow-acting acid that reacts only at oven temperatures (used in cakes baked immediately after mixing).

#### 2.1.6 LIQUIDS — The Hydrating Medium

- **Sources**: Water, milk, buttermilk, fruit juices, eggs (also liquid).
- **Roles**:
  1. Hydrates gluten proteins (essential for network formation).
  2. Dissolves sugar and salt → ensures uniform distribution.
  3. Activates yeast and enzymes.
  4. Controls batter/dough consistency.
  5. Contributes to steam in the oven → aids oven spring.

#### 2.1.7 SALT — The Tiny-but-Critical Regulator

- Used at **1.8 – 2.2 %** of flour weight.
- **Functions**:
  1. **Flavor enhancement** (suppresses bitterness).
  2. **Gluten tightening** — strengthens the gluten network.
  3. **Yeast control** — slows fermentation (prevents over-rising).
  4. **Microbial control** — slight preservative effect.
- **Consequence of omission**: Dough over-ferments, becomes sticky, lacks flavor, and stales faster.

#### 2.1.8 FLAVORING & AUXILIARY INGREDIENTS

- Spices, vanilla, cocoa, fruits, nuts, chocolate chips, dried fruits, food colors.
- Typically added at the **end of mixing** to preserve volatile aromatic compounds.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Baker's Percentage (Ingredient) | $\text{BP}\% = \dfrac{W_{\text{ingredient}}}{W_{\text{flour}}} \times 100$ | Flour is always 100 % baseline |
| 2 | Total Dough Weight | $W_{\text{total}} = \sum W_{\text{all ingredients}}$ | grams or kg |
| 3 | Hydration Ratio | $H = \dfrac{W_{\text{liquid}}}{W_{\text{flour}}} \times 100$ | Bread: 60 – 75 %; Cake: 100 – 130 % |
| 4 | Sugar-to-Flour Ratio | $S/F = \dfrac{W_{\text{sugar}}}{W_{\text{flour}}} \times 100$ | Sweet bread > 25 %; cake batter 80 – 130 % |
| 5 | Fat-to-Flour Ratio | $F/F = \dfrac{W_{\text{fat}}}{W_{\text{flour}}} \times 100$ | Pound cake ~100 %; biscuits 30 – 50 % |
| 6 | Salt Standard | $S_{pct} = 1.8$ to $2.2$ | % of flour weight |
| 7 | Yeast Standard (compressed) | $Y_{pct} = 2$ to $3$ | % of flour weight |
| 8 | CO₂ Yield (Yeast Fermentation) | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$ | 1 mol glucose → 2 mol CO₂ |
| 9 | Gluten Yield Estimate | $G \approx 10$ to $12$ | % of flour weight |
| 10 | Baking Soda Neutralization | $\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$ | 1 mol acid → 1 mol CO₂ |
| 11 | Oven Spring Index | $\text{OSI} = \dfrac{H_{\text{final}} - H_{\text{initial}}}{H_{\text{initial}}} \times 100$ | Measure of leavening efficiency |
| 12 | Moisture Loss in Baking | $M_{\text{loss}} = W_{\text{raw}} - W_{\text{baked}}$ | Typically 10 – 20 % of dough weight |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Baker's Percentage system is the **universal language** of industrial bakeries worldwide. It allows bakers across continents to scale up recipes from 1 kg to 1,000 kg without recalculating every proportion. Every KTU numerical / calculation problem on ingredients is built on this single foundation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Baker's Percentage for a Standard Bread Recipe

**Given Recipe (bread dough for one batch):**
- Wheat flour = 1000 g
- Water = 650 g
- Salt = 20 g
- Sugar = 30 g
- Fat (shortening) = 20 g
- Compressed yeast = 25 g
- Milk powder = 20 g

**Required:** Convert all ingredients into Baker's Percentages.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Reference Base

The reference base is always **flour weight**.

$$
W_{\text{flour}} = 1000 \text{ g} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Flour BP} = 100\%
$$

This is the **only** ingredient that is always taken as 100 %.

#### Step 2 — Apply the Baker's Percentage Formula to Each Ingredient

The general formula is:

$$
\text{BP}_i \; (\%) = \frac{W_i}{W_{\text{flour}}} \times 100
$$

##### (a) Water (Hydration)
$$
\text{BP}_{\text{water}} = \frac{650}{1000} \times 100 = 65 \%
$$
**Interpretation:** This bread uses a *medium hydration* (typical for soft sandwich loaves).

##### (b) Salt
$$
\text{BP}_{\text{salt}} = \frac{20}{1000} \times 100 = 2.0 \%
$$
**Interpretation:** Falls within the **1.8 – 2.2 %** safe band — ideal for flavor and fermentation control.

##### (c) Sugar
$$
\text{BP}_{\text{sugar}} = \frac{30}{1000} \times 100 = 3.0 \%
$$
**Interpretation:** Very low sugar — consistent with a *lean dough* (French baguette style).

##### (d) Fat
$$
\text{BP}_{\text{fat}} = \frac{20}{1000} \times 100 = 2.0 \%
$$
**Interpretation:** Lean dough again; no significant shortening effect.

##### (e) Yeast
$$
\text{BP}_{\text{yeast}} = \frac{25}{1000} \times 100 = 2.5 \%
$$
**Interpretation:** Standard commercial yeast level for ~2-hour fermentation.

##### (f) Milk Powder
$$
\text{BP}_{\text{milk}} = \frac{20}{1000} \times 100 = 2.0 \%
$$
**Interpretation:** Adds minor browning via Maillard reaction and boosts protein content.

#### Step 3 — Compute Total Dough Weight and Total Baker's Percentage

$$
W_{\text{total}} = 1000 + 650 + 20 + 30 + 20 + 25 + 20 = 1765 \text{ g}
$$

$$
\text{Total BP} = 100 + 65 + 2 + 3 + 2 + 2.5 + 2 = 176.5 \%
$$

**Interpretation:** A total BP of ~176 % is typical of medium-hydration bread doughs. Cakes would range from 350 – 450 %, while biscuits stay around 145 – 170 %.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Estimating CO₂ Yield from Yeast Fermentation

**Given:** A bread dough contains **25 g of compressed yeast** with an average fermentation yield of **1.6 g CO₂ per gram of yeast per hour** for the first 2 hours.

**Required:** Estimate the total CO₂ produced in 2 hours.

$$
\text{CO}_2 \; (\text{g}) = W_{\text{yeast}} \times Y_{\text{CO}_2} \times t
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\text{CO}_2 = 25 \times 1.6 \times 2 = 80 \text{ g}
$$

Convert to moles (M(CO₂) = 44 g/mol):

$$
n_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{80}{44} \approx 1.82 \text{ mol}
$$

At standard conditions (STP, 22.4 L/mol), the volume of CO₂ produced is:

$$
V_{\text{CO}_2} = 1.82 \times 22.4 \approx 40.8 \text{ L}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This 40.8 L of CO₂ is what inflates a 1.765 kg dough into a baked loaf of ~1.5 kg (after moisture loss) with a porous, aerated crumb.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Functional Role of Each Ingredient

| Ingredient | Primary Function | Secondary Function | If Omitted / Deficient | KTU Cognitive Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flour | Structure (gluten) | Bulk, nutrition | Dense, flat, inedible | L1 – Remember |
| Sugar | Sweetness | Browning, fermentation, moisture | Pale, tough, dry product | L2 – Understand |
| Fat | Tenderness | Aeration, lubrication | Hard, dry, rubbery texture | L2 – Understand |
| Eggs | Binding & emulsification | Aeration (whites), structure | Crumbly, dense, no lift | L3 – Apply |
| Leavening | Gas production | Volume, lightness | Heavy, pancake-like | L3 – Apply |
| Liquids | Hydration | Solvent, steam | No dough formation | L2 – Understand |
| Salt | Flavor & control | Gluten tightening | Over-fermented, bland | L4 – Analyze |
| Flavorings | Sensory appeal | Variety, branding | Aesthetically unappealing | L1 – Remember |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Strategy:** When a 14-mark question asks "Discuss the functions of major baking ingredients," structure your answer using this analytical matrix — it demonstrates *higher-order thinking* and satisfies the **L4 – Analyze** outcome level under the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ingredient Interaction Map

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the eight major ingredient categories interact functionally inside a baking system. It maps inputs (ingredients) to intermediate transformations (processes) and outputs (final product attributes).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Flour Gluten Matrix] --> S1[Structure Formation]
    A2[Sugar] --> S2[Sweetness Browning Fermentation]
    A3[Fat and Oils] --> S3[Tenderness Aeration Lubrication]
    A4[Eggs] --> S4[Emulsification Aeration Binding]
    A5[Leavening Agents] --> S5[Gas CO2 Production]
    A6[Liquids Water Milk] --> S6[Hydration Activation]
    A7[Salt] --> S7[Flavor Regulation Yeast Control]
    A8[Flavorings and Additives] --> S8[Sensory Identity]

    S1 --> P1[Final Crumb Texture]
    S2 --> P2[Final Color and Sweetness]
    S3 --> P3[Final Mouthfeel]
    S4 --> P4[Final Emulsion Stability]
    S5 --> P5[Final Volume and Aeration]
    S6 --> P6[Final Dough Consistency]
    S7 --> P7[Final Fermentation Quality]
    S8 --> P8[Final Flavor Profile]

    P1 --> OUT[Finished Bakery Product Bread Cake Biscuit Pastry]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    P5 --> OUT
    P6 --> OUT
    P7 --> OUT
    P8 --> OUT
```

**How to Read This Diagram:**
- **Left column (A1–A8)** → The eight major ingredient inputs.
- **Middle column (S1–S8)** → The functional transformations each ingredient enables.
- **Bottom-right column (P1–P8)** → The corresponding product attributes that emerge.
- **Final node (OUT)** → The finished baked product.

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Single-Acting vs Double-Acting Baking Powder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Baking powder needed?} --> Q2{Will batter be baked immediately after mixing?}
    Q2 -- YES --> D1[Use Double Acting Baking Powder]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Will batter be refrigerated before baking?}
    Q3 -- YES --> D2[Use Single Acting Baking Powder]
    Q3 -- NO --> D1
    Q1 -- NO --> D4[Use Biological Leavening Yeast]
```

This diagram summarizes the **leavening selection logic** that KTU examiners frequently test in Part A (3-mark) questions.

### 4.3 Schematic — Hydration Pathway in a Wheat Flour Dough

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flour Particles] --> B[Water Added]
    B --> C[Hydration of Gluten Proteins]
    C --> D[Gluten Network Formation]
    D --> E[Mechanical Mixing Energy]
    E --> F[Aligned Viscoelastic Dough]
    F --> G[Gas Retention from CO2]
    G --> H[Aerated Baked Crumb]
```

**Reading Guide:** This schematic captures the *cause-effect chain* of hydration — from dry powder → hydrated proteins → aligned network → finished crumb. It is the single most important conceptual diagram for Module 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: List the major ingredients used in baking and state the primary function of any three.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarded as 1 + 1 + 1):**
The major baking ingredients are: (i) **Flour** — provides structure via gluten formation, (ii) **Sugar** — provides sweetness and browning, (iii) **Fat** — provides tenderness and aeration, (iv) **Eggs** — provide binding and emulsification, (v) **Leavening agents** — produce gas to aerate the product, (vi) **Liquids** — hydrate the ingredients, (vii) **Salt** — enhances flavor and regulates fermentation, (viii) **Flavorings** — add sensory appeal.

*(For full 3 marks, students must list at least 5 ingredients with one-line functions of any three.)*

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between single-acting and double-acting baking powder.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Single-Acting | Double-Acting |
|---|---|---|
| Reaction Trigger | Reacts as soon as liquid is added (room temp) | Reacts primarily at oven temperature |
| Acid Type Used | Fast-acting acid (e.g., tartaric acid) | Slow-acting acid (e.g., sodium aluminum sulfate) |
| Application | Refrigerated batters, biscuits | Cakes baked immediately after mixing |

*(Full 3 marks = at least 3 distinguishing points with correct technical terms.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

**Q: Discuss in detail the major baking ingredients and their functions. Explain the role of (a) Flour and Gluten Formation (7 marks), and (b) Leavening Agents — Biological vs Chemical (7 marks).**

##### (a) Role of Flour and Gluten Formation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Flour, derived primarily from wheat, is the **structural backbone** of bakery products. Its functional components are:

1. **Starch (~70 %)** — On heating in the presence of water, starch granules gelatinize at 60 – 80 °C, setting the crumb structure permanently.

2. **Gluten Proteins (~10–12 %)** — Composed of **gliadin** (provides extensibility/viscosity) and **glutenin** (provides elasticity/strength). When hydrated and mechanically mixed, these proteins crosslink via disulfide bonds to form a continuous **gluten network**.

3. **Enzymes** — Amylases convert damaged starch to fermentable sugars; proteases moderate gluten strength.

**Gluten Formation Stages:**
- *Hydration* — Flour proteins absorb water (~30 % of their weight).
- *Mixing/Development* — Mechanical energy aligns protein strands into sheets.
- *Gas Retention* — The network traps CO₂ from fermentation.
- *Heat Setting* — At ~70 °C, proteins coagulate and starch gelatinizes — the structure is permanently set.

**Flour Quality Parameters:**
- **Gluten content** (measured by wash test or gluten index).
- **Flour strength** (W-value via alveograph).
- **Extensibility and resistance** (via extensograph).
- Substitutes (rye, rice, oats) lack strong gluten and require blending or binders.

*(For 7 marks: cover all four stages of gluten formation, the role of starch, and at least two flour-quality parameters.)*

##### (b) Leavening Agents — Biological vs Chemical (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Biological Leavening — Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*):**
- A living microorganism that ferments sugars into **CO₂** and **ethanol**.
- Reaction: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$
- Requires controlled conditions: 26 – 32 °C, pH 4 – 6, moisture, food.
- Used in breads, sweet buns, doughnuts.
- Gives characteristic fermented flavor (ethanol + secondary metabolites).

**Chemical Leavening:**

1. **Baking Soda (NaHCO₃):**
   - Needs an acid to release CO₂.
   - $\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$
   - Used in recipes with naturally acidic ingredients (buttermilk, molasses).

2. **Baking Powder:**
   - A pre-mixed combination of NaHCO₃ + acid salt + buffer (cornstarch).
   - **Single-acting** — reacts at room temperature.
   - **Double-acting** — two-stage reaction; first stage in mixing, second in oven. Preferred in commercial cake production.

3. **Other chemical leaveners**: Ammonium bicarbonate (used in low-moisture crackers).

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Biological (Yeast) | Chemical (Baking Powder) |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Slow (1–3 h) | Fast (minutes) |
| Flavor | Fermented aroma | Neutral |
| Products | Bread, buns | Cakes, biscuits, cookies |

*(For 7 marks: cover yeast biochemistry, baking soda chemistry, baking powder composition, single vs double acting, and at least one comparison table.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**Q: Explain the functions of the following in baking: (a) Sugar and Fats (7 marks), and (b) Eggs and Salt (7 marks).**

##### (a) Functions of Sugar and Fats (7 Marks)

**Sugar** performs **five major functions**:

1. **Sweetness** — primary sensory attribute, governed by sugar type and concentration.
2. **Tenderization** — competes with gluten for water, slowing hydration and weakening gluten network → softer texture.
3. **Moisture retention** — hygroscopic; binds water via hydrogen bonds and delays staling (especially in cakes).
4. **Browning** — participates in **Maillard reaction** (with proteins) and **caramelization** (alone) at 140 – 170 °C.
5. **Fermentation substrate** — yeast metabolizes sucrose → glucose + fructose → CO₂ + ethanol.

Different sugar forms (dextrose, fructose, invert sugar) influence sweetness intensity, hygroscopicity, and crystallization behavior.

**Fats** perform **four major functions**:

1. **Shortening / Tenderizing** — fat films coat flour particles and physically block gluten hydration → short, tender, crumbly texture (biscuit effect).
2. **Aeration** — during creaming, fat crystals entrap air cells that expand during baking, providing lift.
3. **Flavor and Mouthfeel** — fat carries fat-soluble flavor compounds and gives the characteristic richness.
4. **Anti-staling** — forms complexes with amylose, slowing starch retrogradation and extending shelf-life.

*(For 7 marks: enumerate and briefly explain at least 4 sugar functions and 3 fat functions, with one reaction/example each.)*

##### (b) Functions of Eggs and Salt (7 Marks)

**Eggs** are the **most multifunctional** ingredient in baking:

1. **Structure** — Egg proteins (ovalbumin, ovomucin, conalbumin) coagulate at 62 – 65 °C, setting the crumb.
2. **Emulsification** — Lecithin in the yolk stabilizes fat-in-water emulsions (key in butter cakes and mayonnaise-style batters).
3. **Aeration / Foaming** — Egg whites form a stable foam when whipped due to protein denaturation at air-water interfaces (meringues, sponge cakes).
4. **Color** — Yolk contributes carotenoid pigments and promotes Maillard browning via lysine residues.
5. **Flavor & Moisture** — Adds richness and retains water.

**Salt** (used at **1.8 – 2.2 %** of flour weight):

1. **Flavor enhancement** — suppresses bitterness and amplifies sweet/savory notes.
2. **Gluten tightening** — strengthens gluten network via ionic interaction, improving dough elasticity.
3. **Fermentation control** — slows yeast activity, preventing over-rising and ensuring stable fermentation.
4. **Shelf-life** — slight antimicrobial effect.

**Consequence of omitting salt:** over-fermented dough, sticky texture, bland flavor, faster staling.

*(For 7 marks: cover at least 4 egg functions and all 4 salt functions with specific temperatures or percentages.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write "sugar makes the product sweet" only — this fetches **0 out of 7 marks** in the sugar question. Always list **at least four distinct functions**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing *single-acting* and *double-acting* baking powder. Single-acting reacts **at room temperature**; double-acting reacts **at oven temperature**. Wrong answer = lose 2 marks instantly.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing "flour provides structure" without explaining the **gluten network formation stages** (hydration → development → retention → heat setting). Examiners deduct marks for missing any one stage.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Ignoring **Baker's Percentage** in numerical problems. Always state that **flour = 100 %** baseline.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Not writing the **fermentation equation** when explaining yeast — $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$ is a guaranteed 1-mark reward.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print / Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ There are **eight** major ingredient categories in baking (Flour, Sugar, Fat, Eggs, Leavening, Liquids, Salt, Flavorings).
- ☐ **Flour** = structural backbone (gluten + starch).
- ☐ **Gluten formation stages**: Hydration → Development → Gas Retention → Heat Setting.
- ☐ **Sugar** has **5 functions**: sweetness, tenderization, moisture, browning, fermentation.
- ☐ **Fat** has **4 functions**: tenderizing, aeration, flavor, anti-staling.
- ☐ **Eggs** are the **most multifunctional** — structure, emulsification, aeration, color, flavor.
- ☐ **Leavening agents**: Biological (yeast) vs Chemical (baking soda + acid = baking powder).
- ☐ **Single-acting baking powder** reacts at room temperature; **double-acting** reacts in the oven.
- ☐ **Yeast fermentation equation**: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$.
- ☐ **Baker's Percentage** always uses **flour = 100 %** as reference.
- ☐ **Salt**: 1.8 – 2.2 % of flour weight; controls flavor + fermentation.
- ☐ **Hydration ratio**: Bread = 60 – 75 %; Cake = 100 – 130 % of flour weight.
- ☐ **Omission of any major ingredient** leads to a specific defect — be ready to state it.
- ☐ **Maillard reaction** = sugar + protein browning; **Caramelization** = sugar-only browning.
- ☐ Use the **8-block Mermaid architecture diagram** for quick visual revision of ingredient functions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Baking Ingredients in Improving the Quality of Bread; Characteristics of Flour Used for Making Bread
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT412 - BAKERY AND CONFECTIONERY PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Role of Baking Ingredients in Improving the Quality of Bread; Characteristics of Flour Used for Making Bread

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Baking Ingredients
In bread technology, **baking ingredients** are the chemically and biologically active components that, when combined in precise proportions, undergo controlled physical, chemical, biochemical, and rheological transformations to produce a leavened, aerated, and palatable final product known as bread. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, these ingredients are classified into **primary structural agents** (flour, water), **leavening agents** (yeast, chemical leaveners), **flavor and conditioning agents** (salt, sugar), and **dough modifiers** (fats, milk solids, enzymes, oxidizing/reducing agents).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The official formulation of standard pan bread is based on a **100% flour basis** where every other ingredient is expressed as a **percentage of flour weight** (also called *Baker's Percentage*). This is the universal language of the bakery industry.

### 1.2 Definition of Bread-Making Flour Characteristics
**Bread flour** is a hard wheat (*Triticum aestivum*) flour characterized by a high protein content (**10–13%**), strong gluten-forming capability, and superior gas-retention capacity during fermentation and oven spring. Its quality is quantified by rheological parameters such as the **Alveograph W value (10⁻⁴ J)**, the **P/L ratio**, the **Farinograph stability (minutes)**, and the **Falling Number (seconds)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** *"Bread is essentially a protein foam stabilized by a starch matrix."* The gluten network holds the gas produced by yeast; the starch granules gelatinize during baking to set the crumb structure permanently.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Bread as a Skyscraper"
Think of making bread like constructing a **multi-story building**:
- **Flour (the Steel Frame):** Provides the gluten skeleton that holds everything together.
- **Water (the Construction Crew):** Activates gluten, hydrates starch, and dissolves other ingredients.
- **Yeast (the Elevator System):** Produces CO₂ "passengers" that travel up and inflate each floor (crumb cell).
- **Salt (the Site Engineer):** Controls the speed, tightens the structure, and adds flavor.
- **Sugar (the Workers' Lunch):** Feeds the yeast and gives the crust its golden tan.
- **Fat (the Interior Polish):** Lubricates the dough, making it soft and extending shelf-life.
- **Milk powder (the Premium Finish):** Enhances browning, nutrition, and softness.

Without any one of these, the building collapses, rises unevenly, or looks dull — the same way a poorly formulated dough gives a dense, flat, or pale loaf.

---

### 1.4 Primary Functions at a Glance

| Ingredient | Principal Function | Sensory/Quality Contribution |
|------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Flour** | Structure & bulk | Crumb texture, volume |
| **Water** | Hydration | Dough consistency |
| **Yeast** | Leavening (CO₂) | Porosity, flavor |
| **Salt** | Flavor & control | Crust color, tightness |
| **Sugar** | Yeast food, sweetness | Crust browning (Maillard) |
| **Fat** | Tenderizer | Softness, shelf-life |
| **Milk solids** | Nutrition, browning | Crust color, flavor |
| **Enzymes** | Dough relaxation | Machinability |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Role of Each Baking Ingredient — Systematic Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Flour (Structural Foundation)
- Provides **gluten proteins** — *glutenin* (elasticity) and *gliadin* (extensibility).
- Supplies **starch granules** (≈ 70% of flour weight) which gelatinize at **60–75 °C**, locking the loaf structure.
- Contributes **enzymes** (amylase, protease, lipase) that catalyze starch and protein breakdown.
- Carries natural **lipids** which complex with amylose to retard staling.

#### 2.1.2 Water (Universal Solvent)
- Hydrates gluten proteins to form a **three-dimensional viscoelastic network**.
- Dissolves salt, sugar, and milk solids for even distribution.
- Controls **dough temperature** (high water % → cooler dough due to high specific heat of water = **4.18 kJ/kg·K**).
- Optimum absorption for bread flour: **60–65%** of flour weight.

#### 2.1.3 Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*)
- Biological leavening agent — **ferments** sugars via the Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP) pathway:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2\uparrow + 2\,ATP$$

- Produces flavor compounds (ethanol, organic acids, esters) and **2% CO₂ by dough volume**.
- Works optimally at **27–32 °C**, pH **4.0–6.0**.

#### 2.1.4 Salt (NaCl)
- Tightens gluten by enhancing ionic cross-linking of gluten proteins.
- Regulates **osmotic pressure** on yeast cells, slowing fermentation (so the dough does not over-proof).
- Strengthens **flavor profile** — a bread without salt tastes flat and yeasty.
- Typical usage: **1.8–2.2%** of flour weight.

#### 2.1.5 Sugar (Sucrose / Dextrose)
- Immediate food for yeast, accelerating the lag phase.
- Participates in **Maillard browning** with amino acids at the crust (≥ 140 °C).
- Adds sweetness, retains moisture, and tenderizes crumb.
- Typical usage: **2–8%** for white pan bread.

#### 2.1.6 Fat (Shortening / Butter)
- Disrupts continuous gluten strands → **tenderizing effect**.
- Lubricates dough for better machinability and gas-cell expansion.
- Increases loaf volume and extends shelf-life by **complexing with amylose** to retard retrogradation.
- Typical usage: **2–4%**.

#### 2.1.7 Milk Solids (Skim Milk Powder — SMP)
- Increases **water absorption** of flour due to casein proteins.
- Enhances Maillard browning (lactose + lysine).
- Adds nutritional value (calcium, protein).

#### 2.1.8 Dough Improvers
- **Oxidizers** (ascorbic acid, KBrO₃, azodicarbonamide) — strengthen gluten by forming disulfide cross-links.
- **Emulsifiers** (SSL, DATEM, mono/diglycerides) — strengthen gas-cell membranes, increase volume and softness.
- **Enzymes** (α-amylase, xylanase, lipase) — modify starch and pentosans.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Industry:** Bakers use the **"Five Functions of Flour"** mnemonic — **HAPSS**: **H**ydration, **A**dhesion, **P**lasticity, **S**haping, **S**tructural support. Any deviation in flour quality directly disrupts all five.

---

### 2.2 Characteristics of Flour for Bread-Making

#### 2.2.1 Physical Characteristics
| Parameter | Specification for Bread Flour |
|-----------|------------------------------|
| **Color** | White to creamy white (Minolta L\* ≥ 90) |
| **Granulation** | Fine, uniform (250–300 µm) |
| **Moisture** | ≤ 14.0% |
| **Ash content** | 0.50–0.65% (mineral residue) |
| **pH** | 6.0–6.8 |

#### 2.2.2 Chemical Characteristics
| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Protein (N × 5.7)** | **10–13%** |
| **Wet Gluten** | 28–34% |
| **Dry Gluten** | 10–12% |
| **Gluten Index** | ≥ 80 (strong gluten) |
| **Falling Number** | 250–350 s (adequate α-amylase) |
| **Damaged Starch** | 5–8% (controlled) |

#### 2.2.3 Rheological Characteristics (Alveograph & Farinograph)
- **W value (deformation energy):** 200–350 × 10⁻⁴ J for strong bread flour.
- **P value (tenacity):** 60–100 mm.
- **L value (extensibility):** 80–150 mm.
- **P/L ratio:** 0.5–1.2 (balanced to slightly tenacious).
- **Farinograph Water Absorption (FWA):** 58–64%.
- **Farinograph Stability:** 8–15 minutes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know:** The W value, also called the *Baking Strength Index*, is the single most quoted indicator in commercial flour trading. A W < 180 → weak flour (cakes, biscuits); W > 300 → strong flour (baguettes, hard rolls).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the most frequently tested numerical relations in PEFTT412.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{Baker's \%} = \dfrac{W_{ingredient}}{W_{flour}} \times 100$ | Expresses any ingredient as a percentage of flour weight | % |
| 2 | $\text{Total Dough Yield (TDY)} = \dfrac{W_{flour} + W_{other}}{W_{flour}} \times 100$ | Used to scale recipes | % |
| 3 | $\text{Hydration Ratio} = \dfrac{W_{water}}{W_{flour}} \times 100$ | Dough softness indicator | % |
| 4 | $W_{CO_2} = \dfrac{2 \times 44}{180} \times W_{sugar} = 0.489 \times W_{sugar}$ | Theoretical CO₂ mass from sugar fermentation | grams |
| 5 | $V_{CO_2} = \dfrac{W_{CO_2}}{\rho_{CO_2}}$ where $\rho_{CO_2} = 1.98 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at STP | Convert CO₂ mass to volume | mL |
| 6 | $\text{Gluten Index} = \dfrac{W_{wet\,gluten\,retained}}{W_{total\,wet\,gluten}} \times 100$ | Strength of gluten network | % |
| 7 | $\text{Loaf Specific Volume} = \dfrac{V_{loaf}}{W_{loaf}}$ | Quality benchmark for bread | mL/g |
| 8 | $\text{Baking Loss} = \dfrac{W_{dough} - W_{bread}}{W_{dough}} \times 100$ | Moisture + volatile loss in oven | % |
| 9 | $\text{W value relation: } W = 1.32 \times \dfrac{V_{curve}}{L}$ | Empirical conversion to alveograph energy | 10⁻⁴ J |
| 10 | $\text{Falling Number} = f(\alpha\text{-amylase activity})$ | Inverse relation with enzymatic activity | seconds |

**Note on Engineering Utility:** The Baker's Percentage system allows a baker to scale any recipe from **1 kg to 1000 kg** without redesigning formulas — the same bread quality is reproduced every time, irrespective of the mixer size. The Falling Number predicts the *sticky-crumb problem*: very low FN (< 200 s) means excess α-amylase damaged the starch, producing a gummy interior.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical CO₂ Production from Sugar
*This calculation is a KTU-favorite 7-mark derivation question.*

**Problem:** A bread formula contains **5 kg sucrose** as part of the dough. Calculate the **theoretical mass** and **volume (at STP)** of CO₂ liberated by yeast fermentation.

**Given:** $W_{sugar} = 5 \text{ kg} = 5000 \text{ g}$

**Step 1 — Write the balanced biochemical equation:**

$$C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,C_6H_{12}O_6$$

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

**Step 2 — Combine the equations (sucrose → 4 CO₂):**

$$C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; 4\,C_2H_5OH + 4\,CO_2$$

**Step 3 — Molecular weight calculation:**

- $M_{sucrose} = 12(12) + 22(1) + 11(16) = 144 + 22 + 176 = 342 \text{ g/mol}$
- $M_{CO_2} = 12 + 2(16) = 44 \text{ g/mol}$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric mass ratio:**

$$\dfrac{4 \times 44 \text{ g CO}_2}{342 \text{ g sucrose}} = \dfrac{176}{342} = 0.5146 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{g sucrose}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the total CO₂ mass:**

$$W_{CO_2} = 0.5146 \times 5000 \text{ g} = 2573 \text{ g} \approx 2.573 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to volume at STP** (using $\rho_{CO_2} = 1.98 \text{ kg/m}^3$):

$$V_{CO_2} = \dfrac{2.573 \text{ kg}}{1.98 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 1.30 \text{ m}^3 = 1300 \text{ L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The actual CO₂ produced is **~85% of theoretical** because yeast uses ~15% of the sugar for cell growth and respiration. Therefore, the *practical* CO₂ is about **1.10 m³** from 5 kg sucrose.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Baker's Percentage Reverse Calculation
*Common 3-mark problem.*

**Problem:** A bread formula is given below. Express the **Baker's Percentage** of each ingredient and compute the **Total Dough Yield (TDY)**.

| Ingredient | Weight (g) |
|------------|-----------|
| Bread flour | 1000 |
| Water | 620 |
| Yeast (compressed) | 25 |
| Salt | 20 |
| Sugar | 50 |
| Fat | 30 |
| Milk powder | 20 |

**Step 1 — Take flour as 100%:**

$$\text{Flour \%} = \dfrac{1000}{1000} \times 100 = 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Apply to other ingredients:**

$$\text{Water \%} = \dfrac{620}{1000} \times 100 = 62\%$$

$$\text{Yeast \%} = \dfrac{25}{1000} \times 100 = 2.5\%$$

$$\text{Salt \%} = \dfrac{20}{1000} \times 100 = 2.0\%$$

$$\text{Sugar \%} = \dfrac{50}{1000} \times 100 = 5.0\%$$

$$\text{Fat \%} = \dfrac{30}{1000} \times 100 = 3.0\%$$

$$\text{Milk \%} = \dfrac{20}{1000} \times 100 = 2.0\%$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Total Dough Yield:**

$$TDY = 100 + 62 + 2.5 + 2.0 + 5.0 + 3.0 + 2.0 = 176.5\%$$

**Step 4 — Verify by weight:**

$$W_{dough} = 1765 \text{ g} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Flour Quality vs. End-Use

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **high-yield KTU table** frequently asked as a 14-mark comparison question.

| Parameter | Weak Flour (Cake/Biscuit) | Medium Flour (Family Bread) | Strong Flour (Pan Bread) |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Protein (%) | 7–9 | 9–11 | **11–13** |
| Wet Gluten (%) | 18–22 | 22–28 | **28–34** |
| W value (10⁻⁴ J) | 80–180 | 180–250 | **250–350** |
| P/L ratio | 0.3–0.6 | 0.6–0.9 | **0.9–1.5** |
| Stability (min) | 2–5 | 5–10 | **10–15** |
| Water Absorption (%) | 50–55 | 55–60 | **60–65** |
| Ideal Application | Cookies, cakes | Soft rolls, buns | Pan bread, baguettes |
| Crumb Texture | Soft, tender | Medium, fine | Chewy, open |

---

### 3.4 Engineering Limitation of Strong vs. Weak Flour
A common KTU trick question asks: *"Why is strong flour not used for cakes?"* The mathematical reason:

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{F_{gluten}}{A_{cell\,wall}}$$

A stronger gluten produces a **higher maximum shear stress** ($\tau_{max}$), which prevents the cake batter (a liquid foam) from expanding uniformly. The cake **rises unevenly**, then collapses, giving a *tunnel-pull* defect. Thus the dough matrix must match the product's rheological need.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
