**Given:** Batch of **2.0 kg sucrose** + **0.4 kg glucose syrup (80 % solids)** + **30 g NaHCO₃** + **500 mL water**.

**Find:** (a) Theoretical CO₂ mass, (b) Approximate foam expansion ratio.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mass of CO₂:**

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.262 \times 30 = 7.86\ \text{g}
$$

**Part (b) — Foam volume ratio:**
At 150 °C, the CO₂ volume is:

$$
V_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.207 \times 30 = 6.21\ \text{L} = 6210\ \text{cm}^3
$$

The unfoamed syrup volume is approximately:

$$
V_{\text{syrup}} = \dfrac{2400\ \text{g}}{1.4\ \text{g/cm}^3} \approx 1714\ \text{cm}^3
$$

(using a typical molten-sugar density of 1.4 g/cm³)

Therefore:

$$
\text{Expansion Ratio} = \dfrac{V_{\text{syrup}} + V_{\text{CO}_2}}{V_{\text{syrup}}} = \dfrac{1714 + 6210}{1714} \approx 4.6
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 2.4 kg batch can theoretically expand to **≈ 4.6× its original volume** at the moment of pour — this is why the moulds must be oversized and the pouring must be done *before* the foam collapses.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Honeycomb vs Other Aerated Confections

| Parameter | **Honeycomb** | Marshmallow | Meringue | Aerated Chocolate |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|----------|-------------------|
| **Aeration agent** | NaHCO₃ (chemical) | Gelatin + whipped air | Egg-white foam | CO₂ under pressure |
| **Stabilizer** | Sugar glass viscosity | Gelatin protein network | Egg albumin + sugar | Cocoa butter crystal network |
| **Process temp** | 150 °C (hard crack) | ~110 °C | ~100 °C (baked) | 32–35 °C (tempered) |
| **Moisture (final)** | < 2 % | 15–20 % | 1–3 % (dried) | < 1 % |
| **Texture** | Brittle, glassy, airy | Soft, elastic, chewy | Crisp, dry, crisp-melt | Crisp, melts, airy |
| **pH** | 6.5–7.5 (alkaline) | 4.5–5.0 (acidic) | 5.0–6.0 (near neutral) | 5.5–6.5 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Weigh Ingredients: Sugar, Glucose, Water, NaHCO3] --> B[Dissolve Sugar and Glucose in Water]
    B --> C[Heat Syrup Gently to ~110 deg C]
    C --> D[Boil to Hard-Crack Stage at 150 deg C]
    D --> E{Optional Acid Addition: Vinegar or Cream of Tartar}
    E --> F[Remove from Heat Source]
    F --> G[Sift NaHCO3 Powder Evenly]
    G --> H[Vigorous Stirring for 5 to 10 Seconds ONLY]
    H --> I[Volumetric Expansion: CO2 Gas Released]
    I --> J[Pour Immediately into Pre-Warmed Greased Mould]
    J --> K[Cool and Set: 30 to 60 Minutes]
    K --> L[Demould and Cut with Serrated Knife]
    L --> M[Pack in Airtight Low Humidity Container]
    M --> N[Final Product: Brittle Golden Honeycomb]
```

## 4.2 Microstructural Block Architecture of Honeycomb

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Continuous Sugar Glass Phase - approx 60 to 70 percent volume]
        SG1[Amorphous Sucrose Matrix]
        SG2[Glucose and Dextrin Chain Network]
        SG3[Trace Na2CO3 Microcrystals]
    end
    subgraph B[Discontinuous Gas Phase - approx 30 to 40 percent volume]
        G1[Large Cells approx 2 to 5 mm]
        G2[Medium Cells approx 0.5 to 2 mm]
        G3[Small Cells less than 0.5 mm]
    end
    A --- B
    B --> C[Bubble Wall Thickness approx 50 to 200 microns]
    C --> D[Closed and Open Cell Mixed Architecture]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Mechanism of Foam Stabilization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Molten Sugar at 150 deg C] --> P2[Very High Viscosity approx 100 to 1000 Pa s]
    P2 --> P3[NaHCO3 Decomposes to CO2]
    P3 --> P4[CO2 Nucleates as Bubbles]
    P4 --> P5[High Viscosity Prevents Bubble Coalescence and Drainage]
    P5 --> P6[Bubbles Lock In Place on Cooling]
    P6 --> P7[Rigid Aerated Glass Structure]
    P7 --> P8[Final Honeycomb Texture]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define honeycomb confection. Mention the role of sodium bicarbonate in its preparation.**

**Model Answer:**
Honeycomb is an aerated sugar confection made by adding sodium bicarbonate to a sugar syrup cooked to the hard-crack stage (150 °C). The bicarbonate decomposes thermally to release **CO₂ gas**, which gets trapped in the viscous molten sugar and produces a porous, brittle, golden product on cooling.

*(Word count: 60. 3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for temperature, 1 for CO₂ mechanism.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**List any four ingredients used in honeycomb and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Ingredient | Function |
|------------|----------|
| Sucrose | Body former / glass former |
| Glucose syrup | Prevents crystallization; humectant |
| Sodium bicarbonate | Aerating agent (releases CO₂) |
| Water | Solvent; evaporates during boiling |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]* (Internal Choice)

**(a)** Explain the manufacturing process of honeycomb candy with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of **each ingredient** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **chemical reactions** occurring during the preparation of honeycomb. Why is **1.0–1.5 %** the recommended dosage of sodium bicarbonate? What defects arise from excess addition? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Manufacturing Process (7 marks):**

1. **Weighing & Mixing:** Sucrose (70–80 %), glucose syrup (10–20 %), and water (15–25 % of batch) are weighed accurately. Accurate scaling ensures predictable final temperature and consistent quality.
2. **Dissolution:** All ingredients are heated gently in a stainless-steel pan with continuous stirring until complete dissolution. No visible sugar crystals must remain.
3. **Boiling to Hard-Crack Stage:** The syrup is boiled to **150 °C** (hard-crack stage) using a candy thermometer. At this temperature, the syrup contains < 2 % moisture and on cooling will form a hard, glassy solid.
4. **Acid Addition (Optional):** A few drops of vinegar or a pinch of cream of tartar are added to acidify the medium, ensuring a more uniform and finer cell structure.
5. **Pan Removal & Bicarbonate Addition:** The pan is removed from heat. Sodium bicarbonate (1.0–1.5 % of sugar mass) is sifted evenly over the surface.
6. **Mixing:** Stirred *vigorously* for **5–10 seconds only**. The mixture foams up to 4–5 times its original volume.
7. **Pouring:** The foam is immediately poured into pre-warmed, lightly greased moulds. Pre-warming prevents premature setting at the edges.
8. **Cooling & Setting:** Allowed to cool undisturbed for 30–60 minutes until fully set into a rigid brittle block.
9. **Demoulding & Cutting:** Demoulded and cut with a serrated knife into desired sizes.
10. **Packaging:** Stored in airtight containers at < 50 % RH to prevent moisture pickup and surface stickiness.

**Ingredient Roles:**

- **Sucrose** — Primary structural sugar; forms the glassy amorphous matrix on cooling.
- **Glucose syrup** — Inhibits sucrose crystallization; provides smoothness, gloss, and prevents graining.
- **Water** — Acts as solvent; evaporates during boiling to concentrate the syrup.
- **Sodium bicarbonate** — Releases CO₂ on thermal decomposition; the sole aerating agent.
- **Vinegar / Cream of Tartar** — Acidifies to uniformize bubble size and improve cell distribution.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Chemical Reactions (7 marks):**

The principal reaction is the **thermal decomposition of sodium bicarbonate**:

$$
2\,\text{NaHCO}_3 \xrightarrow{150^\circ\text{C}} \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}\uparrow + \text{CO}_2\uparrow
$$

A secondary reaction occurs when an acid (vinegar = acetic acid; cream of tartar = tartaric acid) is present. Acetic acid, for example, accelerates and uniformizes the reaction:

$$
\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow
$$

The CO₂ released is trapped in the molten sugar syrup (viscosity ≈ 100–1000 Pa·s) and forms the porous honeycomb architecture. Sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) remains as a fine, slightly hygroscopic residue, while sodium acetate (when vinegar is used) is more bland and less hygroscopic, hence the optional acid addition also improves taste.

**Why 1.0–1.5 % NaHCO₃?**

This dosage range is calculated to:
- Provide **sufficient CO₂** to achieve a 3–5× volumetric expansion.
- Leave **minimal residual Na₂CO₃**, keeping the alkaline taste within acceptable sensory limits.
- Avoid **excess unreacted bicarbonate** that would degrade flavour and texture.

**Defects from Excess NaHCO₃:**

1. **Soapy / Alkaline Taste** — High Na₂CO₃ residue gives an unpleasant soapy aftertaste.
2. **Surface Whitening (Bloom)** — Na₂CO₃ crystallizes on the surface, forming a white powdery bloom.
3. **Hygroscopicity / Stickiness** — Na₂CO₃ is hygroscopic; it absorbs moisture from the air, making the candy sticky and reducing shelf-life.
4. **Coarse Cell Structure** — Excess gas generation ruptures bubble walls, producing uneven, collapsed pores.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> **DO NOT** write "baking soda helps the candy rise" — this is *bread* language. Use precise confectionery terminology: **"NaHCO₃ decomposes to release CO₂ which aerates the molten sugar syrup by gas entrapment."** Marks are lost for vague wording. Also, **always state the temperature (150 °C)** in your answer — the examiner's key explicitly tests this.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the **stages of sugar boiling** in confectionery. Why must honeycomb be cooked to the **hard-crack stage**? What happens if it is under-cooked or over-cooked? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast honeycomb with **marshmallow** and **meringue** under the headings: aeration mechanism, stabilizer, moisture content, and texture. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Stages of Sugar Boiling (7 marks):**

| Stage | Temperature (°C) | Characteristics / Test |
|-------|------------------|------------------------|
| Thread | 110–112 | Syrup drips in fine threads |
| Soft Ball | 112–116 | Forms a soft ball in cold water |
| Firm Ball | 118–120 | Forms a firm but pliable ball |
| Hard Ball | 121–130 | Forms a hard, non-pliable ball |
| Soft Crack | 132–143 | Cracks but is still sticky |
| **Hard Crack** | **146–154** | **Cracks with a clean, hard break** |
| Caramel | 160+ | Sugar darkens; turns to caramel |

**Why hard-crack stage for honeycomb?**
At 150 °C, the syrup has a moisture content of < 2 %. On cooling, the sugar forms a **rigid, glassy amorphous solid** capable of permanently holding the CO₂ cells in their expanded form. Any higher moisture would cause the cell walls to be soft, sticky, and collapsible.

**Defects of Under-cooking (T < 145 °C):**
- Excess moisture remains → soft, sticky candy → bubbles collapse → **dense, non-aerated product**.
- Poor shelf-life due to high water activity.
- The product may bend rather than crack.

**Defects of Over-cooking (T > 160 °C):**
- Sugar **inverts excessively** and begins to **caramelize and darken**.
- The mass becomes too viscous to foam adequately — poor aeration.
- **Bitter, burnt flavour** develops.
- Color becomes dark brown instead of golden.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Honeycomb | Marshmallow | Meringue |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Aeration mechanism** | Chemical (NaHCO₃ → CO₂) | Mechanical whipping of air into gelatin-syrup base | Mechanical whipping of air into egg-white foam |
| **Stabilizer** | Viscosity of molten sugar glass | Gelatin protein network (sets on cooling) | Egg albumin (ovalbumin) + sugar |
| **Final moisture** | < 2 % | 15–20 % | 1–3 % (after drying) |
| **Texture** | Brittle, hard, glassy, airy | Soft, elastic, chewy, spongy | Crisp, dry, dissolves on tongue |
| **Cooking temperature** | 150 °C | ~110 °C | ~100 °C (baked) |

**Key Conceptual Differences:**
- Honeycomb is an **aerated glass** — its stability depends on the *amorphous solidification* of sugar.
- Marshmallow is an **aerated gel** — its stability depends on a *protein hydrogel network*.
- Meringue is an **aerated foam** — its stability depends on *denatured protein films* around air bubbles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> When answering comparison questions, students often write paragraphs without a table. **Always present a table** for comparison questions — the KTU valuation key explicitly awards 1 mark for structured presentation. Also, do NOT mix up "honeycomb" with "honey toffee" — honeycomb is *aerated*, honey toffee is *not aerated*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this conceptual error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Honeycomb**

- ✅ **Honeycomb = Sugar + Glucose + Water + NaHCO₃**, cooked to **150 °C (hard-crack)**, foamed by CO₂.
- ✅ **NaHCO₃ dosage:** **1.0–1.5 % of sugar weight** (NEVER more).
- ✅ **Reaction:** $2\,\text{NaHCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$
- ✅ **Function of glucose syrup:** Prevents crystallization, ensures amorphous glass.
- ✅ **Mixing time after bicarbonate addition:** **5–10 seconds** (over-mixing = foam collapse).
- ✅ **Final moisture:** < 2 %; **Final pH:** 6.5–7.5.
- ✅ **Expansion ratio:** ≈ 4–5× the original syrup volume.
- ✅ **Moulds must be pre-warmed and pre-greased** (prevents edge setting and sticking).
- ✅ **Storage:** Airtight containers, **< 50 % RH**.
- ✅ **Stabilizer of foam = molten sugar viscosity** (NOT protein, NOT starch — this distinguishes it from bread, marshmallow, meringue).
- ✅ **Defects of excess NaHCO₃:** soapy taste, surface bloom (Na₂CO₃ crystals), stickiness, coarse cells.
- ✅ **Defects of under-cooking:** sticky, dense, bubbles collapse.
- ✅ **Defects of over-cooking:** caramelization, bitter taste, dark colour, poor aeration.
- ✅ **Commercial examples:** Cadbury Crunchie®, Violet Crumble®, Golden Wonder Sponge Candy.
- ✅ **Honeycomb is an *aerated glass* — not a gel, not a foam, not a bread.** Memorize this distinction.
- ✅ **Exam keywords to use:** *aeration, hard-crack stage, amorphous sugar glass, CO₂ entrapment, gas volume fraction (φ), thermal decomposition, hygroscopicity, bloom.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Caramel and Fudge; Processing of Toffee; Quality and Standards, Regulations to be Followed, Packaging Requirements for Confectionery Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT412 - BAKERY AND CONFECTIONERY PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Caramel and Fudge; Processing of Toffee; Quality and Standards, Regulations to be Followed, Packaging Requirements for Confectionery Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Caramel — Formal Definition

**Caramel** is a confectionery product obtained by the controlled **non-enzymatic browning** (Maillard reaction followed by caramelization) of a mixture of sugars (sucrose, glucose syrup, invert sugar) with or without the addition of milk solids, fats, emulsifiers, and flavoring agents. The process involves heating sugars to temperatures between **160°C and 180°C** until the desired color, flavor, and viscosity are achieved, followed by controlled cooling, aeration (optional), and forming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Caramel is classified as a *thermoplastic confection* — a category that includes toffee, fudge, and butterscotch. All these products rely on the sugar cook — a critical unit operation that defines the final product texture.

### 1.2 Fudge — Formal Definition

**Fudge** is a soft, crystalline, granular-textured confection made by cooking a mixture of sugar, milk (or condensed milk), butter, and glucose syrup to a specific final moisture content (typically **8–12%**) and a relatively low cook temperature (**112°C–118°C**, soft-ball stage), followed by controlled crystallization during beating/cooling. The characteristic short, melt-in-the-mouth texture is a result of **deliberate seeding of sugar crystals** to prevent the formation of a hard, glassy, amorphous structure.

### 1.3 Toffee — Formal Definition

**Toffee** is a glossy, amorphous, hard or chewy confection produced by cooking a mixture of sugar, glucose syrup, fat (butter or vegetable fat), milk solids/condensed milk, salt, emulsifiers (e.g., lecithin), and flavorings to a high temperature (**140°C–154°C**, hard-crack stage). The cooked mass is cooled, shaped (cut, molded, or extruded), and wrapped. Toffee is **non-crystalline (amorphous)**, contrasting with the crystalline nature of fudge.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine three siblings in a sugar family:

- **Caramel** is the **"bare-bones" sibling** — just sugar cooked until it turns golden brown. Add milk and you get a softer, creamier version (milk caramel). Think of a crème brûlée topping.
- **Fudge** is the **"patient sibling"** — sugar is cooked only mildly (to soft-ball), and then it is **beaten while cooling** to deliberately grow tiny sugar crystals. The result feels like wet sand on your tongue — soft, granular, melts instantly. Think of a *chocolate truffle* but without the chocolate coating.
- **Toffee** is the **"tough sibling"** — sugar is cooked to a much higher temperature (hard-crack), producing a hard, glossy, glass-like snap. Think of a *jawbreaker hard candy* but with butter and milk solids giving a chewier, opaque character.

> [!NOTE]
> **The key visual difference:** Hold a piece of fudge up to a light — you will see a dull, slightly opaque surface (microcrystals). Hold a piece of toffee — the surface is glassy and translucent (amorphous sugar glass).

### 1.5 Quality and Standards — Overview

Quality in confectionery is governed by **physical, chemical, microbiological, and sensory** parameters. The most important metrics include:

- **Moisture content** (typically **8–12%** for fudge; **<3%** for hard toffee).
- **Water Activity (a_w)** — must be below **0.6** for shelf-stable confectionery to prevent microbial growth.
- **Sugar composition ratio** (Sucrose : Glucose Syrup : Invert Sugar).
- **Final cook temperature** (defines the sugar-stage and hence the texture).
- **Color (L*, a*, b*)** — measured by colorimeter.
- **Hardness / Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** — measured by penetrometer or texture analyzer.
- **Microbial limits** (Total Plate Count, Yeast & Mould, Coliforms) per **FSSAI/FDA** standards.

### 1.6 Regulations and Packaging

- **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** — governs all food products in India under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006**.
- **AGMARK** — voluntary certification for specific products.
- **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** — **IS 1166:1986** (Toffee), **IS 6287:1985** (Caramel), and similar standards.
- **Codex Alimentarius** — international food standard reference.
- **Packaging requirements** under **FSSAI Packaging and Labelling Regulations, 2011** (and subsequent amendments) and the **Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 2011**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chemistry of Caramelization

Caramelization is a **pyrolytic decomposition of sugars** that proceeds in three overlapping stages:

1. **Concentration Stage (100°C–120°C):** Water evaporation; syrup becomes increasingly viscous. No color change.
2. **Inversion & Dehydration (120°C–150°C):** Sucrose hydrolyses to glucose + fructose (inversion). Dehydration produces **HMF (5-hydroxymethylfurfural)** and other reactive intermediates.
3. **Condensation & Polymerization (150°C–180°C):** HMF and other intermediates condense with amino groups (if milk present — **Maillard reaction**), forming **melanoidins** — the brown polymers responsible for color and flavor.

> [!NOTE]
> The Maillard reaction is responsible for the rich, **butterscotch / nutty** flavor of milk caramel. Pure sucrose caramelization produces a sharper, more **bitter** flavor.

### 2.2 Sugar Cook Stages — The Heart of Confectionery

The temperature to which the sugar mass is cooked directly defines the final texture. This is the **single most important concept** in confectionery manufacture.

| Sugar Stage | Temperature Range (°C) | Moisture (%) | Final Product Texture |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thread | 110–112 | ~25% | Syrups, liquid fondants |
| Soft Ball | 112–118 | ~12% | Fudge, Fondant, Praline paste |
| Firm Ball | 118–121 | ~10% | Caramel (soft), Marshmallow |
| Hard Ball | 121–130 | ~6% | Nougat, Marshmallow (firm) |
| Soft Crack | 132–143 | ~3% | Butterscotch, Hard caramel |
| Hard Crack | 146–154 | <1% | Toffee, Brittle, Hard candy |
| Caramelization | 160–180 | <1% | Dark caramel, color/flavor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**S**oft, **F**irm, **H**ard, **S**oft, **H**ard, **C**" — **S**yrup, **F**udge, **H**ard ball, **S**oft crack, **H**ard crack, **C**aramelization.

### 2.3 Function of Each Ingredient

| Ingredient | Primary Function | Typical % in Toffee | Typical % in Fudge | Typical % in Caramel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sucrose | Body, sweetness, structure | 35–45 | 30–40 | 40–60 |
| Glucose Syrup | Anti-crystallization, humectancy, body | 20–30 | 15–25 | 10–20 |
| Invert Sugar | Prevents crystallization, softness | 5–10 | 5–10 | 5–10 |
| Butter / Fat | Mouthfeel, flavor, plasticity | 8–15 | 5–10 | 2–8 |
| Condensed Milk | Maillard flavor, body, color | 15–25 | 15–25 | 0–10 (optional) |
| Emulsifier (Lecithin) | Fat dispersion, smoothness | 0.3–0.5 | 0.2–0.4 | — |
| Salt | Flavor enhancement | 0.1–0.3 | 0.2–0.4 | 0.1 |
| Water (process) | Dissolves sugar, evaporates during cook | q.s. | q.s. | q.s. |

### 2.4 Processing of Toffee — Detailed Unit Operations

**Step 1 — Weighing and Mixing (Pre-cook)**
All ingredients except the acid (if any) and a portion of the fat are weighed and blended. Condensed milk is mixed with glucose syrup to prevent scorching.

**Step 2 — Sugar Cook (Boiling)**
The blend is heated in a **steam-jacketed batch cooker** (open pan) or a **continuous vacuum cooker** (Sollich, Latini, or Bühler type) to the target temperature (typically **143°C–154°C** for hard toffee, lower for chewy toffee at **125°C–135°C**). The vacuum cooker operates at lower temperatures (e.g., **125°C at atmospheric equivalent**) to reduce thermal degradation and color formation.

**Step 3 — Addition of Heat-Sensitive Ingredients**
After the cook, the mass is cooled to about **110°C–120°C**, and the reserved butter/fat, emulsifier, flavor (vanilla, fruit essence), and acid (citric/malic) are added with vigorous agitation. This prevents loss of volatile flavor and protects fats from high-temperature oxidation.

**Step 4 — Cooling and Forming**
- **Slab Method:** The mass is poured onto a water-cooled **slab table** (granite or stainless steel) and worked with paddles until plastic.
- **Rope Method:** The mass is fed into a **rope sizer** that extrudes a continuous rope of uniform cross-section, which is then cut by a **rotary cutter** to desired weight.
- **Molding Method:** Poured into starch-molded trays (traditional) or metal molds (modern).

**Step 5 — Wrapping and Packaging**
Each toffee is individually wrapped in **wax paper / cellophane / twist-wrap**, then packed in cartons. See Section 2.6 for packaging details.

### 2.5 Processing of Fudge — Critical Crystallization Step

Fudge manufacture follows the same sugar cook as toffee but stops at the **soft-ball stage (112°C–118°C)**. The unique step is **beating during cooling**:

1. **Cook** sugar, milk, butter, glucose syrup to **soft-ball** (or until a candy thermometer reads **~115°C**).
2. **Cool** to **~75°C–80°C** on a slab or in a jacketed kettle with slow agitation.
3. **Beat** the mass — this **seeds sugar crystals** by mechanical agitation. Sometimes powdered sugar or fondant is added as a **seeding agent** to ensure uniform, fine crystals.
4. **Pour** into trays, allow to set, then **cut** into squares.
5. **Wrap** individually.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Defect in Fudge:** If the mass is beaten too early (above 80°C) or too vigorously, crystals grow too large → **grainy, sandy texture**. If beaten too late, the mass hardens into a **glassy amorphous block** indistinguishable from a soft toffee. KTU questions often test this!

### 2.6 Packaging Requirements for Confectionery Products

Packaging for confectionery must serve **four simultaneous functions**: containment, protection, convenience, and communication.

**Primary Packaging (in direct contact with product):**
- **Twist-wrap** (cellophane, wax paper, PP film) — individual toffees.
- **Flow-wrap** (PE/PP/laminated films) — fudge squares, caramels.
- **Aluminum foil laminate** — for chocolate-coated or high-fat products (oxygen barrier).
- **PVDC-coated films** — for moisture-sensitive products.

**Secondary Packaging:**
- Cartons (corrugated or folding boxboard) — FSSC 22000 / ISO 22000 compliant.
- Pillow pouches, stand-up pouches (laminated: PET/PE, PET/AL/PE).

**Key Requirements per FSSAI / Legal Metrology Act:**
- **Net weight** declaration in metric units.
- **Ingredients** in descending order of predominance.
- **Date of manufacture (DOM)** and **Best Before Date (BBD)**.
- **Batch / Lot number** for traceability.
- **FSSAI Logo + License Number**.
- **Veg / Non-Veg symbol** (green dot / brown dot).
- **Allergen declaration** (milk, soy, nuts).
- **MRP** inclusive of all taxes.
- **Manufacturer/Importer name and address**.
- **Country of origin**.
- **Customer care details**.

**Functional Requirements:**
- **Moisture vapor transmission rate (MVTR)** < 1 g/m²/day.
- **Oxygen transmission rate (OTR)** < 10 cc/m²/day.
- **Grease resistance** (for high-fat confections).
- **Light barrier** (UV protection for color-sensitive products).
- **Hermetic seal** to prevent moisture gain / loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** A change in moisture content of just **+1%** in fudge can cause surface **graining and liquefaction** (syneresis). Packaging barrier properties are not optional — they are a product-quality specification.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water Activity (a_w) | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0} = \dfrac{ERH}{100}$ | Dimensionless | ERH = Equilibrium Relative Humidity (%) |
| Boiling Point Elevation (ΔT) | $\Delta T = K_b \cdot m \cdot i$ | K | $K_b$ for sucrose ≈ 0.512 K·kg/mol |
| Sugar Inversion Rate | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | s⁻¹ | First-order kinetics |
| Moisture Content (wet basis) | $MC = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100$ | % | $m_w$ = mass of water, $m_d$ = mass of dry solids |
| Maillard Reaction Rate | $k_{Maillard} = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Doubles for every +10°C |
| Hardness (TPA) | Force at maximum compression | g or N | Measured by penetrometer |
| Glass Transition Temperature | $T_g = T_{g,water} \cdot x_w + \sum T_{g,i} \cdot x_i$ | K (Gordon–Taylor) | $x$ = mass fraction |
| Boiling Point vs. Vacuum | $T_{cook} = T_{atm} - 0.5 \cdot (P_{atm} - P_{vac})$ approx. | °C | Empirical for sugar syrups |
| Sucrose → Glucose + Fructose | Inversion | — | Catalyzed by acid or enzyme (invertase) |
| Crystallization Driving Force | $\Delta \mu = RT \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C}{C^*}\right)$ | J/mol | $C$ = actual conc., $C^*$ = saturation conc. |
| Dextrose Equivalent (DE) | $DE = \dfrac{\text{Reducing sugars as glucose}}{\text{Total dry substance}} \times 100$ | % | Defines glucose syrup grade |

> [!NOTE]
> The pipe character $\vert$ has been replaced with $\vert$ notation in formulas to preserve the markdown table integrity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Boiling Point Elevation of Sugar Solutions

When sugar is dissolved in water, the boiling point rises due to colligative properties. This is critical because confectioners cannot rely on the water boiling point (100°C) to know when the cook is complete.

Starting from the **Clausius–Clapeyron** relation for a dilute solution:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{p_1}{p_1^*}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_b} - \dfrac{1}{T_b^*}\right)$$

For a dilute solution, $p_1 \approx p_1^*$ and the right-hand side simplifies to:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{p_1}{p_1^*}\right) \approx -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_b^{*2}}$$

By **Raoult's Law** for an ideal solution:

$$\dfrac{p_1}{p_1^*} = x_1 = 1 - x_2$$

where $x_2$ is the mole fraction of solute. Substituting:

$$\ln(1 - x_2) \approx -x_2 = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_b^{*2}}$$

Solving for $\Delta T$:

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{R \cdot T_b^{*2}}{\Delta H_{vap}} \cdot x_2$$

Expressing $x_2$ in terms of **molality** ($m$, mol of solute per kg of solvent):

$$x_2 = \dfrac{m}{m + \dfrac{1000}{M_{solvent}}} \approx \dfrac{m \cdot M_{solvent}}{1000}$$

for dilute solutions, giving the standard form:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m \cdot i}$$

where $K_b$ is the ebullioscopic constant, $m$ is the molality, and $i$ is the van't Hoff factor (≈1 for sucrose, ≈2 for NaCl).

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> *A confectioner dissolves 250 g of sucrose ($M = 342$ g/mol) in 1.0 kg of water. Calculate the boiling point elevation. Take $K_b(\text{water}) = 0.512$ K·kg/mol.*

**Step 1:** Calculate molality

$$m = \dfrac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass} \times \text{kg solvent}} = \dfrac{250}{342 \times 1.0} = 0.7310 \text{ mol/kg}$$

**Step 2:** Apply formula with $i = 1$ (sucrose does not ionize)

$$\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m \cdot i = 0.512 \times 0.7310 \times 1$$

$$\Delta T_b = 0.374 \text{ K} \approx 0.37°C$$

**Step 3:** Final boiling point

$$T_b = 100 + 0.37 = 100.37°C$$

> **Note:** This is the *dilute* value. In a real confectionery cook, sugar concentrations are very high (70–85% solids), and the elevation can reach **+20°C to +50°C**, which is precisely why the cook reaches 140–160°C before all water is driven off.

### 3.2 Derivation: Gordon–Taylor Equation for Glass Transition

The glass transition temperature ($T_g$) of a confection determines whether it is glassy (hard, shelf-stable) or rubbery (soft, prone to sticking). For a multi-component system:

$$T_g = \dfrac{\sum_i x_i \cdot T_{g,i} \cdot k_i}{\sum_i x_i \cdot k_i}$$

A simplified form for a binary sugar–water system (most relevant for confectionery):

$$\boxed{T_g = \dfrac{x_w \cdot T_{g,w} + k \cdot x_s \cdot T_{g,s}}{x_w + k \cdot x_s}}$$

where:
- $T_{g,w}$ = glass transition of pure water = **$135\text{ K} = -138°C$**
- $T_{g,s}$ = glass transition of pure sucrose = **$340\text{ K} = 67°C$**
- $k$ ≈ 0.20 (empirical coupling constant for sucrose–water)
- $x_w$ = mass fraction of water
- $x_s$ = mass fraction of sucrose

**Step-by-Step Calculation for a Fudge at 10% moisture:**

**Step 1:** $x_w = 0.10$, $x_s = 0.90$

**Step 2:** Numerator

$$N = (0.10)(135) + (0.20)(0.90)(340) = 13.5 + 61.2 = 74.7$$

**Step 3:** Denominator

$$D = 0.10 + (0.20)(0.90) = 0.10 + 0.18 = 0.28$$

**Step 4:** Calculate

$$T_g = \dfrac{74.7}{0.28} = 266.8 \text{ K} = -6.4°C$$

> **Interpretation:** A fudge with 10% moisture has $T_g \approx -6°C$. It must be stored **below this temperature** to remain in the glassy state and retain crispness. Storage at typical room temperature (25°C) puts it in a rubbery state — explaining why fudge is soft and pliable at room temperature (this is *desired*) but would become runny and unstable above ~30°C.

### 3.3 Derivation: Water Activity from Composition

For a binary sucrose–water system, $a_w$ is approximated by:

$$\boxed{a_w = \dfrac{p_{water}}{p_{water}^*}(T) = \gamma_w \cdot x_w}$$

For a complex multi-sugar system, the **Norrish equation** is more accurate:

$$a_w = x_w \cdot \exp(-K \cdot x_s^2)$$

where $K$ ≈ 6.5 for sucrose, and $x_s$ is the mole fraction of sucrose on a sugar-only basis.

**Example: Fudge with 10% moisture, 80% sucrose, 10% glucose syrup solids.**

Approximate $a_w$:
- $x_w$ (on a sugar basis) ≈ 0.10 / 0.90 = 0.111
- $x_s$ ≈ 0.80 / 0.90 = 0.889
- $a_w \approx 0.111 \times \exp(-6.5 \times 0.889^2)$
- $a_w \approx 0.111 \times \exp(-5.14)$
- $a_w \approx 0.111 \times 0.0059 \approx 0.00065$

This extremely low $a_w$ confirms **excellent microbial stability** — no bacteria, yeast, or mould can grow below $a_w < 0.60$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study

| Property | Caramel | Fudge | Toffee |
|---|---|---|---|
| Final cook temperature | 160–180°C (caramelization) | 112–118°C (soft ball) | 140–154°C (hard crack) |
| Moisture content | <1–3% | 8–12% | <1–3% |
| Sugar state | Amorphous, dark | Microcrystalline | Amorphous, glassy |
| Texture | Hard, brittle, glossy | Soft, short, granular | Hard to chewy, glassy |
| Color | Dark amber to deep brown | Light cream to tan | Golden to dark brown |
| $T_g$ (typical) | 50–70°C | -10 to 0°C | 30–50°C |
| $a_w$ | 0.2–0.4 | 0.5–0.7 | 0.2–0.4 |
| Key unit op | High-temp cook | Cook + Crystallization | Cook + Forming |
| Shelf life | 12–18 months | 3–6 months | 9–12 months |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Toffee Manufacturing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Receipt and Weighing] --> B[Pre-Mix Sugars, Milk, Glucose Syrup]
    B --> C[Sugar Cook in Steam-Jacketed Kettle at 140 to 154 deg C]
    C --> D{Cook Complete?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Cool to 110 to 120 deg C on Slab]
    E --> F[Add Heat-Sensitive Ingredients: Butter, Emulsifier, Flavor, Acid]
    F --> G[Agitate and Aerate]
    G --> H[Forming: Slab OR Rope OR Molding]
    H --> I[Cooling and Setting Tunnel]
    I --> J[Wrapping: Twist Wrap or Flow Wrap]
    J --> K[Carton Packing]
    K --> L[Metal Detection and QC Check]
    L --> M[Storage at 20 to 25 deg C RH 50 percent]
    M --> N[Distribution]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Fudge Manufacturing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Weighing: Sugar, Condensed Milk, Butter, Glucose Syrup] --> B[Blending and Pre-Mixing]
    B --> C[Sugar Cook to Soft Ball Stage 112 to 118 deg C]
    C --> D[Add Optional Seeding Agent: Fondant or Powdered Sugar]
    D --> E[Cool to 75 to 80 deg C with Slow Agitation]
    E --> F[Beating: Crystal Nucleation and Growth]
    F --> G[Check Crystal Texture: Short, Non-Sticky]
    G --> H[Pour into Greased or Lined Trays]
    H --> I[Setting and Cooling to 25 deg C]
    I --> J[Demolding and Cutting into Squares]
    J --> K[Individually Wrap in Wax Paper or Foil]
    K --> L[Carton Packing]
    L --> M[Storage Below Tg minus 6 deg C for Long Life]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Flow Architecture — Caramel vs Fudge vs Toffee

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CARAMEL_LINE
        CA1[Cook 160-180 deg C] --> CA2[Add Milk Optional] --> CA3[Pour Mold] --> CA4[Cool Set] --> CA5[Demold Wrap]
    end
    subgraph FUDGE_LINE
        F1[Cook 112-118 deg C] --> F2[Cool 75-80 deg C] --> F3[Beat Crystallize] --> F4[Pour Set Cut] --> F5[Wrap]
    end
    subgraph TOFFEE_LINE
        T1[Cook 140-154 deg C] --> T2[Cool 110-120 deg C] --> T3[Add Flavor Fat] --> T4[Form Rope or Slab] --> T5[Cut Wrap]
    end
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Quality Control and Regulatory Compliance Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Incoming Raw Material Inspection] --> Q2[In-Process Checks: Temperature, Brix, pH]
    Q2 --> Q3[Final Product Testing]
    Q3 --> Q3A[Moisture Content]
    Q3 --> Q3B[Water Activity a_w]
    Q3 --> Q3C[Texture Profile: TPA]
    Q3 --> Q3D[Color: L* a* b*]
    Q3 --> Q3E[Microbiological: TPC, Yeast Mould, Coliforms]
    Q3 --> Q3F[Sensory: Triangle Test, Hedonic Scale]
    Q3A --> Q4[Regulatory Compliance Verification]
    Q3B --> Q4
    Q3C --> Q4
    Q3D --> Q4
    Q3E --> Q4
    Q3F --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q4A[FSSAI Standards IS 1166 IS 6287]
    Q4 --> Q4B[Codex Alimentarius]
    Q4 --> Q4C[BIS AGMARK]
    Q4 --> Q4D[Legal Metrology Packaged Commodities Rules]
    Q4A --> Q5[Document and Release for Dispatch]
    Q4B --> Q5
    Q4C --> Q5
    Q4D --> Q5
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Packaging Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Primary Contact: Product] --> P2[Inner Wrap: Wax Paper, Cellophane, or Foil Laminate]
    P2 --> P3[Secondary: Carton or Pouch with Branding and Label]
    P3 --> P4[Tertiary: Corrugated Shipper Case]
    P4 --> P5[Pallet and Stretch Wrap]
    P5 --> P6[Distribution]
    P2 -.Barrier Properties.-> P2A[MVTR less than 1 g per sqm per day]
    P2 -.Barrier Properties.-> P2B[OTR less than 10 cc per sqm per day]
    P2 -.Barrier Properties.-> P2C[Grease Resistant]
    P2 -.Barrier Properties.-> P2D[Light Barrier UV]
    P3 -.Mandatory Labels.-> P3A[Net Weight, MRP, BBD, FSSAI Logo, Veg Symbol]
    P3 -.Mandatory Labels.-> P3B[Ingredients, Allergens, Batch No]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Differentiate between **caramelization** and the **Maillard reaction**. Which one is primarily responsible for color development in milk toffee?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Caramelization | Maillard Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| Substrates | Sugars only | Reducing sugars + amino groups (proteins, amino acids) |
| Temperature | 160°C and above | 50–150°C (faster at higher T) |
| Catalyst | Heat only | Heat, low pH (acidic), moisture |
| First products | HMF, furfural, reductones | Glycosylamine, Amadori compounds |
| Final products | Caramelans, caramelens, caramelins (melanoidins) | Melanoidins (brown polymers) |
| Requires nitrogen? | No | **Yes** (amino acid source) |

**In milk toffee:** Since milk solids (containing casein and whey proteins) are present, the **Maillard reaction** is the **dominant** pathway for both color and flavor development. Pure caramelization contributes secondary notes. The two reactions occur simultaneously, but Maillard is responsible for the characteristic **butterscotch / nutty brown color** of milk toffee. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the role of **glucose syrup** in toffee and fudge manufacture? Why is it preferred over adding only sucrose?

**Model Answer:**

Glucose syrup (a hydrolysis product of starch, with **Dextrose Equivalent (DE) of 38–45** for confectionery grade) serves **four critical functions**:

1. **Inhibits crystallization of sucrose** — the longer glucose polymer chains physically interfere with sucrose crystal lattice formation, ensuring a smooth, non-grainy texture.
2. **Hygroscopicity control** — moderates moisture uptake/loss, preventing the product from drying out or becoming sticky.
3. **Prevents sucrose inversion loss** — reduces the proportion of free monosaccharides, improving storage stability.
4. **Contributes to body and chewiness** — high-molecular-weight dextrins give the characteristic chewy bite.

**Why not only sucrose:** Pure sucrose, when cooked to a high temperature, can **invert** (hydrolyse to glucose + fructose) under acidic conditions, producing an unpredictable texture (sticky, hygroscopic) and browning variability. Glucose syrup buffers the system and ensures batch-to-batch consistency. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **manufacturing process of toffee** with a flow diagram. Discuss the functions of each ingredient used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **different sugar cook stages** with their corresponding temperatures and final product applications. Why is **final cook temperature** the most critical control point in confectionery? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a): Manufacturing Process of Toffee**

**1. Raw Material Preparation (1 mark)**
- Sucrose, glucose syrup, condensed milk, butter, emulsifier (lecithin), salt, and flavor (vanilla/fruit essence) are weighed accurately.
- Condensed milk and glucose syrup are pre-blended to prevent localized scorching.

**2. Sugar Cook / Boiling (2 marks)**
- The blend is heated in a **steam-jacketed batch cooker** (or continuous vacuum cooker) to **140°C–154°C** depending on the desired hardness (hard toffee: 150–154°C; chewy toffee: 125–135°C).
- Continuous stirring ensures even heat distribution.
- The cook is monitored using a **candy thermometer** or **refractometer** (Brix measurement).

**3. Cooling and Addition of Heat-Sensitive Ingredients (1 mark)**
- The cooked mass is cooled to **110°C–120°C** by transferring to a water-cooled slab or by reducing jacket steam.
- Reserved butter, emulsifier, flavor, and acid are added with vigorous agitation.
- This step prevents flavor loss by evaporation and protects the fat from thermal degradation.

**4. Forming (2 marks)**
- **Slab method:** Mass is poured onto an oiled/silicone-coated slab, cooled to plastic consistency, then cut.
- **Rope method (industrial):** Mass is fed through a **rope sizer** to produce a uniform cross-section rope, then cut by a **rotary guillotine cutter** into individual pieces of predetermined weight.
- **Molding:** Poured into metal or starch molds for shaped products.

**5. Cooling, Wrapping, and Packing (1 mark)**
- Cut toffees are conveyed through a **cooling tunnel** (15–20°C, RH 50–55%).
- Each piece is **individually twist-wrapped** in cellophane, wax paper, or laminated foil.
- Wrapped toffees are packed into **cartons**, then into **corrugated shippers**, and palletized.

**Ingredient Functions (already covered in Section 2.3; summarize in tabular form for 1 mark):**

| Ingredient | Function |
|---|---|
| Sucrose | Body, sweetness, structure |
| Glucose syrup | Anti-crystallization, humectancy |
| Condensed milk | Maillard browning, flavor, body |
| Butter | Mouthfeel, flavor, plasticity |
| Lecithin | Emulsification, smoothness |
| Salt | Flavor enhancement |
| Flavor | Sensory appeal |
| Acid | Flavor, slight inversion control |

**[7 marks total — distribute as: process flow (5 marks) + ingredient function (2 marks)]**

---

**Model Answer (b): Sugar Cook Stages**

Refer to the table in **Section 2.2** for the seven cook stages, temperatures, moisture, and applications. **(4 marks for the table and explanation)**

**Why Final Cook Temperature is the Most Critical Control Point (3 marks):**

1. **Defines final texture** — A 2°C error can convert a soft-ball (fudge) into a hard-ball (marshmallow) or a soft-crack (butterscotch) into a hard-crack (toffee). The consumer-perceived texture changes dramatically with small temperature differences.

2. **Defines moisture content** — Each stage corresponds to a specific residual moisture (Section 2.2 table). An under-cook leaves too much water → sticky, low-shelf-life product. An over-cook drives off too much water → hard, brittle, often burnt product.

3. **Defines color and flavor** — Higher temperatures produce deeper Maillard browning and more bitter / caramelized notes. The flavor profile is temperature-dependent.

4. **Defines glass transition and shelf life** — Moisture and composition determine $T_g$ (Gordon–Taylor), which in turn determines storage stability.

5. **Defines quality reproducibility** — Industrial production demands batch-to-batch uniformity. Temperature control is the single variable that allows this.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part A Q1 (Caramelization vs Maillard):**
> - Many students write "Maillard requires proteins" but **forget to state that the sugars involved must be *reducing* sugars** (glucose, fructose, lactose). **Sucrose is non-reducing and does not enter Maillard until it is first inverted.** This omission costs 1 mark.
> - Some students confuse caramelization (sugars only) with **pyrolysis** (general thermal decomposition of all organics). Use the precise term *caramelization* and note that it is a specific subset of pyrolysis of sugars.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part A Q2 (Glucose Syrup):**
> - Students often list only "anti-crystallization" and forget **hygroscopicity control, body contribution, and sucrose inversion buffering**. Listing all four functions is mandatory for full marks.
> - Mentioning the **DE value (38–45)** is highly valued by examiners and earns a *plus mark* in valuation.

---

**Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **manufacturing process of fudge** in detail. Discuss the **crystallization step** and its effect on final texture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **quality parameters, BIS/FSSAI standards, and packaging requirements** for confectionery products with specific reference to toffee and fudge. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a): Fudge Manufacturing and Crystallization**

**1. Raw Material Preparation** — Same as toffee, but with a higher proportion of milk/condensed milk and slightly more butter. **(1 mark)**

**2. Sugar Cook to Soft-Ball (112°C–118°C)** — Final moisture is held at **8–12%**, not driven below 1% as in toffee. This residual moisture is essential for the soft, plastic texture. **(1 mark)**

**3. Cooling to 75°C–80°C** — The mass is transferred to a slab or to a cooling-jacketed kettle. Slow cooling encourages the formation of a **supersaturated sucrose solution**. **(1 mark)**

**4. Beating / Crystallization (THE CRITICAL STEP — 2 marks)**

Mechanism:
- The supersaturated solution is **metastable** — it wants to crystallize but needs a **nucleation trigger**.
- **Mechanical agitation** (beating with paddles) provides the shear energy and the **crystal seeds** (small sucrose crystals broken off by the beater blades).
- Optional: A small amount of **fondant (pre-crystallized sugar paste)** or **powdered icing sugar** is added as a **seeding agent** to control crystal size and uniformity.
- Crystals nucleate and grow to a controlled size of **10–50 µm**, which gives the characteristic *short*, *melt-in-the-mouth*, *non-sticky* texture.
- Over-beating or beating at too high a temperature (above 80°C) produces **large, gritty crystals** (sandy fudge).
- Under-beating or beating at too low a temperature leaves the mass as an **amorphous, glassy solid** (essentially a soft toffee — a common KTU exam error!).

**5. Pouring, Setting, Cutting, Wrapping** — Standard confectionery operations. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (b): Quality Parameters, Standards, and Packaging**

**Quality Parameters (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Toffee | Fudge | Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture | <3% | 8–12% | Hot air oven, IR moisture balance |
| $a_w$ | 0.2–0.4 | 0.5–0.7 | $a_w$ meter (Aqualab) |
| Texture (hardness) | >2000 g | 200–600 g | Texture profile analyzer |
| Color (L*) | 30–50 (dark) | 60–75 (light) | Colorimeter |
| Brix (in-process) | 85–88° | 78–82° | Refractometer |
| Microbial (TPC) | <10⁴ cfu/g | <10⁴ cfu/g | Plate count agar |
| Yeast & mould | <100 cfu/g | <100 cfu/g | YMC agar |
| Coliforms | Absent in 1 g | Absent in 1 g | VRB agar |
| Heavy metals (Pb) | <2.0 ppm | <2.0 ppm | AAS |
| SO₂ (if used) | <350 ppm | <350 ppm | Modified Monier-Williams |

**BIS and FSSAI Standards (2 marks):**
- **IS 1166:1986** — Specification for Toffee (BIS).
- **IS 6287:1985** — Specification for Caramel.
- **FSSAI** — All confectionery products are regulated under the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011**, and subsequent amendments.
- **AGMARK** — Voluntary grading for certain products.
- **Codex Alimentarius** — International reference (e.g., CODEX STAN 192-1995 for food additives).
- **FSSAI License / Registration** is mandatory for all manufacturers.

**Packaging Requirements (2 marks):**
- **Primary:** Food-grade, non-toxic, moisture-proof, grease-proof. Wax paper, cellophane, PVDC film, aluminum foil laminate.
- **Mandatory Labelling (Legal Metrology Act + FSSAI):** Net quantity, MRP, DOM, BBD, ingredients, allergens, FSSAI logo + license no., veg/non-veg symbol, manufacturer address, customer care.
- **Functional barrier requirements:** MVTR < 1 g/m²/day, OTR < 10 cc/m²/day, light barrier for color stability.
- **Storage condition declaration:** "Store in cool, dry place, away from direct sunlight" — mandatory.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B (Process Questions):**
> - **For toffee/fudge/caramel questions:** Students frequently write the **recipe (ingredient list)** but forget to specify the **cook temperature and the cook stage**. Always state: *"Cooked to soft-ball (115°C)"* or *"Cooked to hard-crack (150°C)"* — this single phrase is worth 1–2 marks.
> - **For crystallization in fudge:** Do not just say "beating is done." Explain *why* (nucleation, seeding, crystal size control). A KTU examiner expects the mechanism, not just the action.
> - **For packaging questions:** Many students list "wrap in plastic" and stop. KTU valuation requires **specific barrier values (MVTR, OTR), regulatory citations (FSSAI / Legal Metrology), and label content**. Vague answers lose 3–4 marks easily.
> - **Common unit errors:** Writing moisture as a *ratio* (0.10) when the question asks for *percentage* (10%). Always state units.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section for last-day revision before the KTU ESE.**

✅ **Three Products, Three Cooks:** Caramel = 160–180°C (no crystallization); Fudge = 112–118°C (controlled crystallization); Toffee = 140–154°C (amorphous, no crystallization).

✅ **Sugar Cook Stages (Memorize the Table):** Thread → Soft Ball → Firm Ball → Hard Ball → Soft Crack → Hard Crack → Caramelization.

✅ **Caramelization ≠ Maillard:** Caramelization = sugars only, ≥160°C. Maillard = reducing sugars + amino groups, 50–150°C, needs nitrogen.

✅ **Glucose Syrup DE Value:** Confectionery grade = **DE 38–45**; high DE = more hygroscopic and browning.

✅ **Crystallization in Fudge:** Beat at 75–80°C, optionally seed with fondant, target crystal size 10–50 µm.

✅ **Water Activity Target:** <0.6 for microbial stability. Fudge is borderline (0.5–0.7) — keep storage cool.

✅ **Glass Transition (Gordon–Taylor):** Fudge with 10% moisture has $T_g \approx -6°C$ — store below this for crispness; above this becomes rubbery.

✅ **Mandatory Labels (FSSAI + Legal Metrology):** Net weight, MRP, DOM, BBD, ingredients, allergens, FSSAI logo + license, veg/non-veg symbol, manufacturer address, customer care.

✅ **BIS Standards:** IS 1166 (Toffee), IS 6287 (Caramel). FSSAI License mandatory for all food businesses.

✅ **Packaging Barrier Numbers:** MVTR < 1 g/m²/day, OTR < 10 cc/m²/day, grease-resistant, light barrier.

✅ **Key Equipment:** Steam-jacketed kettle, vacuum cooker, slab table, rope sizer, rotary cutter, cooling tunnel, twist-wrap machine.

✅ **Defects to Know:** Grainy fudge (over-beat / too hot), glassy fudge (under-beat), sticky toffee (under-cook), burnt toffee (over-cook), bloomed fat on surface (temperature fluctuation / improper emulsification).

✅ **High-Yield Keywords for ESE Answers:** "Amorphous vs Microcrystalline", "Glass Transition", "Water Activity", "Maillard vs Caramelization", "Cook Stage", "BIS/FSSAI Compliance", "Barrier Properties", "Sugar Inversion".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Confectionery and Chocolate Processing: Liquorice Processing, Aerated Confectionery, Chewing Gum, Bubble Gum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT412 - BAKERY AND CONFECTIONERY PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Confectionery and Chocolate Processing: Liquorice Processing, Aerated Confectionery, Chewing Gum, Bubble Gum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Confectionery – The Broader Context

**Confectionery** is the art and science of preparing sugar-based food products, broadly classified into two major divisions: *sugar confectionery* (candies, toffees, gums) and *chocolate confectionery*. Within sugar confectionery, a specialized sub-branch deals with **functional and textured confections** — products that are not merely sweet but possess unique structural, masticatory, or sensory properties. This module focuses on four such specialized products: **Liquorice, Aerated Confectionery, Chewing Gum, and Bubble Gum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** This module shifts focus from *flour-based* (bakery) to *sugar/polymer-based* (confectionery) systems, emphasizing phase changes, sugar polymorphism, aeration thermodynamics, and elastomer–plasticizer interactions.

---

## 1.2 Liquorice (Licorice) Confectionery

### Formal Definition
**Liquorice confectionery** is a sugar-based sweet product in which the characteristic flavour and physiological activity are derived from the root extract of *Glycyrrhiza glabra* (the liquorice plant), containing the active compound **glycyrrhizin** (a saponin glycoside ~50 times sweeter than sucrose).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a soft, chewy rope made of sweet tar — that is essentially traditional liquorice. The *root extract* acts like a natural sweetener, foam stabilizer, and emulsifier all rolled into one. The confectioner essentially turns a *pharmaceutical herb* into a *candy*.

### Classification
| Type | Sweetness Profile | Texture |
|------|-------------------|---------|
| **English/Liquorice Paste Type** | Strong, lingering bitterness | Soft, plastic, dark |
| **American/Pan-Processed Type** | Milder | Hard, glossy, salty |
| **Sugar-coated Liquorice** | Variable | Crunchy outer shell |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Composition (Typical):** Sugar syrup (60–70%), wheat flour or starch (5–10%), liquorice extract (5–15%), gelatine (2–4%), fat (1–3%), salt, and colour. **Glycyrrhizin content** in finished product is legally restricted in many countries (e.g., < 4% in EU, regulated due to hypertensive effects).

---

## 1.3 Aerated Confectionery

### Formal Definition
**Aerated confectionery** refers to sugar-based products in which **gas (air, carbon dioxide, or nitrogen) is mechanically or chemically incorporated** into the sugar matrix, producing a foam-like, porous, low-density structure. The product is often called *marshmallow*, *nougat*, *halwa*, or *cinder toffee* depending on the recipe and aeration method.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of **whipped cream versus cold milk** — same milk, but air incorporation transforms its density and texture. In aerated confectionery, the sugar syrup replaces the cream, and a stabilizer (gelatine, egg white, or hydrocolloid) replaces the milk fat. The "air pockets" are stabilized as the sugar solidifies around them, like **frozen soap bubbles in glass**.

### The Three Industrial Aeration Routes
1. **Mechanical Whipping** (e.g., marshmallow) – using a beater to fold air into a syrup-gelatine mix.
2. **Chemical Aeration** (e.g., cinder toffee, popping candy) – using sodium bicarbonate reacting with acid.
3. **Pressure Dissolution** (e.g., compressed aerated candy bars) – gas dissolved under pressure is released during atmospheric pressure drop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Density Metric:** A non-aerated sugar candy has density ~1.4 g/cm³, while an aerated marshmallow has density ~0.3–0.5 g/cm³ (i.e., 60–75% air by volume).

---

## 1.4 Chewing Gum

### Formal Definition
**Chewing gum** is a viscoelastic, non-digestible confectionery product designed to be masticated (chewed) without being swallowed, consisting of a water-insoluble **gum base** (a synthetic elastomer blend), sweeteners, softeners, and flavouring agents. It retains its plastic and elastic properties throughout the chewing period.

### Conceptual Analogy
A chewing gum is essentially **a piece of rubber dressed up as candy**. The gum base behaves like a synthetic balloon — it stretches (elastic recovery) under teeth pressure but returns to shape. The sweeteners dissolve in saliva and gradually wash away, but the rubbery matrix persists for **30+ minutes of chewing**.

### Historical Note
- **Ancient chewing materials:** Chicle (sapodilla tree sap), mastic, gum arabic.
- **Modern era (post-WWII):** Synthetic polymers like **styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR), polyethylene, polyisobutylene (PIB), and polyvinyl acetate (PVA)** replaced natural chicle due to consistency and cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Definition — "Gum Base":** A manufactured, non-nutritive, water-insoluble mixture of elastomers, resins, waxes, and fillers that provides the structural matrix of chewing gum. It is **NOT** the same as the natural product "gum arabic".

---

## 1.5 Bubble Gum

### Formal Definition
**Bubble gum** is a specialized variant of chewing gum in which the gum base is formulated to have **higher extensibility and film-forming ability**, allowing the chewer to inflate a thin, stretchy balloon (bubble) of the gum membrane by blowing air through it.

### Conceptual Analogy
If chewing gum is **silly putty**, bubble gum is **a stretchy latex balloon**. The difference lies in two key properties: **plasticity (the ability to deform without rupturing)** and **film strength (the ability to form a thin, airtight membrane)**. Bubble gum base contains more **polyisobutylene (PIB)** and less hard wax, giving it that signature "stretch."

### Comparative Snapshot
| Property | Chewing Gum | Bubble Gum |
|----------|-------------|------------|
| Primary elastomer | Polyvinyl acetate (PVA) | Polyisobutylene (PIB) |
| Bubble inflation | Not possible | Yes |
| Texture | Firm, resilient | Softer, more plastic |
| Sugar release | Slow, sustained | Moderate |
| Chew time | 30–60 min | 20–45 min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Density of bubble gum film:** ~0.95–1.05 g/cm³ (just under water — this is why properly inflated bubble gum films can float momentarily before popping!).

---

## 1.6 The "Big Picture" — Why These Four Products Are Studied Together

All four belong to the family of **texturally-advanced sugar confections**, where the processing objective is no longer just *sweetness* but **controlling structure at the molecular/mesoscopic level**:

- **Liquorice** → controls the **plastic mass flow** of a sugar-gelatin-licorice dough.
- **Aerated confectionery** → controls the **gas–liquid–solid interface** of a foam.
- **Chewing gum** → controls the **viscoelastic recovery** of a polymer–plasticizer blend.
- **Bubble gum** → extends that control to **membrane extensibility**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Course Outcome (CO4) Mapping:** Students should be able to *describe the manufacturing processes, key ingredients, and quality control parameters* of these specialty confectionery products (KTU 2024 Scheme — PEFTT412).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Liquorice Processing — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Raw Materials and Their Functional Roles

| Ingredient | Function | Typical % |
|------------|----------|-----------|
| **Sugar (sucrose + glucose syrup)** | Bulk sweetener, body | 55–70 |
| **Wheat flour / starch** | Filler, texture, water absorption | 8–12 |
| **Liquorice extract** (block or powder) | Flavour, sweetness, dark colour, foam stabilizer | 5–15 |
| **Gelatine / Gum Arabic** | Binder, plasticizer | 2–4 |
| **Hardened vegetable fat** | Lubricant, mouthfeel | 1–3 |
| **Salt** | Flavour enhancer, bitterness modulator | 0.5–1 |
| **Glycerine / Sorbitol** | Humectant, plasticity | 1–2 |
| **Water** | Solvent, plasticizer | 10–15 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Glycyrrhizin Chemistry:** Glycyrrhizin (C₄₂H₆₂O₁₆, MW = 822.93 g/mol) is a triterpenoid saponin. Its sweetness threshold is ~1.6 × 10⁻⁴ mol/L — about **50–100× sweeter than sucrose** on a molar basis. The ammoniated salt form is used in foods (less bitter aftertaste).

### 2.1.2 The Manufacturing Process — Stepwise

**Step 1: Preparation of Liquorice Extract**
- The dried roots of *Glycyrrhiza glabra* are crushed and boiled in water at 95–100°C for 4–6 hours.
- The decoction is filtered, concentrated by vacuum evaporation to ~60–65% solids → "**liquorice block**."
- Alternatively, the root is extracted with dilute ammonia solution to yield "**ammoniated glycyrrhizin**."

**Step 2: Sugar Syrup Preparation**
- Sucrose and glucose syrup (40 DE) are dissolved in water at 80°C to form a syrup of ~70% solids.
- The syrup is boiled to ~145–150°C (Brix ~85–88%) to develop a plastic mass.

**Step 3: Mixing and Kneading**
- The hot sugar mass, flour, and pre-softened liquorice extract are loaded into a **double-Z-arm sigma mixer (kneader)** operating at 30–60 rpm, 70–80°C, for 30–60 min.
- Gelatine solution and glycerine are added to control plasticity.

**Step 4: Extrusion / Forming**
- The plastic dough is passed through an **extruder** fitted with a die to form ropes, strips, or tubes.
- Traditional process: **"rope-twisting"** — a screw extruder pushes the mass through a multi-hole die, and the ropes are cut and twisted by hand or mechanical twisters.
- American type: a **rotary mould** forms individual pieces.

**Step 5: Drying**
- Drying is done in tunnel or cabinet dryers at 50–60°C, RH ~30–40%, for 18–36 hours until moisture drops from ~12% to **< 8%**.

**Step 6: Optional Coating**
- Pan coating with sugar syrup, wax, or salt for glossy finish (American-type "Pontefract cakes").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Process Control Point (CCP):** Final moisture must be **6–8%** to prevent both *mould growth* (>10% moisture) and *excessive hardening* (<5% moisture). The Water Activity (a_w) target is **0.55–0.65**.

### 2.1.3 Quality Parameters for Liquorice

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|----------------|
| Moisture | 6–8% |
| Water activity (a_w) | 0.55–0.65 |
| Glycyrrhizin content | 0.5–4% (regulatory max) |
| pH | 5.5–6.5 |
| Texture (penetration test) | 3–8 mm at 5 s, 1 mm probe |
| Colour | Deep brown-black (E150d caramel or natural) |

---

## 2.2 Aerated Confectionery — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Physics of Aeration

Aeration in confectionery is fundamentally a **foam stabilization** problem. A foam is a **dispersion of gas bubbles in a continuous liquid/solid matrix**, with the gas fraction (φ) typically ranging from 0.4 to 0.85.

The **relative density** of an aerated product is given by:

$$\rho_{aerated} = \rho_{syrup} \times (1 - \phi) + \rho_{gas} \times \phi$$

Since $\rho_{gas} \approx 0.0012$ g/cm³ (air at STP) is negligible:

$$\rho_{aerated} \approx \rho_{syrup} \times (1 - \phi)$$

This is a **Key KTU formula** linking gas fraction to product density — a common exam question.

### 2.2.2 Foam Stability Theory

A foam is thermodynamically unstable because of high interfacial energy. Stabilization is achieved by:
1. **Surface-active agents** (gelatine, egg albumin, hydrocolloids) — lower surface tension.
2. **High viscosity of the continuous phase** — slows drainage.
3. **Gibbs–Marangoni effect** — surface tension gradient resists thinning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Young–Laplace Equation** (governs bubble pressure in foams):
>
> $$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma}{r}$$
>
> where $\Delta P$ = pressure difference across the bubble film, $\gamma$ = surface tension (N/m), $r$ = bubble radius (m). This explains why *smaller bubbles exert higher pressure* and tend to merge into larger ones (Ostwald ripening in foams).

### 2.2.3 Industrial Methods of Aeration

**Method 1: Mechanical Beating (Marshmallow, Nougat)**
- A sugar syrup boiled to 110–118°C (depending on desired firmness) is combined with pre-hydrated **gelatine** (5–10% bloom) at 60–70°C.
- The mixture is whipped in a continuous high-shear **beater** at 200–500 rpm.
- Air is incorporated as 0.3–1.0 mm bubbles. The mix expands 2–4× in volume.
- The aerated foam is deposited into moulds, dusted with starch, and dried at 30–40°C for 12–24 hours.

**Method 2: Chemical Aeration (Cinder Toffee / Sponge Candy)**
- Sugar is heated to ~150°C ("hard crack" stage).
- A small amount of **sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃)** + cream of tartar (KHC₄H₄O₆) is added.
- The acid–base reaction:
  $$\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{KHC}_4\text{H}_4\text{O}_6 \rightarrow \text{KNaC}_4\text{H}_4\text{O}_6 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$
- CO₂ is released, forming a honeycomb-like porous structure that solidifies on cooling.

**Method 3: Carbon Dioxide Under Pressure (Compressed Candy / "Aero-style")**
- Sugar syrup is saturated with CO₂ under **4–6 bar pressure** at 80°C.
- When the pressure is suddenly released, dissolved CO₂ nucleates, producing a fine, uniform cellular structure.
- Used in products like **Aero** chocolate (a UK brand).

### 2.2.4 Critical Process Parameters

| Parameter | Marshmallow | Cinder Toffee | Compressed |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|------------|
| Sugar boil temperature | 110–118°C | 145–155°C | 80°C (syrup) |
| Aeration agent | Gelatine/egg white | NaHCO₃ + acid | CO₂ (pressure) |
| Final density (g/cm³) | 0.3–0.5 | 0.4–0.6 | 0.5–0.7 |
| Air/gas fraction (φ) | 0.55–0.75 | 0.50–0.65 | 0.45–0.60 |
| Stabilizer | Gelatine 200–250 bloom | Caramel matrix (sucrose invert) | Cocoa butter (in chocolate) |

---

## 2.3 Chewing Gum — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 The Gum Base — The Heart of Chewing Gum

The gum base is the **structural heart** of chewing gum. Modern gum bases are formulated from 4–6 functional ingredient classes:

| Component | Function | Typical Examples | % in Base |
|-----------|----------|------------------|-----------|
| **Elastomers** | Provide elasticity, bounce | SBR, PIB, Polyisoprene, PVA | 15–30 |
| **Resins** | Tacky, helps bind sweetener | Polyvinyl acetate (low MW), terpene resins | 25–40 |
| **Waxes/Hardeners** | Control softening, melt profile | Microcrystalline wax, paraffin, candelilla | 5–15 |
| **Fillers** | Reduce cost, modify texture | Calcium carbonate, talc, titanium dioxide | 10–25 |
| **Plasticizers/Softeners** | Modify flexibility | Lanolin, glycerol esters, vegetable oils | 5–15 |
| **Antioxidants** | Prevent oxidation | BHT, BHA, tocopherols | 0.05–0.1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Viscoelasticity Principle:** A chewing gum behaves as a **viscoelastic solid**, exhibiting both **elastic (storage modulus G')** and **viscous (loss modulus G'')** responses. For a chewy yet coherent feel: $G' \approx 10^5$–$10^6$ Pa and $G'' \approx 10^4$–$10^5$ Pa. If $G''$ >> $G'$: gum is "sloppy"; if $G'$ >> $G''$: gum is "rubbery/hard."

### 2.3.2 The Chewing Gum Manufacturing Process

**Stage 1: Gum Base Preparation**
- Elastomers are melted at 90–120°C in a high-shear **sigma mixer**.
- Resins and waxes are added incrementally to ensure uniform dispersion.
- Fillers are added to bulk up and control texture.
- The base is cooled and sheeted into slabs or pellets.

**Stage 2: Sweetener/Flavor Incorporation**
- The cooled base is re-melted at 60–70°C.
- Sugar (50–65%) and glucose syrup (10–20%) are added and mixed until homogeneous.
- Flavors (mint, fruit) and softeners are added last to avoid volatilization.

**Stage 3: Forming**
- **Sheeting and Scoring** — the gum mass is rolled into a sheet (2–5 mm thick) and cut into sticks/pellets.
- **Extrusion** — for pellet/chunk gums: extruder with die plate.
- **Rolling and Wrapping** — automatic wrappers at 600–1500 pieces/min.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sugar-Free Note:** In sugar-free gums, sucrose is replaced by **sorbitol, xylitol, mannitol, or isomalt**. Xylitol additionally provides **anti-cariogenic** properties (does not feed *Streptococcus mutans*).

### 2.3.3 Critical Quality Parameters

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| Moisture | 2.5–4.0% |
| Texture (hardness) | 800–2000 g (penetration force, 2 mm probe) |
| Sugar release rate | 70–85% in 5 min of chewing |
| Chewing time before flavor loss | 15–25 min |
| Softening temperature | 50–60°C (sticks) |
| Shelf life | 18–24 months |

---

## 2.4 Bubble Gum — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.4.1 The "Bubble" Phenomenon — A Polymer Physics Insight

A bubble is a **thin film of gum** stretched by pulmonary air pressure (~0.05–0.1 atm in the lungs). For the film to inflate without rupturing, the gum base must satisfy three conditions:

1. **High elongational viscosity** — film can be stretched thin without breaking.
2. **Self-sealing** — small punctures close via surface tension-driven polymer flow.
3. **Slow strain-hardening** — as the film thins, it doesn't become brittle.

These are achieved by:
- **Higher PIB content** (40–60% of elastomer phase) → gives long, flexible polymer chains.
- **Lower wax content** → keeps the base soft and extensible.
- **Plasticizer addition** → increases chain mobility.

### 2.4.2 Comparative Polymer Formulation

| Polymer | Chewing Gum (%) | Bubble Gum (%) | Effect |
|---------|-----------------|----------------|--------|
| Polyvinyl acetate (PVA) | 30–40 | 10–20 | Chewy/firm |
| Polyisobutylene (PIB) | 5–15 | 30–45 | Stretchy, elastic |
| SBR rubber | 10–20 | 5–10 | Bounce |
| Terpene resin | 20–30 | 20–30 | Tack, flavor carrier |
| Waxes | 10–20 | 3–8 | Soften / harden control |
| Plasticizer | 5–10 | 8–15 | Softness, extensibility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exam:** Bubble gum has **more PIB and more plasticizer** but **less wax and less high-MW PVA** than regular chewing gum. The base is typically **20–30% softer** (lower Shore A hardness) than a comparable chewing gum base.

### 2.4.3 Bubble Gum Processing (Same as Chewing Gum, but with modified base)

The process flow is **identical** to chewing gum — the difference is **entirely in the formulation** of the base. The "bubble-ability" is a function of:
- PIB/PVA ratio
- Plasticizer-to-wax ratio
- Molecular weight distribution of the elastomer

> [!NOTE]
> **Density of bubble gum film** ≈ 0.95–1.05 g/cm³, while the bulk ball is ~1.1–1.3 g/cm³. The thinned film is *less dense* because the polymer chains are oriented and air gaps exist at the molecular level. This is why a freshly blown bubble **floats momentarily** if it detaches!

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gas Fraction (φ) from Density Data

This is a **standard KTU problem**: "A marshmallow mix has syrup density 1.35 g/cm³. The final marshmallow density is 0.45 g/cm³. Calculate the % air by volume."

### Step 1 — Set up the conservation of mass
Consider a volume $V$ of the aerated product. The volume occupied by syrup (condensed phase) is $V_s$, and the volume occupied by gas is $V_g$.

$$V = V_s + V_g$$

The total mass $M$ is only from the syrup (gas mass is negligible):

$$M = \rho_{syrup} \cdot V_s$$

### Step 2 — Express the bulk density
The bulk (apparent) density of the aerated product is:

$$\rho_{aerated} = \frac{M}{V} = \frac{\rho_{syrup} \cdot V_s}{V_s + V_g}$$

### Step 3 — Introduce the gas fraction
Define the gas fraction:

$$\phi = \frac{V_g}{V}$$

Therefore $V_g = \phi \cdot V$ and $V_s = (1 - \phi) \cdot V$.

### Step 4 — Substitute and simplify
Substituting into the density expression:

$$\rho_{aerated} = \frac{\rho_{syrup} \cdot (1-\phi) \cdot V}{V}$$

The $V$ terms cancel:

$$\rho_{aerated} = \rho_{syrup} \cdot (1 - \phi)$$

### Step 5 — Solve for φ
Rearranging:

$$(1 - \phi) = \frac{\rho_{aerated}}{\rho_{syrup}}$$

$$\phi = 1 - \frac{\rho_{aerated}}{\rho_{syrup}}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical evaluation
Given: $\rho_{syrup} = 1.35$ g/cm³, $\rho_{aerated} = 0.45$ g/cm³.

$$\phi = 1 - \frac{0.45}{1.35}$$

$$\phi = 1 - 0.3333$$

$$\phi = 0.6667$$

Therefore, the air volume fraction is **66.67%**, i.e., the marshmallow is **two-thirds air by volume**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Industrial marshmallows have air fraction 55–75%, so our calculated value of 66.67% lies **comfortably within range**. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CO₂ Yield from Sodium Bicarbonate in Cinder Toffee

**Problem:** A candy manufacturer uses 50 g of NaHCO₃ per 10 kg of sugar mass for cinder toffee. Calculate the theoretical volume of CO₂ released at STP (T = 273 K, P = 1 atm, molar volume = 22.4 L/mol).

### Step 1 — Write the balanced equation
For the reaction with cream of tartar (KHC₄H₄O₆):

$$\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{KHC}_4\text{H}_4\text{O}_6 \rightarrow \text{KNaC}_4\text{H}_4\text{O}_6 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

### Step 2 — Identify stoichiometry
From the balanced equation, **1 mole of NaHCO₃ produces 1 mole of CO₂**.

Molar masses:
- $M_{NaHCO_3} = 23 + 1 + 12 + 48 = 84$ g/mol
- $M_{CO_2} = 12 + 32 = 44$ g/mol

### Step 3 — Calculate moles of NaHCO₃
$$n_{NaHCO_3} = \frac{50 \text{ g}}{84 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.5952 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate moles of CO₂
By 1:1 stoichiometry:
$$n_{CO_2} = 0.5952 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate volume at STP
$$V_{CO_2} = n_{CO_2} \times V_m = 0.5952 \times 22.4 \text{ L}$$

$$V_{CO_2} = 13.33 \text{ L (at STP)}$$

### Step 6 — Account for process conditions (correction factor)
At candy temperature (~150°C = 423 K), using the ideal gas law:

$$V_{actual} = V_{STP} \times \frac{T_{process}}{T_{STP}}$$

$$V_{actual} = 13.33 \times \frac{423}{273}$$

$$V_{actual} = 20.66 \text{ L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Implication:** A 50 g NaHCO₃ dose produces about **20 L of CO₂ gas at 150°C**, which is sufficient to create the porous honeycomb structure in 10 kg of candy. In practice, only 30–50% of this CO₂ is retained (rest escapes); the rest of the porosity comes from thermal expansion of the trapped gas during cooling.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bubble Film Thickness and Stability

**Problem:** A bubble gum ball of mass 2.0 g is blown into a bubble of diameter 12 cm. Assuming all the gum is used in the film, estimate the average film thickness.

### Step 1 — Volume of the bubble
The blown bubble is roughly a sphere of radius $r = 6$ cm = 0.06 m.

$$V_{bubble} = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (0.06)^3$$

$$V_{bubble} = \frac{4}{3} \times 3.1416 \times 0.000216$$

$$V_{bubble} = 9.05 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

Wait — this is the volume **enclosed by air**, not the gum. The bubble **film** is a thin shell of gum that forms the surface of the sphere.

### Step 2 — Surface area of the sphere
$$A_{sphere} = 4 \pi r^2 = 4 \pi (0.06)^2 = 0.0452 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 3 — Volume of gum (using density)
The gum density is ~1.1 g/cm³ = 1100 kg/m³.

$$V_{gum} = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}}{1100 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 1.818 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 4 — Film thickness
The film is a spherical shell. For a thin shell, $V_{film} \approx A_{sphere} \times t$, where $t$ = thickness.

$$t = \frac{V_{gum}}{A_{sphere}} = \frac{1.818 \times 10^{-6}}{0.0452}$$

$$t = 4.02 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 40.2 \text{ \mu m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A blown bubble gum film is only about **40 μm thick** — about 3× the thickness of a human hair! This is why it appears transparent and why surface tension is so critical to its stability. If the film thins below ~5–10 μm, it ruptures (Rayleigh–Taylor instability).

---
