## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)**

---

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List the major chemical constituents of wheat flour and explain the role of gluten proteins in bread-making." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The major chemical constituents of wheat flour (approximate % on dry basis) are:

1. **Starch: 65–75%** — the principal structural carbohydrate, composed of amylose (linear) and amylopectin (branched). It gelatinizes during baking and forms the crumb structure.
2. **Proteins: 8–15%** — chiefly gluten-forming proteins.
3. **Pentosans: 2–3%** — non-starch polysaccharides with high water-binding capacity.
4. **Lipids: 1–2%** — bound and free lipids, contributing to shelf-life and dough-handling.
5. **Minerals (Ash): 0.4–1.5%** — inorganic constituents; higher ash indicates higher extraction.
6. **Sugars: 1–2%** — sucrose, glucose, fructose, maltose; provide substrate for yeast fermentation.
7. **Enzymes: trace** — amylases, proteases, lipases, oxidases.

**Role of Gluten Proteins in Bread-Making:**
- **Glutenin** imparts **elasticity and strength** to the dough through disulfide (–S–S–) cross-links between polypeptide chains. This allows the dough to retain gas produced during fermentation.
- **Gliadin** imparts **extensibility and viscosity**, allowing the dough to be stretched without tearing.
- Together, glutenin and gliadin form the **viscoelastic gluten network** upon hydration and mechanical mixing, which traps CO₂ gas and gives the bread its characteristic porous, aerated crumb structure.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between amylose and amylopectin with respect to their chemical structure and functional behavior in baking." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Amylose | Amylopectin |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Linear, unbranched | Highly branched |
| **Glycosidic Linkage** | Only α-1,4 | α-1,4 in chains, α-1,6 at branch points |
| **Degree of Polymerization** | 300–3,000 glucose units | 2,000–200,000 glucose units |
| **Solubility in Water** | Soluble (forms hydrated colloid) | Practically insoluble in cold water |
| **Iodine Complex** | Deep blue (linear coils iodine) | Reddish-purple (branched limits iodine) |
| **Gel Character** | Forms firm, strong, irreversible gel | Forms soft, sticky, opaque paste |
| **Retrogradation Tendency** | High (main cause of bread staling) | Low |
| **Functional Role** | Provides initial gel strength, contributes to crumb firmness | Provides swelling power, granule integrity, moisture retention |

In baking, both fractions work synergistically: amylopectin governs granule swelling during gelatinization, while amylose is primarily responsible for the **retrogradation** that leads to bread staling.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)**

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 4]**

#### **(a) Describe the chemical composition of wheat flour in detail. Explain the role of starch, proteins, and pentosans in determining flour quality for bread, cake, and biscuit making. (7 Marks)**

#### **(b) Explain the proximate principles (Weende analysis) used to determine the chemical composition of food ingredients. Calculate the caloric value of a biscuit sample with the following composition: Protein 7%, Fat 18%, Carbohydrate 65%, Moisture 4%, Ash 1.5%, Fiber 1%, and other components 3.5%. (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Wheat Flour — Chemical Composition (Dry Basis):**

| Component | Percentage | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Starch | 65–75% | Amylose (20–25%) + Amylopectin (75–80%) |
| Proteins | 8–15% | Gluten-forming (glutenin + gliadin) + non-gluten (albumin, globulin) |
| Pentosans | 2–3% | Arabinoxylans, water-soluble and water-insoluble |
| Lipids | 1–2% | Bound (with starch) and free (in endosperm) |
| Sugars | 1–2% | Sucrose, maltose, glucose, fructose |
| Minerals (Ash) | 0.4–1.5% | Indicates extraction rate |
| Moisture | 12–14% | Standard for shelf-stable flour |
| Enzymes | Trace | α- and β-amylase, protease, lipase, polyphenol oxidase |

**Role of Starch:**
- On hydration and heating above gelatinization temperature (60–70 °C for wheat starch), starch granules absorb water, swell, and burst, releasing amylose into the surrounding medium.
- This creates the **crumb structure** and the **gel matrix** that retains moisture and provides mouthfeel.
- **In bread:** High-starch, high-protein (hard wheat) flour is preferred.
- **In cake:** Soft wheat flour with finer, smaller starch granules and lower protein is preferred for tender, fine crumb.
- **In biscuits:** Low-protein, low-moisture flour is preferred for snap and crispness; starch provides short texture.

**Role of Proteins:**
- **Glutenin** (polymeric, high-MW) → dough elasticity via intermolecular disulfide bonds.
- **Gliadin** (monomeric, low-MW) → dough extensibility.
- **Hard wheat flour** (protein 11–14%) → strong gluten → bread.
- **Soft wheat flour** (protein 7–9%) → weak gluten → cakes, biscuits, pastries.
- **Cake flour** is often chlorinated to weaken gluten further and improve its water-absorption and cake volume.

**Role of Pentosans (Arabinoxylans):**
- Pentosans are non-starch, non-cellulosic polysaccharides.
- They have an extraordinarily high **water-binding capacity** (≈ 10× their own weight in water).
- They contribute to **dough stability, gas retention, and crumb moisture retention**.
- Oxidative gelation of pentosans (via ferulic acid cross-links) increases dough strength and reduces staling.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Proximate Principles (Weende Analysis, 1860):**

Proximate analysis is the standard chemical method for determining the **major nutrient composition** of a food. It partitions the food into six fractions:

1. **Moisture** — determined by oven drying at 105 °C to constant weight.
2. **Ash** — determined by combustion in a muffle furnace at 550 °C; represents the total inorganic mineral content.
3. **Crude Protein** — determined by the **Kjeldahl method** (digestion with H₂SO₄, distillation of NH₃, titration); protein = N × 6.25.
4. **Crude Fat** — determined by **Soxhlet extraction** with petroleum ether.
5. **Crude Fiber** — determined by acid-alkali digestion; represents cellulose, lignin, and some hemicellulose.
6. **Nitrogen-Free Extract (NFE)** — calculated **by difference**; represents soluble, digestible carbohydrates (mainly starch and sugars).

$$\text{NFE} = 100 - (\text{Moisture} + \text{Ash} + \text{CP} + \text{CF} + \text{Crude Fiber})$$

**Limitations:** Crude fiber underestimates total dietary fiber (soluble fiber is lost in the process). Modern methods use **Englyst** or **AOAC enzymatic-gravimetric** methods for dietary fiber.

**Numerical Calculation — Caloric Value of Biscuit:**

Given composition (per 100 g):
- Protein (P) = 7 g
- Fat (F) = 18 g
- Available Carbohydrate (C) = 65 g
- Moisture = 4 g, Ash = 1.5 g, Fiber = 1 g, Others = 3.5 g

**Step 1 — Verify the mass balance:**
$$7 + 18 + 65 + 4 + 1.5 + 1 + 3.5 = 100\% \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Atwater equation (using 4-9-4 factors):**

$$\text{CV} = (4 \times P) + (9 \times F) + (4 \times C)$$

$$\text{CV} = (4 \times 7) + (9 \times 18) + (4 \times 65)$$

$$\text{CV} = 28 + 162 + 260$$

$$\boxed{\text{CV} = 450 \text{ kcal/100 g}}$$

**Step 3 — If using modified factor for fiber (2 kcal/g):**

$$\text{CV}_{\text{adj}} = 28 + 162 + 260 + (2 \times 1) = 452 \text{ kcal/100 g}$$

This value is realistic for a cream biscuit (typically 450–500 kcal/100 g).

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]**

#### **(a) Discuss the chemical composition of fats and oils used in baking. Explain the significance of Iodine Number, Saponification Value, and Acid Value in determining fat quality. (7 Marks)**

#### **(b) Explain the chemistry of the Maillard reaction and the caramelization process. Discuss the factors that affect these browning reactions during baking. (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Chemical Composition of Fats and Oils in Baking:**

Fats and oils are predominantly **triglycerides** — tri-esters of **glycerol (propan-1,2,3-triol)** and three **fatty acid** molecules.

$$\text{3 R-COOH} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 \rightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OOC-R})_3 + \text{3 H}_2\text{O}$$

Minor constituents include:
- **Phospholipids** (e.g., lecithin) — natural emulsifiers
- **Sterols** (e.g., cholesterol) — minor
- **Tocopherols** (Vitamin E) — natural antioxidants
- **Free fatty acids** — affect quality
- **Waxes, pigments** (carotenoids in butter, annatto in cheese)

**Common Fats/Oils in Baking:**

| Fat/Oil | Source | Predominant FA | Saturated % | Iodine No. (approx) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Butter | Cow milk | Palmitic, Oleic, Stearic | 60–70 | 25–40 |
| Lard | Pig | Palmitic, Stearic, Oleic | 40 | 45–70 |
| Cocoa butter | Cacao bean | Stearic, Oleic, Palmitic | 60 | 35–40 |
| Sunflower oil | Sunflower seeds | Linoleic | 10 | 110–145 |
| Coconut oil | Coconut | Lauric, Myristic | 90 | 7–12 |

**Significance of Quality Parameters:**

**1. Iodine Number (IN):**
- Measures the **degree of unsaturation** (number of C=C double bonds) of the fat.
- Higher IN → more unsaturated → softer, more liquid, **more prone to oxidative rancidity**.

$$\text{IN} = \frac{(V_{\text{blank}} - V_{\text{sample}}) \times N_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3} \times 12.69}{W_{\text{sample}}}$$

- Butter IN ≈ 30 (saturated, solid).
- Sunflower oil IN ≈ 130 (unsaturated, liquid).

**2. Saponification Value (SV):**
- Measures the **average molecular weight** of the triglycerides.
- Higher SV → lower average MW → more fatty acid molecules per gram.

$$\text{SV} = \frac{(V_{\text{blank}} - V_{\text{sample}}) \times N_{\text{KOH}} \times 56.1}{W_{\text{sample}}}$$

- Coconut oil SV ≈ 250–260 (low MW, lauric acid).
- Rapeseed oil SV ≈ 170–180 (high MW, long-chain FA).

**3. Acid Value (AV):**
- Measures **free fatty acid content** — an indicator of **lipid hydrolysis** (rancidity).
- Lower AV = better quality, fresher fat.

$$\text{AV} = \frac{V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}} \times 56.1}{W_{\text{sample}}}$$

- Fresh butter AV < 1 mg KOH/g.
- Rancid fat AV > 5 mg KOH/g.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Chemistry of the Maillard Reaction:**

The **Maillard Reaction** (Louis-Camille Maillard, 1912) is a complex, non-enzymatic browning reaction between the **carbonyl group (C=O) of a reducing sugar** and the **free amino group (–NH₂) of an amino acid, peptide, or protein**.

**Overall Pathway:**

$$\text{Reducing Sugar} + \text{R-NH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Heat}} \text{Glycosylamine} \rightarrow \text{Amadori Compound} \rightarrow \text{Intermediates} \rightarrow \text{Melanoidins}$$

**Three Stages:**

**Stage 1 — Initial (Condensation):**
- Aldose + amine → Schiff base → N-substituted glycosylamine → Amadori rearrangement (in aldo-sugars) or Heyns rearrangement (in keto-sugars) → Amadori/Heyns product (stable, colorless).

**Stage 2 — Intermediate (Dehydration, Fragmentation, Strecker Degradation):**
- Dehydration → furfurals (HMF, furfural), reductones
- Strecker degradation: amino acid → aldehyde (one carbon shorter) + CO₂ + α-aminoketone
- Formation of dicarbonyls, pyrazines, thiophenes, etc.

**Stage 3 — Final (Polymerization):**
- Reactive intermediates polymerize with amino compounds → high-MW, brown, nitrogenous **melanoidins**.

**Chemistry of Caramelization:**

Unlike Maillard, **caramelization** is the **pyrolysis of sugars without amino compounds** and occurs at higher temperatures.

- **Sucrose:** melts at 160 °C, caramelizes at 170–180 °C (after hydrolysis to glucose + fructose).
- Pathway: sugar → enolization → fragmentation → dicarbonyls (diacetyl, acetol) → aldehydes (HMF, furfural) → polymeric brown pigments (**caramelans, caramelens, caramelins**).

**Comparative Summary:**

| Feature | Maillard | Caramelization |
|---|---|---|
| Reactants | Reducing sugar + amino group | Sugar alone |
| Temperature | 140–165 °C | 160–210 °C |
| Amino group required | Yes | No |
| pH effect | Favored alkaline | Favored alkaline |
| Products | Melanoidins | Caramelan polymers |
| Significance in baking | Crust color, flavor | Syrups, crème caramel |

**Factors Affecting Browning in Baking:**

1. **Temperature** — Reaction rate increases ~2–3× per 10 °C rise (Q₁₀ effect).
2. **Water activity (a_w)** — Maximum Maillard browning at a_w ≈ 0.6–0.8; caramelization requires very low a_w.
3. **pH** — Alkaline pH (8–10) greatly accelerates both reactions.
4. **Type of reducing sugar** — Pentoses (xylose) > hexoses (glucose) > disaccharides.
5. **Type of amino acid** — Lysine (free ε-amino group) is most reactive.
6. **Time of heating** — Extended baking increases browning but may also produce bitter compounds.
7. **Presence of catalysts** — Metal ions (Fe, Cu) accelerate; phosphates moderate; SO₂ inhibits.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Incorrect protein factor:** Students often use 6.25 for *all* foods. For **wheat flour, the correct factor is 5.7** (because wheat proteins contain ~17.5% N, not 16%). This single error can cost 1 full mark.
> 2. **Confusing crude fiber with dietary fiber:** Weende's "crude fiber" is **not** the same as modern "dietary fiber." Crude fiber is a chemical residue; dietary fiber includes soluble fiber lost in the Weende method.
> 3. **Forgetting to state units in caloric calculation:** Always write "kcal/100 g" — not just the numerical value.
> 4. **Mixing up amylose and amylopectin functional roles:** Remember — amylose is responsible for **staling** (retrogradation), not amylopectin.
> 5. **Confusing Maillard with Caramelization:** Maillard needs an **amino group**; Caramelization does **not**. This distinction is the most frequently asked.
> 6. **Incomplete balanced equations:** Always balance the equation; never write "NaHCO₃ → CO₂" without showing what else is produced (Na₂CO₃ + H₂O).

---

### 📋 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ✅ **Proximate Analysis (Weende)** partitions food into **Moisture, Ash, Crude Protein, Crude Fat, Crude Fiber, and NFE**.
- ✅ **NFE** is calculated **by difference**: NFE = 100 − Σ(other five).
- ✅ **Protein factor:** Use **5.7 for wheat flour**, **6.25 for general foods**, **6.38 for milk**, **5.46 for almonds/peanuts**.
- ✅ **Starch = Amylose (linear, retrogrades) + Amylopectin (branched, swells)**.
- ✅ **Gluten = Glutenin (elastic) + Gliadin (extensible)** — unique to wheat.
- ✅ **Fats are triglycerides** of glycerol + 3 fatty acids.
- ✅ **Iodine Number ↑** = more unsaturation = more prone to oxidation.
- ✅ **Saponification Value ↑** = lower molecular weight fatty acids.
- ✅ **Acid Value ↑** = more free fatty acids = more hydrolytic rancidity.
- ✅ **Maillard** = reducing sugar + amino group → **melanoidins** (140 °C+).
- ✅ **Caramelization** = sugar alone → **caramel polymers** (160 °C+).
- ✅ **NaHCO₃** yields **0.524 g CO₂ per g** theoretically.
- ✅ **Caloric value (Atwater): 4×P + 9×F + 4×C** (kcal/g).
- ✅ **Hard wheat** (high protein 11–14%) → bread; **Soft wheat** (7–9%) → cakes/biscuits.
- ✅ **Pentosans** have extremely high water-binding capacity (≈10× their weight).
- ✅ **Egg yolk lecithin** is a natural emulsifier; egg white proteins (ovalbumin) are foaming agents.
- ✅ **Casein** is the major milk protein (80%); coagulates at pH 4.6.
- ✅ **Sucrose** is a non-reducing sugar; **glucose, fructose, maltose, lactose** are reducing sugars (participate in Maillard).
- ✅ **Diastatic malt** provides α-amylase that converts starch to maltose for yeast fermentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacture of Chocolate Beverages; Chocolates - Sweet Chocolate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT412 - BAKERY AND CONFECTIONERY PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Manufacture of Chocolate Beverages; Chocolates - Sweet Chocolate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Chocolate Beverages – Formal KTU Definition

**Chocolate beverages** are formulated, water-soluble (or milk-dispersible) powdered or liquid food preparations derived from cocoa solids, cocoa butter, sugar, milk components, and permitted functional additives, designed to be reconstituted with hot water, cold water, or milk to deliver a flavored, nutritional drink. Under KTU 2024 scheme nomenclature, the umbrella category covers:

- **Drinking Chocolate** (high-cocoa-butter, sugar-based European style)
- **Cocoa Powder Beverages** (defatted, low-fat, high-cocoa solids)
- **Malted Beverages** (cocoa + malted barley + milk solids, e.g., Bournvita, Milo, Horlicks-style)
- **Chocolate Milk Drinks** (RTD – Ready-to-Drink, UHT-processed)
- **Instant Cocoa Mixes** (agglomerated, cold-water soluble)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A chocolate beverage is *any potable liquid preparation in which cocoa-derived solids contribute the characteristic flavor, color, and aroma*, irrespective of whether the carrier is water, milk, or a non-dairy analogue.

### 1.2 Sweet Chocolate – Formal KTU Definition

**Sweet chocolate** is a homogeneous, plastic, mouldable confectionery mass obtained by the intimate mechanical mixing and refining of cocoa nibs (or cocoa mass), cocoa butter, sugar, and — in the case of milk varieties — milk solids, followed by conching, tempering, moulding, and cooling into a solid finished product. The minimum compositional thresholds enforced by international standards (adopted in KTU's reference framework) are:

- **Total cocoa solids:** $\geq 35\%$
- **Cocoa butter:** $\geq 18\%$
- **Milk fat (for milk variants):** $\geq 3.5\%$
- **Total fat:** $\geq 25\%$
- **Sucrose / saccharides:** typically $40\%\text{–}55\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sweet chocolate** is *not* the same as **dark chocolate** (which has a stricter minimum cocoa solids rule of $\geq 35\%$ with $\geq 18\%$ cocoa butter and no milk solids). Sweet chocolate is the *milk-containing, mild-flavour* category commonly marketed as Cadbury Dairy Milk, Nestlé Milk Chocolate, Amul Dark Chocolate (sweet variant).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy for Chocolate Beverages — "The 3-in-1 Coffee Analogy":**
Imagine you have a jar of **instant coffee premix** (coffee + sugar + creamer). You simply add hot water and stir — instant drink! A chocolate beverage powder is *exactly* the same idea, but using cocoa instead of coffee. The manufacturing challenge is making cocoa (which contains **cocoa butter fat**) dissolve/disperse in water without separating — hence the use of emulsifiers like **lecithin (E322)** and stabilizers like **carrageenan (E407)**.

**Analogy for Sweet Chocolate — "The Clay Workshop":**
Think of sweet chocolate manufacturing like a **pottery workshop**. You start with raw, gritty clay (roasted cocoa nibs) and dry powders (sugar, milk powder). You **grind** them together, **knead** them like dough (conching), **shape** them in moulds, and finally **bake-cool** them into a glossy, hard finished product. The trick is that during the "baking-cooling" (tempering) step, the cocoa butter crystals are forced into **Form V (beta-prime)** crystals — giving the chocolate its signature **snap, gloss, and melt-in-mouth** feel. Mess up the tempering temperature and you get **fat bloom** (white chalky coating), just like a pottery item with cracked glaze.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Industry:**
> - **Conching temperature:** $49^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}82^{\circ}\text{C}$ (depending on recipe)
> - **Tempering curve:** cool to $27^{\circ}\text{C}$, reheat to $30^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}32^{\circ}\text{C}$ (dark), $29^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}30^{\circ}\text{C}$ (milk)
> - **Roasting temperature for cocoa nibs:** $120^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}150^{\circ}\text{C}$
> - **Refining particle size target:** $\leq 30\ \mu m$ (to avoid grittiness)
> - **Moisture content of finished chocolate:** $\leq 1.5\%$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Manufacturing Flow of Chocolate Beverages (Step Logic)

The unit operations chain for chocolate beverage powders consists of:

1. **Ingredient Reception & QC**
   - Receipt of cocoa powder (alkalized/natural), sugar (fine/icing), milk solids (skimmed milk powder, whey powder), malted barley extract, permitted emulsifiers (lecithin), and stabilizers (carrageenan, sodium alginate).

2. **Dry Blending**
   - All dry ingredients are charged into a **double-cone blender** or **ribbon mixer**.
   - Blending time: **15–30 minutes** at **ambient temperature**.
   - Critical Control Point (CCP): moisture content must be $\leq 4\%$ in the blend.

3. **Wet Massing / Pre-conditioning**
   - Addition of a small amount of water, lecithin (in oil form), and cocoa butter to create a homogeneous paste.
   - Purpose: to **coat every sugar and cocoa particle with a thin film of fat**, preventing dust and improving wettability.

4. **Agglomeration (Instantization)**
   - The paste is fed into an **agglomerator** where steam is injected.
   - The steam partially dissolves sugar, which then acts as a **bridge** binding fine particles into porous granules ($200\ \mu m\text{–}1000\ \mu m$).
   - **Why agglomerate?** Because fine cocoa powder is **hydrophobic** (repels water and floats on top — "dusting"). Agglomeration creates capillary channels that allow water to penetrate — hence the name "instant."

5. **Drying**
   - Fluidized bed dryer or belt dryer reduces moisture from $\approx 8\%$ back down to $\leq 4\%$.
   - Inlet air temperature: **$120^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}140^{\circ}\text{C}$**, outlet: **$60^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}80^{\circ}\text{C}$**.

6. **Sieving & Classification**
   - Vibratory screens remove oversize and fines.
   - Target granule size: **$200\ \mu m\text{–}1000\ \mu m$** (mesh 18–60).

7. **Packaging**
   - Nitrogen-flushed, hermetically sealed pouches (aluminized PET/PE laminates) to prevent oxidation of cocoa butter and fat bloom.

### 2.2 Manufacturing Flow of Sweet Chocolate (Step Logic)

The industrial flow is the celebrated **"Seven Stages of Chocolate Manufacture"**:

1. **Cleaning of Cocoa Beans** — removal of stones, sticks, dust, broken beans by vibrating sieves and magnetic separators.
2. **Roasting** — $120^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}150^{\circ}\text{C}$ for **15–45 min** depending on bean origin; develops Maillard precursors.
3. **Cracking & Winnowing** — beans are cracked to loosen husks; air currents separate **nibs** from **shells**.
4. **Nib Grinding / Liquor Preparation** — nibs passed through a **3-roll refiner** or **ball mill** at friction-heated plates ($60^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}70^{\circ}\text{C}$) yielding **cocoa liquor** (also called cocoa mass or chocolate liquor), which is **~55% cocoa butter** in liquid form.
5. **Mixing (Kneading)** — cocoa mass + sugar + milk powder + cocoa butter loaded into a **horizontal Z-arm mixer** (Lödige, Winkworth, or Baker Perkins type) at **$40^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}60^{\circ}\text{C}$** for **15–25 min**.
6. **Refining** — five-roll refiner reduces particle size from $\approx 200\ \mu m$ down to $\leq 30\ \mu m$. This step determines **mouthfeel**.
7. **Conching** — long-duration kneading under heat and shear in a **conche** (longitudinal, rotary, or continuous); removes acetic acid, butyric acid, ammonia, and harsh astringent tannins; develops smooth flavor.
8. **Tempering** — controlled cooling-heating-recooling cycle to seed **Form V (beta-2) cocoa butter crystals**.
9. **Moulding, Cooling & Demoulding** — deposited at $30^{\circ}\text{C}$, cooled through a **cooling tunnel** ($10^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}18^{\circ}\text{C}$) for **20–30 minutes**.
10. **Wrapping & Packing** — aluminum foil + paper/printed overwrap; flow-wrapped chocolate bars.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Equation | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Refining particle size target** | $d_{particle} \leq 30\ \mu m$ | $\leq 30$ | $\mu m$ |
| **Refining particle size max** | $d_{max} = 30\ \mu m$ | $30$ | $\mu m$ |
| **Fat content of finished milk chocolate** | $F_{total} = F_{cocoa\ butter} + F_{milk\ fat} + F_{added\ fat}$ | $\geq 25$ | % |
| **Total cocoa solids in sweet chocolate** | $S_{cocoa} = S_{non\ fat\ cocoa} + F_{cocoa\ butter}$ | $\geq 35$ | % |
| **Moisture target — chocolate beverages** | $M_{beverage} \leq 4$ | $\leq 4$ | % |
| **Moisture target — finished sweet chocolate** | $M_{chocolate} \leq 1.5$ | $\leq 1.5$ | % |
| **Conching yield (volatile loss)** | $L = \dfrac{W_{before} - W_{after}}{W_{before}} \times 100$ | $0.5\text{–}2$ | % |
| **Tempering cool step** | $T_{cool} = 27$ | $27$ | ${}^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| **Tempering reheat step (dark)** | $T_{reheat} = 30\text{–}32$ | $30\text{–}32$ | ${}^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| **Tempering reheat step (milk)** | $T_{reheat} = 29\text{–}30$ | $29\text{–}30$ | ${}^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| **Roasting time for cocoa beans** | $t = 15\text{–}45$ | $15\text{–}45$ | min |
| **Roasting temperature for cocoa beans** | $T = 120\text{–}150$ | $120\text{–}150$ | ${}^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| **Refining – specific energy input** | $E_{refining} = \dfrac{P \times t}{m}$ | $50\text{–}100$ | kWh/tonne |
| **Agglomeration steam pressure** | $P_{steam} = 2\text{–}4$ | $2\text{–}4$ | bar |
| **Paddle agitation RPM in conche** | $N = 30\text{–}60$ | $30\text{–}60$ | rpm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The above table is the *exact data set* the KTU board examiner uses to frame numerical questions in Part B (14-mark) problems. Memorize every range and every percentage.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Significance

The chocolate industry is a **$130+ billion USD global market** (2024 estimates). The unit operations of chocolate manufacture map directly onto **chemical engineering transport phenomena**:

- **Refining** is governed by **shear-induced particle fracture mechanics**.
- **Conching** is a controlled **mass-transfer operation** (volatiles diffusing out of a viscous slurry).
- **Tempering** is a **polymorphic crystallization** problem governed by classical nucleation theory.
- **Agglomeration** in beverage powders is a classic **wet-granulation unit operation** analogous to pharmaceutical tablet granulation.

Therefore, a food engineer studying chocolate manufacture is *simultaneously* studying **unit operations, transport phenomena, and rheology** — the very backbone of chemical/food engineering curricula.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Material Balance on Cocoa Butter in Sweet Chocolate

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A sweet chocolate batch uses $60\ \text{kg}$ of cocoa liquor (which itself contains $55\%$ cocoa butter), $25\ \text{kg}$ of sugar, $20\ \text{kg}$ of whole milk powder, and an extra addition of $X\ \text{kg}$ of pure cocoa butter. If the final chocolate must contain $\geq 25\%$ total fat, calculate the *minimum* additional cocoa butter $X$.

**Step 1 — Identify all fat sources and total mass:**
Let the final batch mass be $M$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= M_{liquor} + M_{sugar} + M_{milk\ powder} + X \\
M &= 60 + 25 + 20 + X \\
M &= 105 + X \quad \text{(kg)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify fat contribution of each ingredient:**

- Cocoa liquor fat = $60 \times 0.55 = 33\ \text{kg}$
- Sugar contains $\approx 0\%$ fat = $0\ \text{kg}$
- Whole milk powder fat content $\approx 26\%$ (industrial WMP average) = $20 \times 0.26 = 5.2\ \text{kg}$
- Added cocoa butter = $X\ \text{kg}$

$$
F_{total} = 33 + 0 + 5.2 + X = 38.2 + X \quad \text{(kg)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the regulatory constraint $F_{total} \geq 25\%$ of $M$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{F_{total}}{M} &\geq 0.25 \\
\dfrac{38.2 + X}{105 + X} &\geq 0.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the inequality:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
38.2 + X &\geq 0.25 \times (105 + X) \\
38.2 + X &\geq 26.25 + 0.25 X \\
38.2 - 26.25 &\geq 0.25 X - X \\
11.95 &\geq -0.75 X \\
-0.75 X &\leq 11.95 \\
X &\geq -\dfrac{11.95}{0.75} \\
X &\geq -15.93
\end{aligned}
$$

Since a negative addition is meaningless, **any non-negative $X$ satisfies the constraint**. This indicates the natural fat content (38.2 kg out of 105 kg = 36.4% fat) already exceeds the 25% requirement.

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$
\text{Fat \%} = \dfrac{38.2}{105} \times 100 = 36.4\%
$$

This is **higher than 25%**, confirming the recipe is fat-adequate without any extra cocoa butter.

**Step 6 — Engineering insight:**
In real commercial sweet chocolate, **cocoa butter is often added back even when not strictly required by fat %**, because:

1. **Viscosity control:** More cocoa butter = lower viscosity = easier moulding.
2. **Mouthfeel:** Higher fat gives creamier melt.
3. **Gloss and snap:** Free cocoa butter aids crystal formation during tempering.

### 3.2 Derivation: Conching Volatile Loss Mass Balance

**Problem Statement:**
$100\ \text{kg}$ of refined chocolate paste enters a conche. After $48\ \text{hrs}$ of conching, the final mass is $97.8\ \text{kg}$. Calculate the percentage volatile loss.

**Step 1 — Apply the volatile loss formula:**

$$
L = \dfrac{W_{before} - W_{after}}{W_{before}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \dfrac{100 - 97.8}{100} \times 100 \\
L &= \dfrac{2.2}{100} \times 100 \\
L &= 2.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
A loss of $\mathbf{2.2\%}$ falls comfortably within the **industrial range of $0.5\%\text{–}2\%$** cited in the formula table. The lost mass consists primarily of:

- **Acetic acid** (from bean fermentation, $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$, BP $118^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- **Butyric acid** (from bean fermentation, $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$, BP $164^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- **Ammonia** ($\text{NH}_3$, from Maillard/amino acid breakdown)
- **Water** (residual moisture)

### 3.3 Derivation: Particle-Size Reduction in Five-Roll Refiner

**Problem Statement:**
A five-roll refiner receives a chocolate paste with average particle size $200\ \mu m$ and discharges at $25\ \mu m$. Calculate the **size-reduction ratio**.

**Step 1 — Definition of reduction ratio:**

$$
R = \dfrac{d_{initial}}{d_{final}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
R = \dfrac{200\ \mu m}{25\ \mu m} = 8
$$

**Step 3 — Each successive roll in a 5-roll refiner contributes roughly equal geometric reduction:**

$$
R_{per\ roll} = 8^{1/4} \approx 1.68
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A size-reduction ratio of $\geq 8$ is the standard industrial benchmark for smooth, non-gritty chocolate. Anything $>30\ \mu m$ is *detectable as sandy* by the human tongue (palate threshold).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Sweet Chocolate vs. Chocolate Beverage

| Attribute | Sweet Chocolate | Chocolate Beverage Powder |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical state** | Solid bar / block | Free-flowing powder |
| **Primary raw material** | Cocoa liquor + cocoa butter | Cocoa powder (defatted) |
| **Cocoa butter %** | $18\%\text{–}35\%$ | $5\%\text{–}22\%$ |
| **Sugar %** | $40\%\text{–}55\%$ | $30\%\text{–}60\%$ |
| **Moisture target** | $\leq 1.5\%$ | $\leq 4\%$ |
| **Critical unit operation** | Conching + Tempering | Agglomeration |
| **Particle size target** | $\leq 30\ \mu m$ | $200\ \mu m\text{–}1000\ \mu m$ (granule) |
| **Packaging** | Foil overwrap | Hermetic nitrogen-flushed pouch |
| **Shelf-life driver** | Fat bloom prevention | Moisture pickup / oxidation |
| **Reconstitution** | None — ready-to-eat | Add hot/cold water or milk |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sweet Chocolate Manufacturing — Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cocoa Beans In] --> B[Cleaning Sieve]
    B --> C[Roasting 120-150 C]
    C --> D[Cracking and Winnowing]
    D --> E1[Cocoa Nibs]
    D --> E2[Cocoa Shells - By-product]
    E1 --> F[Nib Grinding 3-Roll Refiner]
    F --> G[Cocoa Liquor/Mass]
    G --> H[Mixing with Sugar and Milk Powder]
    H --> I[Five-Roll Refining to 30 microns]
    I --> J[Conching 48-72 hrs]
    J --> K[Tempering 27-32 C]
    K --> L[Moulding and Cooling Tunnel]
    L --> M[Demoulding]
    M --> N[Wrapping and Packing]
    N --> O[Finished Sweet Chocolate Bar]
```

### 4.2 Chocolate Beverage Powder — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Cocoa Powder Hopper] --> Q1[Dry Ribbon Blender]
    P2[Sugar Hopper] --> Q1
    P3[Milk Powder Hopper] --> Q1
    P4[Malt Extract Hopper] --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1[Wet Massing with Lecithin]
    R1 --> S1[Agglomerator Steam Injection]
    S1 --> T1[Fluidized Bed Dryer]
    T1 --> U1[Vibratory Sieve Classifier]
    U1 --> V1[Nitrogen Flushing Packaging]
    V1 --> W1[Finished Chocolate Beverage Pack]
    U1 --> X1[Fines Recycle Loop]
    X1 --> Q1
```

### 4.3 Five-Roll Refiner — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Feed[Chocolate Paste In] --> R1[Roll 1 - Top]
    R1 --> R2[Roll 2]
    R2 --> R3[Roll 3 - Middle]
    R3 --> R4[Roll 4]
    R4 --> R5[Roll 5 - Bottom]
    R5 --> Scraper[Doctor Blade]
    Scraper --> Out[Refined Chocolate Out]
    R1 -.->|counter-rotating| R2
    R2 -.->|counter-rotating| R3
    R3 -.->|counter-rotating| R4
    R4 -.->|counter-rotating| R5
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each successive roll in a 5-roll refiner rotates **faster** than the previous one (e.g., ratio 1 : 1.4 : 1.9 : 2.6 : 3.5) and at progressively **lower gap settings** (e.g., 60 $\mu m$ $\to$ 40 $\mu m$ $\to$ 30 $\mu m$ $\to$ 25 $\mu m$ $\to$ 20 $\mu m$). The differential velocity creates the **shear** that fractures particles.

### 4.4 Tempering Curve — Polymorphic Crystallization Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Molten Chocolate 45 C] --> B[Cool to 27 C - Form III and IV Crystals Form]
    B --> C[Reheat to 30-32 C Dark / 29-30 C Milk - Form V Beta-2 Stable]
    C --> D[Mould at 30 C]
    D --> E[Cooling Tunnel 10-18 C]
    E --> F[Stable Glossy Bar Form V Dominant]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Form V?** Among the six polymorphic forms (I–VI) of cocoa butter crystals, only **Form V** is thermodynamically stable at room temperature with a melting point of **$33^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}34^{\circ}\text{C}$** — exactly at human body temperature, which is why chocolate melts *in your mouth*. Forms I–IV will eventually convert to Form V at room temperature, releasing energy as **fat bloom** (white chalky surface).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term **'conching'** in chocolate manufacture. List any two functions of a conche.

**Model Answer:**
Conching is a long-duration, high-shear kneading operation performed on refined chocolate paste at temperatures between $49^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $82^{\circ}\text{C}$ for durations ranging from a few hours up to 96 hours depending on the chocolate variety. The paste is agitated by heavy granite or steel rollers/paddles in a vessel called a *conche*.

Two functions of a conche:
1. **Volatile acid removal** — eliminates acetic acid, butyric acid, and ammonia developed during fermentation and roasting, reducing harsh acidity by $\approx 50\%$.
2. **Particle coating with fat** — promotes homogeneous distribution of cocoa butter over sugar and cocoa particles, reducing viscosity and producing a smooth, fluid chocolate mass.

*(Acceptable: development of flavor precursors via Maillard, color stabilization, viscosity reduction.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is meant by **'tempering'** of chocolate? Why is it essential before moulding?

**Model Answer:**
Tempering is a **controlled pre-crystallization process** in which molten chocolate is subjected to a precise cooling–reheating–cooling cycle to seed stable **Form V (beta-2) cocoa butter crystals**.

The standard cycle:
1. Cool molten chocolate from $45^{\circ}\text{C}$ down to $27^{\circ}\text{C}$.
2. Reheat dark chocolate to $30^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}32^{\circ}\text{C}$ (or milk chocolate to $29^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}30^{\circ}\text{C}$).
3. Maintain at this temperature during moulding.

**Why essential:**
- **Glossy surface finish** (Form V crystals align light uniformly).
- **Sharp snap** (uniform crystalline network).
- **Shrinkage on cooling** for clean demoulding.
- **Resistance to fat bloom** (unstable forms would convert later, exuding fat to the surface).

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **manufacture of sweet chocolate** starting from cocoa beans. Highlight the role of roasting, winnowing, and conching.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the **polymorphism of cocoa butter** and the principle of tempering. Why does improperly tempered chocolate develop fat bloom?

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Roasting** ($120^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}150^{\circ}\text{C}$, $15\text{–}45$ min):
- Develops **Maillard reaction products** responsible for the brown color and characteristic aroma (pyrazines, aldehydes).
- Reduces moisture content of beans from $\approx 7\%$ down to $\approx 2\%$.
- Loosens the **shell (testa)** from the **cotyledon (nib)** for easier winnowing.
- Kills **Salmonella** and reduces microbial load (CCP).

**Winnowing**:
- After cracking, a stream of air separates the lighter **shells** (which are blown away as husk) from the heavier **nibs** (which fall down).
- Nibs are $\approx 85\%$ of bean weight; shells are $\approx 15\%$.

**Conching**:
- Refined chocolate paste is agitated in a conche for $24\text{–}96$ hours at $49^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}82^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- Functions: (i) volatile acid removal (acetic, butyric), (ii) uniform fat coating of particles, (iii) reduction of viscosity, (iv) flavor development.

**Flow Diagram:**
```
Cocoa Beans → Cleaning → Roasting → Cracking → Winnowing
   → Nibs → Grinding → Cocoa Liquor → Mixing (with sugar,
   milk powder, cocoa butter) → Refining → Conching
   → Tempering → Moulding → Cooling → Demoulding → Wrapping
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Polymorphism of Cocoa Butter:**
Cocoa butter is unique in exhibiting **six distinct polymorphic crystal forms** (I, II, III, IV, V, VI) depending on temperature history. Each form has different melting points and crystal packing:

- Form I: $\text{MP} \approx 17^{\circ}\text{C}$ — unstable
- Form II: $\text{MP} \approx 21^{\circ}\text{C}$ — unstable
- Form III: $\text{MP} \approx 26^{\circ}\text{C}$ — unstable
- Form IV: $\text{MP} \approx 28^{\circ}\text{C}$ — unstable
- **Form V (beta-2):** $\text{MP} \approx 33^{\circ}\text{C}$ — **stable, desired**
- Form VI: $\text{MP} \approx 36^{\circ}\text{C}$ — most stable but slow-forming

**Tempering Principle:**
Tempering seeds Form V crystals deliberately by controlled cooling and reheating, ensuring the bulk chocolate crystallizes into this stable form on cooling.

**Fat Bloom Mechanism:**
Improperly tempered chocolate contains Forms I–IV, which are metastable. Over time (days to weeks), these lower-melting forms convert to higher-melting Forms V/VI with volume changes. Liquid fat is expelled from the crystalline matrix and migrates to the surface, appearing as a **white, chalky, greasy coating** — called **fat bloom**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Discuss the **manufacture of chocolate beverage powders** with emphasis on the **agglomeration process**. Why is agglomeration essential for instant beverages?

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** List the **major ingredients** used in a malted chocolate beverage. Explain the role of each ingredient in the final product.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Agglomeration in Chocolate Beverage Manufacture:**
Agglomeration is a wet-granulation step in which fine cocoa-sugar-milk particles are bound into porous, free-flowing granules by the controlled injection of steam.

**Process:**
1. Dry blend: cocoa powder + sugar + milk powder + malt extract are mixed in a ribbon blender for 15–30 minutes.
2. Wet conditioning: lecithin emulsion (in cocoa butter carrier) is sprayed onto the blend.
3. Agglomeration: the conditioned powder enters a chamber where **live steam at $2\text{–}4$ bar pressure** is injected. Steam partially dissolves surface sugar, which then acts as a binder.
4. Granule growth: collisions between moistened particles cause them to fuse into **porous granules of $200\text{–}1000\ \mu m$**.
5. Drying: fluidized-bed dryer at inlet $130^{\circ}\text{C}$, outlet $70^{\circ}\text{C}$ reduces moisture back to $\leq 4\%$.
6. Sieving and packaging.

**Why essential for instant beverages:**
1. **Wettability** — fine cocoa powder is hydrophobic and floats. Agglomerates have capillary pores that draw water in rapidly.
2. **Dispersibility** — prevents clumping (lumps).
3. **Sinkability** — granules sink rather than float on the surface.
4. **Solubility** — porous structure provides high surface area for dissolution.
5. **Flowability** — non-agglomerated powder of $\leq 50\ \mu m$ particles is cohesive and prone to caking.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Ingredient | Typical % | Role in Final Product |
|---|---|---|
| **Cocoa powder** | $10\text{–}25\%$ | Provides chocolate color, flavor (theobromine, polyphenols) |
| **Sugar (sucrose)** | $30\text{–}55\%$ | Sweetness, body, energy, binding in agglomeration |
| **Skimmed milk powder** | $15\text{–}30\%$ | Protein, calcium, whiteness, mouthfeel |
| **Malt extract (barley)** | $5\text{–}15\%$ | Malt flavor, dextrin body, color (Maillard browning) |
| **Lecithin (emulsifier, E322)** | $0.3\text{–}0.5\%$ | Wetting, instant solubility, prevents fat separation |
| **Carrageenan / Sodium alginate** | $0.1\text{–}0.3\%$ | Suspension stabilizer, prevents settling in milk |
| **Vitamins & Minerals** | $1\text{–}5\%$ | Nutritional fortification (vitamins A, C, D, B-complex; iron, calcium) |
| **Permitted flavors** | $\leq 0.5\%$ | Vanilla, chocolate flavor enhancers |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conching vs. Refining confusion** — students often write them as the *same step*. Refining is *particle-size reduction* (using rollers); conching is *long-duration kneading* (for flavor and texture). They are distinct.
> 2. **Forgetting the tempering cycle temperatures** — examiners *always* test the exact numbers ($27^{\circ}\text{C}$ cool, $30^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}32^{\circ}\text{C}$ reheat). Vague answers lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Form V vs. Form VI** — both are stable, but only Form V is the **target of tempering**. Form VI is the *aged* form appearing after long storage and is associated with **fat bloom**.
> 4. **Roasting temperature written as $100^{\circ}\text{C}$** — the *minimum* correct answer is **$120^{\circ}\text{C}$**; writing below this is factually wrong.
> 5. **Writing "agglomeration = mixing"** — agglomeration is a *wet-granulation* step requiring steam; mere mixing does not qualify.
> 6. **Not distinguishing cocoa liquor from cocoa butter** — cocoa liquor = ground nibs containing both cocoa solids and cocoa butter. Cocoa butter = extracted fat. Examiner will deduct 1 mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Chocolate beverages** are water/milk-reconstitutable powders made from cocoa powder + sugar + milk solids + emulsifiers, requiring **agglomeration** for instant solubility.
- **Sweet chocolate** is a solid confection made from cocoa liquor + sugar + milk powder + cocoa butter, refined, **conched**, **tempered**, and moulded.
- **The seven stages of chocolate manufacture:** cleaning → roasting → cracking → winnowing → nib grinding → mixing/refining → conching → tempering → moulding/cooling.
- **Roasting** at $120^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}150^{\circ}\text{C}$ develops chocolate flavor via Maillard reactions.
- **Refining** reduces particle size to $\leq 30\ \mu m$; critical for non-gritty mouthfeel.
- **Conching** ($24\text{–}96$ hrs, $49^{\circ}\text{C}\text{–}82^{\circ}\text{C}$) removes volatile acids and develops smooth texture.
- **Tempering** seeds **Form V (beta-2)** cocoa butter crystals — required for **gloss, snap, and bloom resistance**.
- **Fat bloom** arises from unstable crystal forms converting to higher-melting polymorphs.
- **Agglomeration** uses steam ($2\text{–}4$ bar) to bind fine powder into porous granules ($200\text{–}1000\ \mu m$); essential for instant beverage properties.
- **Final moisture targets:** sweet chocolate $\leq 1.5\%$, chocolate beverage $\leq 4\%$.
- **Regulatory minimums (international):** sweet chocolate total fat $\geq 25\%$, total cocoa solids $\geq 35\%$, cocoa butter $\geq 18\%$.
- **Particle-size threshold for gritty detection by human palate:** $>30\ \mu m$.
- **Cocoa butter polymorphism:** Forms I–VI; only Form V is the tempering target.
- **Lecithin (E322)** is the key emulsifier in both beverage powders (instant wetting) and chocolate (viscosity reduction).
- **Critical Control Points (CCPs):** bean roasting (microbial kill), conching (acid removal), tempering (crystal form), packaging (oxygen/moisture barrier).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Milk Chocolates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT412 - BAKERY AND CONFECTIONERY PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Milk Chocolates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Milk Chocolate** is a sweet, solid confectionery product manufactured from a homogeneous blend of cocoa products (cocoa mass/liquor and cocoa butter), milk solids (milk powder or condensed milk), sugar, emulsifiers (lecithin), and permitted flavoring agents (vanillin), subjected to refining, conching, tempering, moulding, and cooling operations.

According to the **Codex Alimentarius Standard CXS 87-1981** and the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011 (FSSAI)**, milk chocolate must contain a minimum of:

$$\text{Total Cocoa Solids} \geq 25\%$$

$$\text{Milk Solids (on dry basis)} \geq 14\%$$

$$\text{Milk Fat} \geq 3.5\% \quad \text{(with total fat} \geq 25\%)$$

$$\text{Defatted Cocoa Solids} \geq 2.5\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Milk chocolate is classified as a *compound chocolate variant* in the KTU 2024 module, distinguishing it from dark chocolate (≥35% cocoa solids) and white chocolate (no non-fat cocoa solids). The defining component is the **milk solids** content derived from whole or skim milk powder.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of milk chocolate like a **smooth orchestra performance**:
- **Cocoa mass** is the lead violinist — bold, aromatic, slightly bitter (the soul).
- **Cocoa butter** is the violin body — silky, melts on the tongue.
- **Sugar** is the percussion — sweet rhythm that balances the bitterness.
- **Milk solids** are the cello — the warm, mellow, creamy backbone that softens every sharp note.
- **Emulsifiers (lecithin)** are the conductor — keeping all instruments flowing in sync without separation (no fat bloom).

Without the cello (milk), the orchestra (chocolate) becomes harsh — that's **dark chocolate**. Without the violin (cocoa mass), it's just sweet cream — that's **white chocolate**. Milk chocolate is therefore the **balanced harmony** of all four ingredients.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metric:** A premium milk chocolate (e.g., Lindt, Cadbury Dairy Milk) typically contains **20–30% cocoa solids**, **20–25% milk solids**, **40–50% sugar**, and **25–30% total fat**. The U.S. FDA standard differs slightly — it requires only **≥10% chocolate liquor** and **≥12% milk solids**.

### 1.3 Classification of Milk Chocolates

| Type | Distinguishing Feature | Typical Use |
|------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Standard Milk Chocolate** | Balanced cocoa-milk-sugar ratio | Bars, slabs |
| **Swiss Milk Chocolate** | High milk fat (~6%), conched for 24+ hrs | Premium bars |
| **Filled Milk Chocolate** | Hollow shell with caramel/nut paste | Eggs, bars |
| **Coverture Milk Chocolate** | High cocoa butter, fluid when molten | Enrobing truffles |
| **Aerated Milk Chocolate** | Bubbles injected (e.g., Cadbury Bubbles) | Novelty bars |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Role of Each Ingredient

Each ingredient in milk chocolate performs one or more critical physico-chemical functions:

**1. Cocoa Mass (Liquor)**
- Provides **flavour** (pyrazines, aldehydes), **colour** (polyphenols oxidize to brown), and **bulk**.
- Source of non-fat cocoa solids (~55% of mass is cocoa butter).

**2. Cocoa Butter**
- The **sole natural fat** in chocolate. Melts at human body temperature (~34°C).
- Determines the **snap, gloss, and mouthfeel**.
- Composed of triglycerides POP, POS, SOS — the polymorphic crystal system.

**3. Milk Solids (Whole/Skim Milk Powder)**
- Source of **proteins (casein + whey)** which bind cocoa polyphenols, reducing astringency.
- Contributes **lactose (Maillard browning)** during conching → nutty/caramelly notes.
- Whole milk powder adds extra **milk fat** (creaminess).

**4. Sugar (Sucrose)**
- Provides **sweetness** and bulk.
- Acts as **abrasive** during refining to break down particles to <30 μm.
- Particle size of sugar directly controls **mouthfeel grittiness**.

**5. Lecithin (Emulsifier)**
- Typically **0.3–0.5%** of recipe.
- Reduces **yield value (τ₀)** and **plastic viscosity (η)** of molten chocolate.
- Allows reduction of cocoa butter content without affecting flow.

**6. PGPR (Polyglycerol Polyricinoleate)**
- Synthetic emulsifier used up to **0.3%** (often blended with lecithin at 1:1 ratio).
- Drastically reduces viscosity — enables thinner shell coating.

**7. Vanillin / Ethyl Vanillin**
- Synthetic or natural flavour enhancer; <0.05% used.
- Reinforces the chocolate aromatic perception.

### 2.2 Critical Physico-Chemical Parameters

| Parameter | Required Range | Effect of Deviation |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Moisture content** | < 1.0% | >1% → sugar recrystallization, microbial spoilage |
| **Particle size (D90)** | < 30 μm | >35 μm → gritty mouthfeel |
| **Fat content (total)** | 25–32% | <25% → dry, brittle; >35% → fat bloom risk |
| **Casson yield value (τ₀)** | 5–15 Pa | Too low → air entrainment; too high → poor moulding |
| **Casson plastic viscosity (η)** | 1.5–3.5 Pa·s | Controls flow and shell formation |
| **Conching temperature** | 50–80 °C | <45°C → inefficient Maillard; >85°C → burnt notes |
| **Conching duration** | 12–24 hours | Under-conched → harsh acidic off-notes |

### 2.3 The Casson Flow Equation (High-Yield for KTU)

The rheology of molten milk chocolate is classically described by the **Casson Model**:

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)
- $\tau_0$ = **Casson yield value** (Pa) — minimum stress to initiate flow
- $\eta$ = **Casson plastic viscosity** (Pa·s)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)

**Why it matters for KTU exams:** This equation is used to predict moulding behaviour, enrobing thickness, and to determine how much emulsifier is needed to bring viscosity within acceptable range.

### 2.4 Standard Mass Balance Equation for Recipe Formulation

For a 100 kg batch of milk chocolate meeting Codex standards:

$$m_{\text{cocoa mass}} + m_{\text{cocoa butter}} + m_{\text{milk powder}} + m_{\text{sugar}} + m_{\text{lecithin}} + m_{\text{vanillin}} = 100 \text{ kg}$$

Subject to constraints:
- Total cocoa solids ≥ 25 kg
- Milk solids ≥ 14 kg
- Total fat ≥ 25 kg
- Lecithin ≤ 0.5 kg

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In industrial plants, this mass-balance is the **starting point for batch costing, ingredient procurement, and FSSAI compliance certification**. A single deviation triggers either reformulation or regulatory labelling as "sub-standard".
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Recipe Calculation (Mass Balance)

**Problem (KTU-style):** Formulate **1000 kg** of milk chocolate containing **30% total cocoa solids**, **20% milk solids**, and **48% sugar**. The remaining mass is emulsifier (lecithin) and flavouring (vanillin). The cocoa mass used contains **55% cocoa butter** and **45% non-fat cocoa solids**. Whole milk powder contains **26% milk fat** and **70% milk solids** (the rest is moisture + ash). Determine the quantities of cocoa mass, cocoa butter, and milk powder required.

---

**Step 1 — Define unknowns**

Let $M$ = mass of cocoa mass (kg), $B$ = mass of additional cocoa butter (kg), $P$ = mass of whole milk powder (kg).

**Step 2 — Cocoa solids constraint**

The non-fat cocoa solids come only from cocoa mass (we assume added cocoa butter is 100% pure fat).

$$0.45 \times M \geq 250 \text{ kg}$$

Solving for M:

$$M \geq \frac{250}{0.45} = 555.56 \text{ kg}$$

For minimum compliance, take $M = 555.56$ kg.

**Step 3 — Milk solids constraint**

Whole milk powder provides 70% milk solids.

$$0.70 \times P = 200 \text{ kg}$$

$$P = \frac{200}{0.70} = 285.71 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Sugar constraint**

$$m_{\text{sugar}} = 0.48 \times 1000 = 480 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Cocoa butter available from cocoa mass**

$$\text{Cocoa butter from } M = 0.55 \times 555.56 = 305.56 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Total fat balance**

Total fat target = 25% of 1000 kg = 250 kg.

Total fat = (fat from cocoa mass) + (fat from milk powder) + (added cocoa butter)

$$\text{Fat from milk powder} = 0.26 \times 285.71 = 74.29 \text{ kg}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Added cocoa butter } B = 250 - 305.56 - 74.29 = -129.85 \text{ kg}$$

> The negative value indicates the formula is **fat-overloaded** from cocoa mass and milk powder. We must reduce cocoa mass and substitute more cocoa butter with sugar or milk powder — typical industrial practice is to reduce cocoa mass and use **cocoa powder (10–12% fat)** instead, or use **skim milk powder** to maintain milk solids without excess fat.

---

### 3.2 Corrected Industrial Recipe (Practical Adjustment)

Replace whole milk powder with **skim milk powder** (1% fat, 96% milk solids):

$$P_{\text{skim}} = \frac{200}{0.96} = 208.33 \text{ kg}$$

New fat from milk = 2.08 kg. We can now keep the cocoa mass at 555.56 kg (305.56 kg butter) and reach the 250 kg fat target by adding supplementary cocoa butter:

$$\text{Total fat} = 305.56 + 2.08 + B = 250$$

$$B = -57.64 \text{ kg}$$

Still over. Take **20% extra cocoa mass** reduction:

$$M_{\text{new}} = \frac{200}{0.45} = 444.44 \text{ kg (still satisfies 250 kg constraint slightly)}$$

Cocoa butter from new mass = $0.55 \times 444.44 = 244.44$ kg.

Now:

$$B = 250 - 244.44 - 2.08 = 3.48 \text{ kg}$$

> Final composition: **Cocoa mass = 444.44 kg**, **Cocoa butter (added) = 3.48 kg**, **Skim milk powder = 208.33 kg**, **Sugar = 480 kg**. Lecithin (0.3%) = **3.0 kg**, vanillin = **0.025 kg**. Total ≈ **1139 kg** — scale down proportionally if needed.

This demonstrates the **iterative nature of recipe balancing** in industrial R&D.

### 3.3 Particle Size Refinement Calculation

Refining reduces particle size from ~100 μm (raw sugar/cocoa) to <30 μm. The reduction follows the energy-input relation:

$$E = k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{d_i}{d_f}\right)$$

Where:
- $E$ = specific energy input (kWh/tonne)
- $d_i$ = initial particle diameter (≈ 100 μm)
- $d_f$ = final particle diameter (≈ 25 μm)
- $k$ = constant (≈ 12 kWh/tonne for 5-roll refiners)

$$E = 12 \times \ln\left(\frac{100}{25}\right) = 12 \times 1.386 = 16.64 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

> **Real-world meaning:** A 1000 kg batch requires ~16.64 kWh of refining energy — a primary contributor to the chocolate's *mouthfeel quality* and overall manufacturing cost.

### 3.4 Conching Time vs Flavour Development

Empirical relationship (Beckett's chocolate science):

$$F = F_{\max} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_c}\right)$$

Where:
- $F$ = developed flavour intensity at time $t$
- $F_{\max}$ = maximum achievable flavour
- $\tau_c$ = characteristic conching time (≈ 6–8 hours)

After **24 hours** of conching, development ≈ $1 - e^{-24/7} = 1 - 0.033 = 96.7\%$.

This justifies the industry-standard 24-hour conching cycle for premium milk chocolate.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete Process Flow — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Receiving and Weighing] --> B[Pre-Mixing in Sigma Mixer]
    B --> C[Five Roll Refiner]
    C --> D[Conche Vessel]
    D --> E[Standardization and Addition of Lecithin]
    E --> F[Tempering Machine]
    F --> G[Moulding on Belt]
    G --> H[Cooling Tunnel 12 to 15 C]
    H --> I[Demoulding and Inspection]
    I --> J[Wrapping and Packaging]
    J --> K[Storage 15 to 18 C]

    C -. By-Product: Cocoa Press Cake .-> L[Cocoa Powder Line]
    D -. By-Stream: Moisture and Volatiles .-> M[Condenser and Vent System]

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
    style C fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    style D fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    style F fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
    style K fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#6c3483
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of Conching Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Refined Chocolate Paste] --> R2[Conche Loading Hopper]
    R2 --> R3[Main Conching Vessel with Paddle Agitator]
    R3 --> R4[Heating Jacket 50 to 80 C]
    R3 --> R5[Vacuum System for Moisture Removal]
    R5 --> R6[Vent Condenser]
    R3 --> R7[Shear and Friction Action]
    R7 --> R8[Maillard and Flavour Reaction Zone]
    R8 --> R9[Discharge Pump]
    R9 --> R10[Storage Tank Prior to Tempering]

    style R1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b
    style R3 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#1e8449
    style R5 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#1f618d
    style R10 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
```

### 4.3 Crystallization (Tempering) Phase Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Liquid Chocolate 45 C] --> S1[Cooling to 27 C Forms Seed Crystals]
    S1 --> S2[Rewarming to 30 to 32 C]
    S2 --> S3[Stable Form V Crystals Dominate]
    S3 --> S4[Moulding and Cooling]
    S4 --> S5[Glossy Snap and Easy Demould]

    X1[Skipped Cooling Stage] --> X2[Form IV Crystals Soft Bloom]
    Y1[Over-Cooled Stage] --> Y2[Form III Crystals Fat Bloom]

    style S0 fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#b9770e
    style S3 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d
    style S5 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d
    style X2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
    style Y2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** The five-roll refiner and conche are the **two most energy-intensive stations** in a chocolate plant — together they account for ~60% of total electrical load. The tempering step is the **quality-defining station** — a single 1°C deviation produces visible fat bloom within 7 days of shelf storage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define milk chocolate. List the five essential ingredients used in its manufacture.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Milk chocolate is a homogeneous confectionery product obtained by refining, conching, and tempering a blend of cocoa products, milk solids, and sugar, conforming to minimum compositional standards prescribed under the **Codex Alimentarius (CXS 87-1981)** and **FSSAI Regulations, 2011**.

The five essential ingredients are:

1. **Cocoa mass (liquor)** — provides cocoa solids and cocoa butter (flavour + fat)
2. **Cocoa butter** — adjusts fat content and provides melting/mouthfeel properties
3. **Milk powder** (whole or skim) — provides milk solids and milk fat
4. **Sugar** (sucrose) — provides sweetness and aids refining
5. **Lecithin** — emulsifier that reduces viscosity

*(Vanillin is optional flavouring; PGPR is optional emulsifier.)*

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing ingredients with their role.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is **conching** in chocolate manufacture? Why is it considered the most critical step?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Conching is the prolonged **shearing, agitating, and heating** of refined chocolate paste in a heated vessel (conche) at temperatures of **50–80°C** for **12–72 hours**, depending on product grade.

It is the most critical step because it performs three simultaneous functions:

1. **Flavour development** — promotes Maillard reactions between milk proteins and reducing sugars; removes volatile acetic acid and other harsh volatiles.
2. **Rheology stabilization** — coats every solid particle with a continuous fat film, ensuring smooth flow and preventing lumps.
3. **Moisture reduction** — drops moisture from ~2% to <0.5% via vacuum application.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the three functions.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **manufacturing process of milk chocolate**, with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **role of cocoa butter, milk solids, and emulsifiers** in milk chocolate with reference to their physico-chemical functions. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Manufacturing Process of Milk Chocolate** *(7 marks)*

The manufacturing sequence consists of the following stages:

**1. Raw Material Preparation (0.5 mark)**
- Cocoa beans are roasted (110–150°C, 15–45 min), winnowed (cracked and husk-separated), then ground into cocoa mass (liquor) at ~40°C.
- Sugar is pulverized; milk powder is sieved; cocoa butter is melted.

**2. Mixing / Pre-blending (0.5 mark)**
- All dry ingredients (cocoa mass, sugar, milk powder) are blended in a **sigma-blade mixer** at 40–50°C for 10–15 min to form a stiff paste.

**3. Refining (1 mark)**
- The paste passes through a **5-roll refiner**, which reduces particle size from ~100 μm to **18–30 μm**.
- The five rolls rotate at progressively higher speeds (1 : 2 : 4 : 8 : 16 ratio) with decreasing gaps.
- Output is a fine flake/powder.

**4. Conching (1.5 marks)**
- Refined paste is transferred to a **conche** — a vessel with rotating paddles/rollers.
- Conditions: **50–80°C, 12–24 hours**, with or without vacuum.
- Three sub-stages:
  - *Dry conching* (no added fat) — moisture and acid removal
  - *Wet/paste conching* (cocoa butter + lecithin added) — viscosity adjustment
  - *Liquid conching* — final homogenization

**5. Tempering (1 mark)**
- Molten chocolate is subjected to a precise temperature cycle:
  - Cool from ~45°C → **27°C** (seed crystals form, mostly unstable Forms IV and V).
  - Rewarm to **30–32°C** (only stable Form V remains).
- This step determines the final **gloss, snap, and shelf-life**.

**6. Moulding and Cooling (1 mark)**
- Tempered chocolate is deposited into cooled plastic/metal moulds via volumetric depositor.
- Moulds pass through a **cooling tunnel at 12–15°C for 25–30 minutes** — solidification without thermal shock.

**7. Demoulding, Wrapping, Storage (0.5 mark)**
- Chocolate is ejected, visually inspected, foil-wrapped, and stored at **15–18°C, 50–55% RH**.

---

**(b) Role of Cocoa Butter, Milk Solids, and Emulsifiers** *(7 marks)*

**1. Cocoa Butter (2.5 marks)**
- **Melting behaviour**: Melts sharply at **33–34°C** (just below body temperature) — gives the characteristic "melts in your mouth" feel.
- **Polymorphism**: Exists in six crystal forms (I–VI). Only **Form V (β₂)** is desirable — provides gloss, snap, and shrinkage (for clean demoulding).
- **Vehicle for fat-soluble flavours**: Carries pyrazines and aromatic compounds to taste receptors.
- **Viscosity control**: Without adequate cocoa butter, chocolate is too thick to mould/enrobe.

**2. Milk Solids (2.5 marks)**
- **Flavour smoothing**: **Casein proteins** bind astringent cocoa polyphenols (tannins), reducing bitterness.
- **Maillard browning**: During conching, **lactose + milk proteins** undergo Maillard reactions generating **furans, maltol, pyrazines** — nutty, caramelly, creamy notes.
- **Texture**: Milk fat globules contribute **creaminess**; milk proteins contribute to **structure**.
- **Nutritional enrichment**: Adds calcium, protein, vitamin B-complex.

**3. Emulsifiers — Lecithin and PGPR (2 marks)**
- **Lecithin (0.3–0.5%)**: An amphiphilic phospholipid that **aligns at the fat-sugar interface**, lowering interfacial tension and reducing **yield value** by up to 50%.
- **PGPR (≤0.3%)**: Even more powerful viscosity reducer — preferred in enrobing operations where thin coating is desired.
- **Mechanism**: Replace surface air voids on sugar particles with a fat film → smoother flow.
- **Anti-bloom effect**: Proper emulsification slows migration of liquid triglycerides to surface, delaying fat bloom.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Codex Alimentarius compositional standards** for milk chocolate. How do these differ from **FSSAI and US-FDA** standards? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **tempering process** in detail. What are the consequences of **improper tempering** on the final product? *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Codex / FSSAI / FDA Standards Comparison** *(7 marks)*

**1. Codex Alimentarius (CXS 87-1981, revised 2016)** *(2.5 marks)*

| Parameter | Minimum Requirement |
|-----------|--------------------|
| Total cocoa solids | **≥ 25%** |
| Milk fat | **≥ 3.5%** |
| Total milk solids | **≥ 14%** |
| Cocoa butter | **≥ 18%** |
| Total fat | **≥ 25%** |
| Defatted cocoa solids | **≥ 2.5%** |
| Moisture | < 1.5% |

**2. FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Act, 2011)** *(2 marks)*
- FSSAI largely **adopts Codex** for chocolates.
- Requires **"Milk Chocolate"** to contain **≥ 5% milk solids (revised later to ≥ 14%)** depending on the variant.
- Permitted additives: lecithin (≤0.5%), PGPR (≤0.3%), vanillin (≤100 mg/kg).
- Mandatory **vegetarian/non-vegetarian symbol** on wrapper.

**3. US-FDA Standard of Identity (21 CFR §163.130)** *(1.5 marks)*
- Requires only **≥ 10% chocolate liquor** (much lower than Codex).
- Milk solids requirement: **≥ 12%** (only of non-fat cocoa solids content is factored differently).
- Allows **vegetable fats (CBEs) up to 5%** as cocoa butter equivalents.

**4. Key Differences (1 mark)**

| Aspect | Codex | FSSAI | US-FDA |
|--------|-------|-------|--------|
| Cocoa solids minimum | 25% | 25% | ~10–15% (via liquor) |
| Milk solids minimum | 14% | 14% | 12% |
| Vegetable fat allowance | None | ≤ 5% (as per amendment) | ≤ 5% |

> **Engineering Note:** Indian milk chocolate (e.g., Cadbury Dairy Milk) typically contains **~25% cocoa solids** and **~20% milk solids** — comfortably above Codex minimum.

---

**(b) Tempering Process and Consequences of Improper Tempering** *(7 marks)*

**1. Theoretical Basis — Polymorphism (2 marks)**
Cocoa butter exhibits **six polymorphic forms**:

| Form | Melting Point | Stability |
|------|---------------|-----------|
| I (γ) | 17°C | Unstable |
| II (α) | 21°C | Unstable |
| III (β′) | 26°C | Unstable |
| IV (β′) | 28°C | Metastable |
| V (β₂) | 34°C | **Stable — desired** |
| VI (β₁) | 36°C | Most stable but slow-forming |

Only **Form V** gives the glossy surface, crisp snap, and resistance to fat bloom.

**2. Tempering Procedure (3 marks)**
- **Step 1 — Cool**: Molten chocolate at **45°C** is cooled to **27–28°C**, inducing formation of mixed Forms IV and V crystal seeds.
- **Step 2 — Re-warm**: Temperature is raised to **30–32°C**, melting all unstable Forms I–IV while preserving Form V seeds.
- **Step 3 — Hold**: Chocolate held at **30–32°C** for 15–30 min during moulding/depositing.
- **Step 4 — Cool**: Solidify in cooling tunnel at **12–15°C** so Form V crystallizes uniformly.

**3. Consequences of Improper Tempering (2 marks)**

- **Under-tempered (too warm)** → chocolate sets slowly, dull surface, soft body, sticks to mould.
- **Over-tempered (too cold)** → chocolate becomes too thick, "seized", moulding defects.
- **Skip-re-warming** → Forms I–IV dominate → **fat bloom** (white-grey surface film of unstable crystals that melt and re-crystallize) within 5–10 days.
- **Sugar bloom** — separate issue caused by moisture on chocolate surface.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical)**
> 1. Many students confuse **fat bloom** (recrystallization of unstable triglycerides) with **sugar bloom** (moisture-induced sugar dissolution). Marks are deducted if these are interchanged.
> 2. Do NOT state that tempering "improves flavour" — it does not. It only controls **crystal form, gloss, snap, and shelf-life**.
> 3. Avoid writing "chocolate has 4 crystal forms" — the correct number is **six** (Forms I–VI), with Form V being the target.
> 4. For manufacturing questions, the examiner expects **units** (°C, μm, %, hours). Vague answers without numbers lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. Conching is **not** the same as tempering — students frequently interchange them. Conching = flavour development; Tempering = crystal control.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Milk Chocolates**

**Definition & Standards**
- Milk chocolate = cocoa products + milk solids + sugar + emulsifier, refined, conched, tempered.
- Codex minimum: **25% cocoa solids, 14% milk solids, 3.5% milk fat, 18% cocoa butter, 25% total fat**.
- FSSAI largely mirrors Codex; US-FDA is more permissive (10% liquor).

**Ingredients & Roles**
- **Cocoa mass** → flavour + colour + bulk.
- **Cocoa butter** → melting, gloss, snap (Form V crystals).
- **Milk powder** → creaminess, smooths tannins via casein–polyphenol binding, drives Maillard flavour.
- **Sugar** → sweetness + abrasive in refining.
- **Lecithin (0.3–0.5%) + PGPR (≤0.3%)** → reduce yield value and viscosity.
- **Vanillin** → flavour enhancer.

**Process Flow**
- Mixing → **5-roll refining** (100 → 25 μm) → **Conching** (50–80°C, 12–24 h) → Tempering (45 → 27 → 32°C) → Moulding → Cooling tunnel (12–15°C) → Demoulding → Wrapping → Storage (15–18°C).

**Critical Numerical Facts to Memorise**
- Particle size target: **< 30 μm** (D90).
- Moisture target: **< 1%**.
- Conching temperature: **50–80°C**.
- Conching duration: **12–24 hours**.
- Tempering temperature window: **27–32°C**.
- Cooling tunnel: **12–15°C**.
- Storage: **15–18°C, 50–55% RH**.

**Crystal Forms**
- **Six forms (I–VI)**; only **Form V (34°C melting)** is desired.
- Improper tempering → **fat bloom** in 5–10 days.

**Casson Equation**
$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$

**Common Exam Traps**
- Fat bloom ≠ sugar bloom.
- Conching ≠ tempering.
- Six crystal forms (not 4, not 5).
- Codex requires 25% cocoa solids (not "20%" or "30%").

**Differences to remember**
- Dark chocolate: ≥35% cocoa solids, no milk solids.
- White chocolate: no non-fat cocoa solids (only cocoa butter).
- Milk chocolate: ≥25% cocoa solids + ≥14% milk solids.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
